topics.title: Define Key Concepts and Principles of Systems Security Engineering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Define Key Concepts and Principles of Systems Security Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Systems Security Engineering (SSE)?

**Systems Security Engineering (SSE)** is a specialty discipline of systems engineering that applies scientific, engineering, and management principles to deliver **trustworthy secure systems** that satisfy stakeholder requirements within a defined operational environment. It is the discipline of building *security in* (by design) rather than bolting it on after the fact.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition (aligned with NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1):**  
> *“Systems Security Engineering is an engineering discipline that provides the scientific and engineering basis for designing, building, and maintaining trustworthy secure systems that satisfy stakeholder requirements for trust, assurance, and resilience in the face of adversity.”*

The phrase **“Trustworthy Secure System”** is the heart of this course. A *trustworthy* system is one that is worthy of being trusted to meet its security-relevant requirements, while a *secure* system exhibits the protective properties we expect (confidentiality, integrity, availability).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — “The Bank Vault Design”

Imagine you are asked to design a **bank vault** (the system) that will store money (the asset) for 50 years (the lifecycle).

- A naïve designer would only put a thick door on a room.
- A *Systems Security Engineer* would think holistically:
  1. **Who are the adversaries?** (Threat modeling — robbers, insider employees, flood, fire)
  2. **What are the valuables?** (Asset identification — cash, gold, data, reputation)
  3. **What could go wrong?** (Risk identification — theft, natural disaster, power failure)
  4. **How badly would each failure hurt?** (Impact analysis)
  5. **What controls reduce each risk?** (Defense-in-depth: walls, cameras, alarms, guards, insurance)
  6. **How do we prove it works?** (Assurance, testing, certification)

Just as a vault is *engineered* (not just a door), a **secure system is engineered** (not just a firewall). This is the essence of SSE.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight for Exams:** SSE is *systems-focused*, not *component-focused*. It treats security as an emergent property of the *whole* system — people, processes, hardware, software, and environment — interacting together.

---

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary — The Language of SSE

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition | Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Asset** | Anything of value to the stakeholder (data, hardware, people, reputation) | The gold inside the vault |
| **Threat** | Any circumstance or event with the potential to adversely impact the system | A robber with a drill |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in the system that can be exploited | The old rusted hinge on the vault door |
| **Risk** | A measure of the extent to which an entity is threatened by a potential circumstance or event | Probability × Impact of the vault being cracked |
| **Adversary** | An entity that attempts to exploit a vulnerability | The thief, the insider, the spy |
| **Control (Safeguard / Countermeasure)** | A protective measure that reduces risk | The CCTV camera, the alarm, the guard |
| **Trustworthiness** | The degree to which a system can be relied upon to satisfy its security requirements | The vault passing a 50-year durability test |
| **Resilience** | The ability of a system to withstand adverse conditions and recover | Vault walls that bend but do not break |

> [!TIP]  
> Examiners love asking: **“Differentiate Threat, Vulnerability, and Risk.”**  
> A *threat* exploits a *vulnerability* to create *risk*. A vulnerability without a threat is harmless. A threat without a vulnerability is ineffective.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Trustworthiness

A system is deemed **trustworthy** when it demonstrates a balanced posture across these three properties:

1. **Security** — Protection against intentional harm (attacks).
2. **Safety** — Protection against unintentional harm (accidents, failures).
3. **Reliability/Resilience** — Continued correct operation under stress and recovery from failures.

> [!WARNING]  
> A common student mistake is equating *security* with *trustworthiness*. **Security is a subset of trustworthiness.** A system can be insecure (no malware) yet untrustworthy (crashes during a storm, leaks customer data accidentally). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction for full marks.

---

## 1.5 Why SSE Matters in 2024+ — Engineering Context

Modern engineered systems (IoT, autonomous vehicles, smart grids, healthcare, fintech) are **deeply interconnected, software-intensive, and adversarial-exposed**. Traditional perimeter security is dead — attackers exploit weak links across the supply chain, the firmware, the user, and the network. KTU’s PECCT754 course exists to train engineers to design systems that remain *trustworthy under real-world attack and failure conditions* — a skill set now demanded by every industry vertical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Principles of Systems Security Engineering

NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1 defines **eight security design principles** that KTU 2024 expects you to know. Each is presented with its engineering rationale.

| # | Principle | Engineering Meaning | Real-World Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defense in Depth** | Apply layered controls so no single failure causes total loss. | Bank has door + lock + camera + guard + alarm. |
| 2 | **Least Privilege** | Grant only the minimum permissions needed. | A cashier can ring up sales but cannot open the safe. |
| 3 | **Separation of Duties (SoD)** | No single individual should control an entire critical process. | Two keys needed to launch a nuclear missile. |
| 4 | **Fail-Safe Defaults** | Default state must deny access when uncertain. | Door locks when power fails, not unlocks. |
| 5 | **Economy of Mechanism** | Keep security designs small, simple, and verifiable. | A simple padlock is more reliable than an over-engineered smart lock. |
| 6 | **Complete Mediation** | Every access must be checked, every time. | Re-validating the ticket at the movie hall, not just at entry. |
| 7 | **Open Design** | Security should not depend on secrecy of mechanism. | Locks can be public; only the key is secret. |
| 8 | **Least Common Mechanism** | Minimize shared resources that could become a covert channel. | Don’t share one user account across 100 employees. |

> [!NOTE]  
> These eight principles are **examination-grade material**. A 14-mark question in KTU ESE may ask you to **explain any five** with engineering examples. Memorize the principle name, its meaning, and a one-line example.

---

## 2.2 The Trustworthiness Lifecycle (TWL)

A trustworthy system is not achieved by a single act; it is the output of a structured engineering lifecycle. KTU follows a five-phase model adapted from NIST SP 800-160.

1. **Concept Phase** — Define the mission, stakeholders, and protection needs. (Asset & threat identification.)
2. **Development Phase** — Design and build security into architecture, components, and code.
3. **Production Phase** — Manufacture, integrate, and verify (assurance arguments, certification).
4. **Operations Phase** — Deploy, monitor, patch, and operate securely.
5. **Support & Retirement Phase** — Sustain, upgrade, and finally decommission securely (no data leak in disposal).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why “lifecycle” matters in exams:** Security cannot be retrofitted. The cost of fixing a security flaw discovered in *Operations* is up to **100×** higher than catching it in the *Concept* phase. This is the *“shift-left security”* argument.

---

## 2.3 Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact

The single most important equation in security engineering. In its simplest qualitative form:

$$R = f(T, V, I)$$

Where:
- $R$ = Level of Risk
- $T$ = Threat likelihood (probability that an adversary will act)
- $V$ = Vulnerability presence and ease of exploitation
- $I$ = Business impact if the event occurs

A more quantitative, KTU-favored form uses the classical **Risk = Probability × Impact** model:

$$R = P \times I$$

Where:
- $P$ = Probability of threat exploiting vulnerability (0 ≤ P ≤ 1)
- $I$ = Impact magnitude in monetary or operational units (₹, downtime hours, data records lost)

> [!TIP]  
> For 3-mark short questions, the qualitative form $R = f(T, V, I)$ is sufficient. For 7-mark derivations, derive the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** form shown in Section 3.

---

## 2.4 The CIA Triad — Extended to CIANA+

The classical *Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability* triad is now extended in modern KTU syllabus to include:

| Property | Symbol | Meaning | Engineering Control Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | C | Data is disclosed only to authorized parties | AES-256 encryption, access control lists |
| **Integrity** | I | Data is not altered in an unauthorized way | SHA-256 hashing, digital signatures |
| **Availability** | A | System is accessible when needed | Redundant servers, DDoS mitigation |
| **Non-Repudiation** | N | Sender/receiver cannot deny the action | PKI digital certificates, audit logs |
| **Authenticity** | A | Identity of a party is verified | Multi-factor authentication, biometrics |

> [!NOTE]  
> The **CIANA+** expansion is increasingly asked in KTU 2024 modular tests. If the question is from 2024 scheme, write **all five** properties, not just CIA.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| Formula | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $R = P \times I$ | Qualitative risk | dimensionless score 0–10 |
| $R = T \times V \times I$ | NIST three-factor risk | score |
| $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Annual Loss Expectancy | ₹ / year |
| $SLE = Asset\ Value \times EF$ | Single Loss Expectancy | ₹ |
| $ROSI = \dfrac{(ALE \times \%RiskMitigated) - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Return on Security Investment | ratio (decimal) |
| $MTBF = \dfrac{Total\ Operating\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | Reliability | hours |
| $MTTR = \dfrac{Total\ Downtime}{Number\ of\ Repairs}$ | Maintainability | hours |
| $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | System uptime | ratio 0–1 (or %) |
| $Residual\ Risk = Inherent\ Risk - Control\ Effectiveness$ | Post-control risk | score |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **NEVER use the pipe symbol `|` inside the markdown table.** For absolute value $ \vert x \vert $, use the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` — this prevents the markdown table parser from breaking your answer sheet.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

SSE is the foundation of every regulated engineering domain:
- **Aerospace & Defense** — DO-326A / DO-356 (Airworthiness Security)
- **Automotive** — ISO/SAE 21434 (Cybersecurity Engineering for Road Vehicles)
- **Medical Devices** — IEC 80001-2 (Risk Management of Health IT)
- **Industrial Control** — IEC 62443 (OT/ICS Security)
- **Fintech & Banking** — RBI Cyber Security Framework, PCI-DSS
- **Public Sector** — India’s National Cyber Security Policy, NCIIPC guidelines

A graduate engineer trained in SSE is therefore directly employable in any of these sectors — KTU’s industry-aligned curriculum rationale.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)

The ALE is the cornerstone quantitative metric of SSE. We derive it step-by-step from first principles.

### Step 1 — Define Asset Value (AV)

The **Asset Value** is the monetary worth of the asset being protected.

$$AV \in \mathbb{R}^+, \quad AV > 0$$

Units: **₹ (Rupees)**, USD, or any consistent currency.

### Step 2 — Define Exposure Factor (EF)

The **Exposure Factor** is the percentage of asset value lost if a single threat event occurs. It is a fraction between 0 and 1 (or 0% to 100%).

$$EF \in [0, 1]$$

Example: A ransomware attack encrypts 60% of a database → $EF = 0.6$.

### Step 3 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)

The **SLE** is the monetary loss expected from a *single* occurrence of the threat.

$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

**Worked Example (Inline):**  
If $AV = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$ and $EF = 0.6$:

$$SLE = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 = \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000$$

### Step 4 — Define Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)

The **ARO** is the estimated number of times per year that the threat will materialize.

$$ARO \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$$

Example: A DDoS attack is expected 4 times a year → $ARO = 4$.

### Step 5 — Compute Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)

The **ALE** is the expected monetary loss per year due to a specific threat.

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

**Continuation of Worked Example:** With $SLE = \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000$ and $ARO = 4$:

$$ALE = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 4 = \text{₹}24{,}00{,}000\ \text{per\ year}$$

### Step 6 — Decide on a Control (Cost-Benefit Analysis)

A control should be implemented **iff** its annual cost is less than the ALE it mitigates:

$$C_{control} < ALE \times \%RiskMitigated$$

If a firewall costs ₹8,00,000/year and mitigates 80% of the DDoS loss:

$$Benefit = 24{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80 = \text{₹}19{,}20{,}000$$
$$Net\ Benefit = 19{,}20{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}11{,}20{,}000}$$

Since $Net\ Benefit > 0$, the control is justified. **This is the ROSI derivation.**

### Step 7 — Compute Return on Security Investment (ROSI)

$$ROSI = \dfrac{(ALE \times \%RM) - C_{control}}{C_{control}}$$

Plugging in:

$$ROSI = \dfrac{(24{,}00{,}000 \times 0.80) - 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000}$$

$$ROSI = \dfrac{19{,}20{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = \dfrac{11{,}20{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000}$$

$$ROSI = 1.4 = 140\%$$

A positive ROSI confirms the control is a profitable investment.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: System Availability

Availability is the fraction of time a system performs its intended function.

$$Availability = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Worked Example:** A server has $MTBF = 900$ hours and $MTTR = 100$ hours.

$$A = \dfrac{900}{900 + 100} = \dfrac{900}{1000} = 0.90 = 90\%$$

This is the famous **"five-nines"** target ($A = 0.99999$) that telecom and banking systems chase.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Security Principle × Engineering Use

| Principle | Architectural Element | Cryptographic Element | Operational Element | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Defense in Depth | Multiple firewalls + DMZ | Layered encryption (TLS + AES at rest) | CCTV + guards + SIEM | Use the word “layered” in your answer. |
| Least Privilege | RBAC model | Per-key ACL in HSM | Just-in-time access provisioning | Mention “minimum necessary” explicitly. |
| Fail-Safe Defaults | Default-deny firewall rules | Default to no trust in certificates | Lock-on-power-fail doors | Always give a counter-example (default-permit fails). |
| Complete Mediation | Re-authentication on every API call | Re-validate session tokens on each request | Periodic badge re-validation | Stress “every access, every time.” |
| Open Design | Published algorithm (AES) | Public-key cryptography (RSA) | Public security audits | Contrast with “security through obscurity.” |
| Separation of Duties | Two-person rule in code deployment | Multi-signature wallets (crypto) | Maker-checker in banking | Always give a 2-person example. |

---

## 3.4 Risk Treatment Matrix (Standard KTU Table)

| Risk Level | Inherent Risk Score | Recommended Treatment | Action Timeline |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Critical** | 20–25 | Mitigate immediately; accept if no alternative | Within 1 month |
| **High** | 15–19 | Mitigate or transfer (insurance) | Within 3 months |
| **Medium** | 8–14 | Mitigate if cost-effective | Within 6 months |
| **Low** | 1–7 | Accept (document formally) | Annual review |

This matrix is built on the **Risk Score = Likelihood (1–5) × Impact (1–5)** grid, which is the KTU-favored qualitative scheme.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Systems Security Engineering Process (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stakeholder Needs] --> B[Concept Phase]
    B --> C[System Requirements]
    C --> D[Security Architecture]
    D --> E[Implementation and Integration]
    E --> F[Verification and Validation]
    F --> G[Operations and Sustainment]
    G --> H[Disposal and Retirement]
    H --> I[Feedback to Concept Phase]

    D --> D1[Defense in Depth]
    D --> D2[Least Privilege]
    D --> D3[Fail Safe Defaults]
    D --> D4[Complete Mediation]
    D --> D5[Open Design]
    D --> D6[Separation of Duties]
    D --> D7[Economy of Mechanism]
    D --> D8[Least Common Mechanism]

    F --> V1[Assurance Case]
    F --> V2[Penetration Testing]
    F --> V3[Vulnerability Assessment]
    F --> V4[Compliance Audit]

    G --> O1[Continuous Monitoring]
    G --> O2[Patch Management]
    G --> O3[Incident Response]
    G --> O4[Configuration Management]
```

---

## 4.2 The Trustworthiness Framework (Layered Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TR["TRUSTWORTHINESS"]
        direction TB
        SE["SECURITY"]
        SA["SAFETY"]
        RE["RELIABILITY AND RESILIENCE"]
        PR["PRIVACY"]
        SE --- SA
        SA --- RE
        RE --- PR
    end

    subgraph PR2["PROTECTIVE PILLARS"]
        direction LR
        P1[Confidentiality]
        P2[Integrity]
        P3[Availability]
        P4[Non Repudiation]
        P5[Authenticity]
    end

    TR --> PR2

    subgraph EN["ENABLERS"]
        direction LR
        E1[Risk Management]
        E2[Assurance Arguments]
        E3[Standards Compliance]
        E4[Continuous Monitoring]
    end

    PR2 --> EN
```

---

## 4.3 The Risk Management Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Asset] --> B[Identify Threat]
    B --> C[Identify Vulnerability]
    C --> D[Compute Risk Score]
    D --> E{Risk Acceptable?}
    E -- No --> F[Select Control]
    F --> G[Implement Control]
    G --> H[Recompute Residual Risk]
    H --> E
    E -- Yes --> I[Document and Monitor]
    I --> J[Periodic Review]
    J --> A
```

---

## 4.4 The CIANA+ Property Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TRUSTED SYSTEM CORE] --> B[Confidentiality]
    A --> C[Integrity]
    A --> D[Availability]
    A --> E[Non Repudiation]
    A --> F[Authenticity]
    A --> G[Privacy]

    B --> B1[Encryption and ACLs]
    C --> C1[Hashing and Signatures]
    D --> D1[Redundancy and Failover]
    E --> E1[PKI and Audit Logs]
    F --> F1[MFA and Biometrics]
    G --> G1[Data Minimization and Consent]
```

---

## 4.5 The Defense in Depth “Onion” Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    O1[Policies and Governance] --> O2[Physical Security]
    O2 --> O3[Perimeter Network Controls]
    O3 --> O4[Internal Network Segmentation]
    O4 --> O5[Host Hardening]
    O5 --> O6[Application Security]
    O6 --> O7[Data Encryption]
    O7 --> O8[Crown Jewel Asset]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> This concentric “onion” view is the standard answer to **“Explain defense-in-depth with a diagram”** — a frequent 7-mark question in KTU ESE. Each layer is breached individually; the asset at the center is protected by *all* layers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define Systems Security Engineering. List any four security design principles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Systems Security Engineering (SSE) is an engineering discipline that applies scientific, engineering, and management principles to design, build, and maintain trustworthy secure systems that satisfy stakeholder requirements for trust, assurance, and resilience in the face of adversity. *(As per NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1.)*

**Any Four Principles (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Defense in Depth** — Layered security controls so no single failure causes total compromise.
2. **Least Privilege** — Grant minimum permissions necessary to perform a task.
3. **Fail-Safe Defaults** — Default to a secure (deny) state when the system is uncertain.
4. **Open Design** — Security must not rely on the secrecy of the mechanism itself.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Differentiate between Threat, Vulnerability, and Risk with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per term):**

| Term | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Threat** | Any potential event or adversary action that can cause harm. | A hacker trying to break into a server. |
| **Vulnerability** | A weakness in the system that can be exploited. | An unpatched Apache Log4j flaw. |
| **Risk** | The probability and impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability. | 60% chance of a breach via Log4j causing ₹24 lakh/year loss. |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner’s Pitfall:** Students often confuse *threat* with *vulnerability*. Memorize the arrow: **Threat → exploits → Vulnerability → creates → Risk**. Writing the arrow direction wrong costs 1 full mark.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> In KTU ESE, Module 1 carries **14 marks** for a single Part-B question. You will be given an internal choice — answer **one of two** options. We provide both below.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Systems Security Engineering Lifecycle** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of *assurance* and *resilience* in the lifecycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** and **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** for the following scenario and recommend whether the control should be implemented. **(7 marks)**

> *Scenario:* A company has a customer database valued at ₹50,00,000. A SQL injection attack is expected to compromise 40% of the data, and based on threat intelligence, such attacks are expected to occur 3 times per year. The proposed WAF (Web Application Firewall) costs ₹6,00,000 per year and is estimated to mitigate 90% of the risk.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — SSE Lifecycle, Assurance, and Resilience (7 marks)

**Lifecycle Phases (4 marks — 1 phase × 4 phases, 1 mark each):**

1. **Concept Phase** — Stakeholder needs, mission definition, asset and threat identification. Establishes the *protection needs*.
2. **Development Phase** — Security architecture design, principle application, secure coding. Builds *security in*.
3. **Production Phase** — Integration, verification, validation, certification, and accreditation. Builds *assurance*.
4. **Operations Phase** — Deployment, monitoring, patching, incident response, sustainment. Provides *resilience*.
5. **Support & Retirement** — Decommissioning with secure data disposal and feedback to the concept phase for future systems.

**Role of Assurance (1.5 marks):** *Assurance* is the grounds for confidence that the system meets its security requirements. It is built through evidence collection: testing, formal verification, penetration testing, code review, and compliance audits. Assurance is *demonstrated*, not assumed.

**Role of Resilience (1.5 marks):** *Resilience* is the system’s ability to maintain mission capability despite adverse events (attacks, faults, disasters). It is achieved through redundancy, fail-over, recovery procedures, and adaptive controls. Resilience is the *operational twin* of assurance — one is “we proved it works,” the other is “it keeps working.”

**Block Diagram (Refer Section 4.1 of these notes).**

---

#### Part (b) — ALE and ROSI Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Asset Value and Exposure Factor (1 mark):**
$$AV = \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000, \quad EF = 0.40$$

**Step 2 — Compute SLE (1 mark):**
$$SLE = AV \times EF = 50{,}00{,}000 \times 0.40 = \text{₹}20{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE (1 mark):**
$$ALE = SLE \times ARO = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 3 = \text{₹}60{,}00{,}000\ \text{per\ year}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Risk-Mitigated Benefit (1 mark):**
$$Benefit = ALE \times \%RM = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.90 = \text{₹}54{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 5 — Compute Net Benefit (1 mark):**
$$Net\ Benefit = Benefit - C_{control} = 54{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}48{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 6 — Compute ROSI (1 mark):**
$$ROSI = \dfrac{Net\ Benefit}{C_{control}} = \dfrac{48{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000} = 8.0 = 800\%$$

**Step 7 — Recommendation (1 mark):** Since $ROSI = 800\% > 0$ and $C_{control} \ll ALE$, the WAF control is **strongly recommended** for implementation. The investment is recovered **8 times over** in mitigated risk.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modeled]**

**(a)** Explain the **eight security design principles** of SSE. For each principle, give one engineering example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An e-commerce server has $MTBF = 1500$ hours and $MTTR = 50$ hours. Compute the system availability. If the business requires 99.9% availability (three nines), determine how much the MTTR must be reduced to meet the SLA. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Eight Security Design Principles (7 marks — 0.75 per principle, 0.5 for example)

1. **Defense in Depth** — Layered controls (firewall + IDS + encryption). *Example:* Bank vault with door, lock, alarm, guard.
2. **Least Privilege** — Minimum necessary permissions. *Example:* A user with read-only access cannot modify the database.
3. **Separation of Duties** — No single person controls entire critical process. *Example:* Two officers required to authorize a wire transfer.
4. **Fail-Safe Defaults** — Default to deny. *Example:* Firewall blocks all traffic by default; only specific rules allow traffic.
5. **Economy of Mechanism** — Keep security simple. *Example:* Using a vetted TLS library instead of custom cryptography.
6. **Complete Mediation** — Every access checked every time. *Example:* Re-authenticating a session token on every API call.
7. **Open Design** — Security via public, peer-reviewed algorithms. *Example:* AES, RSA — algorithms are public; only the key is secret.
8. **Least Common Mechanism** — Minimize shared resources. *Example:* Use per-tenant encryption keys in a cloud SaaS platform.

---

#### Part (b) — Availability Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Current Availability (2 marks):**
$$A_{current} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \dfrac{1500}{1500 + 50} = \dfrac{1500}{1550}$$

$$A_{current} = 0.9677 \approx 96.77\%$$

**Step 2 — Target Availability (1 mark):**
$$A_{target} = 99.9\% = 0.999$$

**Step 3 — Formulate the equation (1 mark):**

We keep $MTBF = 1500$ and solve for new $MTTR$ such that $A = 0.999$.

$$0.999 = \dfrac{1500}{1500 + MTTR_{new}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for MTTR_new (2 marks):**

$$1500 + MTTR_{new} = \dfrac{1500}{0.999}$$

$$1500 + MTTR_{new} = 1501.5015\ldots$$

$$MTTR_{new} = 1501.5015 - 1500 = 1.5015\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 5 — Required Reduction (1 mark):**
$$\Delta MTTR = 50 - 1.5015 = 48.4985\ \text{hours}$$

**Conclusion (extra credit remark):** The MTTR must be reduced by approximately **48.5 hours** (a 97% reduction) to meet the three-nines SLA. This typically requires automation, hot-standby systems, and a mature incident response runbook.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 1, Topic 1**

- **SSE** = Systems Security Engineering. Builds *trustworthy secure systems* (not just secure, but *trustworthy*).
- **Trustworthiness** ⊃ {Security, Safety, Reliability, Resilience, Privacy}. Security alone is insufficient.
- **CIA Triad** → extended to **CIANA+** in 2024 scheme. Memorize all five properties.
- **Eight Principles** (Defense-in-Depth, Least Privilege, SoD, Fail-Safe Defaults, Economy of Mechanism, Complete Mediation, Open Design, Least Common Mechanism) — exam-favorite list.
- **Risk Formula** = $R = P \times I$ (qualitative) and $R = f(T, V, I)$ (NIST-style).
- **ALE** = $SLE \times ARO$ = $(AV \times EF) \times ARO$. **ROSI** = $\frac{(ALE \times \%RM) - C_{control}}{C_{control}}$.
- **Availability** = $\frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$. Five-nines = 99.999%.
- **Lifecycle** has 5 phases: Concept → Development → Production → Operations → Retirement. Security must be engineered in from *Concept*.
- **Cost-of-fix** rises dramatically with lifecycle stage — shift-left argument.
- **Defense-in-Depth** is a layered, concentric protection model — draw the onion.
- **Differentiation Triplet:** Threat (the *who/what*), Vulnerability (the *weakness*), Risk (the *consequence*). Memorize the arrow direction.
- **NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1** is the authoritative reference — KTU aligns with it. Do not cite a specific page number; the KTU examiner accepts the volume reference.

> [!WARNING]  
> **Final KTU Valuation Warning:**  
> - Do NOT use `|` for absolute value in markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid`.  
> - Always state *units* (₹, hours, %) when writing numerical answers.  
> - When asked to “explain a principle,” always give: (i) name, (ii) definition, (iii) example, (iv) engineering benefit. Four points earn full 7 marks.  
> - For 14-mark questions, draw the **block diagram** in addition to the prose. A diagram without labels loses 2 marks.

*End of Module 1, Topic 1 — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Policies, Standards, Procedures, and Guidelines in Securing Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Role of Policies, Standards, Procedures, and Guidelines in Securing Systems # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Foundation of Security Governance Information security in any engineering organization does not stand on technology alone. It is anchored on a structured hierarchy of **documentation artifacts** that translate management intent into actionable engineering behavior. These artifacts are **Policies, Standards, Procedures, and Guidelines (PSPG)**. Together, they form the *governance spine* of a trustworthy secure system. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PECCT754 / Module 1):** > Students must be able to *distinguish, design, and apply* security policies, standards, procedures, and guidelines as the *foundational governance instruments* that secure critical engineering systems against both intentional attacks and unintentional failures. --- ## 1.2 Formal Definitions > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 1 — Security Policy** > A **Policy** is a high-level, formal statement of management intent, strategic objectives, and principles that govern the protection of information assets. It answers the question *"WHAT must be protected and WHY?"* Policies are **mandatory**, organization-wide, and require executive sign-off. They are technology-agnostic. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 2 — Security Standard** > A **Standard** is a mandatory, detailed specification that supports a policy. It defines the *uniform technical, physical, or administrative requirements* that systems, products, or processes must satisfy. It answers *"WHAT specific tools, configurations, or metrics must be used?"* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 3 — Security Procedure** > A **Procedure** (or *Standard Operating Procedure – SOP*) is a mandatory, step-by-step, chronological set of instructions that an employee or system administrator must follow to perform a specific security task. It answers *"HOW is the task performed, step by step?"* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition 4 — Security Guideline** > A **Guideline** is a non-mandatory, advisory recommendation that suggests best-practice approaches for achieving compliance with policies, standards, and procedures. It answers *"WHAT is the recommended way to do things?"* — leaving room for professional judgment. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Traffic" Model Imagine you are designing a national highway system. The four documents map to traffic governance perfectly: | Governance Layer | Traffic Analogy | Authority | |---|---|---| | **Policy** | *"All vehicles on Indian highways must be operated safely to protect human life."* | Ministry of Road Transport (Executive) | | **Standard** | *"All cars must have functional seat-belts, airbags, and a maximum speedometer of 200 km/h."* | Automotive Regulatory Body (BIS) | | **Procedure** | *"Step 1: Engage clutch. Step 2: Shift to 1st gear. Step 3: Release handbrake..."* | Driving School Manual | | **Guideline** | *"It is recommended to maintain a 3-second gap from the vehicle ahead during monsoon."* | Road Safety NGO Advisory | The hierarchy is **non-substitutable**. Skipping the policy makes the standard legally baseless; skipping the procedure makes the standard operationally useless; skipping the guideline makes the procedure inefficient. --- ## 1.4 Why PSPG Documents Matter in Trustworthy Systems Engineering In the KTU 2024 framework, *trustworthiness* of a system rests on three pillars: **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA)**, plus **Accountability** and **Assurance**. Without PSPG documents: - Engineers make **ad-hoc decisions**, creating inconsistent security postures. - Auditors (e.g., ISO 27001) cannot verify *due diligence*. - Legal defense in breach cases collapses (the *reasonableness defense* requires documented controls). > [!TIP] > **Board-Examiner Mnemonic — "P-S-P-G = Purpose, Spec, Process, Path":** > - **P**olicy = **P**urpose (intent) > - **S**tandard = **S**pecification (concrete rule) > - **P**rocedure = **P**rocess (workflow) > - **G**uideline = **G**uidance (path of wisdom) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework ## 2.1 The Hierarchical Pyramid of Security Documentation The four documents are **NOT interchangeable**. They follow a strict, top-down hierarchy in which each lower layer *derives its authority* from the layer above it. $$ \text{Policy} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Standard} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Procedure} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Guideline} $$ The pyramid structure is: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Level 1 — POLICY (Strategic, Broad, Rarely Changed)} \\ &\text{Level 2 — STANDARDS (Tactical, Technical, Periodically Updated)} \\ &\text{Level 3 — PROCEDURES (Operational, Detailed, Frequently Revised)} \\ &\text{Level 4 — GUIDELINES (Advisory, Contextual, Continuously Evolving)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Key Operating Principles - **Derivation Principle:** Every Standard must trace back to a Policy clause. Every Procedure must trace back to a Standard requirement. - **Mandatory vs Advisory:** Policy, Standard, and Procedure are **mandatory**; deviation requires formal exception. Guideline is **advisory**. - **Stability Principle:** Higher-level documents change less frequently. Policy revision = board meeting; Procedure revision = team meeting. - **Audience Principle:** Policy is read by *everyone*; Standards by *engineers/admins*; Procedures by *operators*; Guidelines by *decision-makers*. --- ## 2.2 Detailed Functional Role Analysis ### A. The Policy — The "Constitution" A Policy document contains the following engineering elements: - **Scope Statement** — which systems, people, and data it covers. - **Objective** — the security goal (e.g., "ensure CIA of customer PII"). - **Roles and Responsibilities** — who is the *Data Owner*, *Data Custodian*, *User*. - **High-Level Rules** — non-negotiable principles (e.g., "least privilege is mandatory"). - **Compliance and Enforcement** — penalties for non-compliance. - **Review Cycle** — typically every 12–24 months. > [!EXAMPLE] > **Sample Clause (Access Control Policy):** > *"All access to production databases containing personally identifiable information (PII) must be granted strictly on a need-to-know basis, authenticated via multi-factor authentication, and logged for a minimum of 365 days."* This sentence contains NO technology name. It is a *principle* that survives technology changes. ### B. The Standard — The "Rulebook" A Standard translates a Policy clause into measurable, technology-specific requirements. Each requirement must be **testable**. Standard formats use the pattern: $$ \text{[Policy Reference]} \rightarrow \text{[Requirement]} \rightarrow \text{[Metric/Threshold]} $$ > [!EXAMPLE] > **Sample Standard Clause (Password Standard derived from Access Control Policy):** > - All system passwords must be a minimum of **12 characters** in length. > - Passwords must contain at least 3 of: uppercase, lowercase, digit, special character. > - Passwords must be rotated every **90 days** for privileged accounts. > - Password history of last **5 passwords** must be maintained. ### C. The Procedure — The "Recipe" A Procedure is the *operational manual*. It is written in **imperative voice** (do this, then this) and must be reproducible by a junior engineer. Structure: $$ \text{Trigger Event} \rightarrow \text{Pre-conditions} \rightarrow \text{Step-by-Step Actions} \rightarrow \text{Expected Outcome} \rightarrow \text{Escalation Path} $$ > [!EXAMPLE] > **Sample Procedure (Suspected Malware Incident Response):** > 1. Disconnect the affected endpoint from the network (do not power off). > 2. Notify the SOC analyst on duty within 15 minutes. > 3. Capture volatile memory using `winpmem_mini.exe` → save to encrypted USB. > 4. Submit hash of suspicious file to internal threat-intel platform. > 5. Await SOC instructions; do NOT reboot or reimage. ### D. The Guideline — The "Mentor's Advice" A Guideline is written in **indicative voice** (you may consider, it is recommended). It is the only document where professional judgment is explicitly permitted. > [!EXAMPLE] > **Sample Guideline (Secure Software Development):** > *"It is recommended that developers perform peer code review using a checklist of the OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities before merging pull requests into the main branch. Automated SAST tools (e.g., SonarQube) should supplement, not replace, manual review."* --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparative Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table 2.1 — Definitive PSPG Comparison Matrix (Board-Exam Ready)** | Attribute | Policy | Standard | Procedure | Guideline | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Mandatory?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No (Advisory) | | **Audience** | All employees | Engineers / Admins | Operators / End-users | Decision-makers | | **Granularity** | Very High (Strategic) | High (Technical) | Low (Operational) | Variable (Contextual) | | **Frequency of Change** | 1–2 years | 6–12 months | As needed | Continuous | | **Answers** | WHY & WHAT | WHAT exactly | HOW step-by-step | WHAT is best practice | | **Failure Consequence** | Legal / Regulatory | Audit Failure | Operational Error | Sub-optimal Outcome | | **Tone** | Declarative | Prescriptive | Imperative | Suggestive | | **Author** | Senior Management / CISO | Security Architect | Team Lead / SME | SME / Best-Practice Body | | **Compliance Frameworks Referenced** | ISO 27001 A.5 | NIST 800-53 | ITIL / SOPs | OWASP / SANS | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In the **KTU 2024 outcome-based curriculum**, the ability to design PSPG artifacts is a **Program Outcome (PO-5: Modern Tool Usage)** and **PO-12: Life-long Learning** enabler. Specifically: - **Software Industry:** ISO/IEC 27001 certification of a company requires documented Policy + Standards. PCI-DSS for payment systems requires documented Procedures. - **Critical Infrastructure:** Power-grid SCADA systems require NIST 800-82 standards and NERC-CIP procedures. - **Healthcare:** HIPAA Security Rule mandates documented policies for ePHI protection. - **Automotive:** ISO/SAE 21434 for automotive cybersecurity mandates a *Cybersecurity Policy* and *Engineering Process Standards* for every ECU. > [!TIP] > **University Exam Tip:** A common 14-mark KTU question asks you to *"Design a security policy framework for a given scenario."* Always structure your answer using the four-layer PSPG hierarchy — this is the examiner's expected template. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Design Methodology > [!NOTE] > **Why this section exists in a non-mathematical topic:** > In Engineering of Trustworthy Secure Systems, "derivation" is replaced by *design methodology*. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step logical framework** for designing a PSPG suite, as required for KTU 14-mark board exam answers. Each step is fully expanded. --- ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation of a PSPG Suite from Business Requirements ### **Step 1 — Asset and Risk Identification** Before writing any document, perform a structured *Asset-Risk Inventory*. $$ \text{Asset} = \{ \text{Hardware},\; \text{Software},\; \text{Data},\; \text{People},\; \text{Process} \} $$ For each asset, list: - **Threats** — what can go wrong? (e.g., unauthorized access, ransomware) - **Vulnerabilities** — what weakness exists? (e.g., unpatched server, weak password) - **Impact** — what is the damage? (e.g., ₹50 lakh loss, regulatory fine) - **Likelihood** — probability of occurrence (1–5 scale). > **Engineering Logic:** The Policy will *prioritize* assets based on this risk ranking. The asset at highest risk (e.g., customer PII database) becomes the *anchor* of the Access Control Policy. --- ### **Step 2 — Draft the Security Policy (Top-Down)** Use the following derivation template: $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Policy Drafting Equation:} \\ &\text{Policy} = \text{Scope} + \text{Objective} + \text{Principles} + \text{Roles} + \text{Enforcement} \end{aligned} $$ **Sub-step 2.1 — Define Scope.** List the *boundaries*: which systems, geographies, business units, and personnel are covered. **Sub-step 2.2 — State the Objective.** Use a single sentence in *future tense* (e.g., "The organization shall protect all customer PII from unauthorized disclosure, modification, and destruction."). **Sub-step 2.3 — Enumerate Principles.** Principles are *immutable* (e.g., least privilege, defense-in-depth, separation of duties, zero-trust). Each principle becomes a **clause number** in the policy document. **Sub-step 2.4 — Assign Roles.** $$ \text{Classic Three-Role Model} = \{ \text{Data Owner},\; \text{Data Custodian},\; \text{Data User} \} $$ - **Data Owner** (e.g., Business Head) — accountable for the asset. - **Data Custodian** (e.g., IT/SOC) — responsible for technical protection. - **Data User** (e.g., employee) — responsible for compliant usage. **Sub-step 2.5 — Specify Enforcement.** Define the *consequence matrix*: $$ \text{Violation Severity} = \{ \text{Minor} \rightarrow \text{Counseling},\; \text{Major} \rightarrow \text{Access Revocation},\; \text{Critical} \rightarrow \text{Termination + Legal} \} $$ --- ### **Step 3 — Derive Standards from Policy Clauses** Apply the mapping function: $$ \text{Standard Requirement} = f(\text{Policy Clause},\; \text{Technology Stack},\; \text{Best Practice Framework}) $$ **Sub-step 3.1 — Reference the Policy Clause.** Every standard clause must begin with *"As mandated by Policy §X.Y..."* **Sub-step 3.2 — Specify Measurable Metrics.** Replace vague terms with numbers: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Vague:} & \quad \text{"Strong password required."} \\ \text{Mapped:} & \quad \text{"Minimum 12 characters, MFA enforced, rotation every 90 days."} \end{aligned} $$ **Sub-step 3.3 — Cite the Authority Standard.** Reference external frameworks (NIST, ISO, CIS Benchmarks) to give the standard *defensibility* in an audit. **Sub-step 3.4 — Define Exceptions.** No security rule is absolute. The standard must specify the *exception process* — typically a written waiver approved by the CISO with a defined expiry date. --- ### **Step 4 — Author Procedures from Standards** The derivation equation is: $$ \text{Procedure} = \text{Use Case} + \text{Pre-conditions} + \text{Numbered Actions} + \text{Verification} + \text{Escalation} $$ **Sub-step 4.1 — Identify the Use Case.** Each procedure addresses *one* standard requirement expressed as an action (e.g., "How to onboard a new employee with system access"). **Sub-step 4.2 — Pre-conditions.** List what must be true *before* starting (e.g., "HR ticket raised, manager approval attached"). **Sub-step 4.3 — Numbered Actions.** Use the *imperative voice* with no ambiguity: $$ \begin{aligned} &1. \text{ Open the IAM console as an administrator.} \\ &2. \text{ Click 'New User' and enter the employee ID from the HR ticket.} \\ &3. \text{ Assign the role group 'Standard\_User\_Group' per the Access Standard §3.2.} \\ &4. \text{ Enable MFA and verify the user's mobile number.} \\ &5. \text{ Document the access grant in the access register log.} \end{aligned} $$ **Sub-step 4.4 — Verification Step.** Add a *check* at the end: "Verify by logging in as the new user from a clean browser session." **Sub-step 4.5 — Escalation Path.** "If MFA fails to register, escalate to the IAM on-call engineer via ServiceNow P1 ticket." --- ### **Step 5 — Compose Guidelines for Ambiguous Zones** Guidelines are derived where: - The Standard cannot anticipate every technical scenario. - Professional judgment is required (e.g., choosing between two equally compliant tools). $$ \text{Guideline} = \text{Context} + \text{Recommended Approach} + \text{Trade-off Discussion} + \text{Decision Criteria} $$ > **Example Derivation:** Policy says "use encryption." Standard mandates "AES-256 for data at rest." Guideline then says: *"When storing keys in AWS, prefer AWS KMS over self-managed HSMs unless regulatory mandate requires on-premises HSMs (e.g., RBI Cyber Security Framework). Evaluate based on: (a) regulatory scope, (b) operational cost, (c) audit trail requirement."* --- ### **Step 6 — Cross-Traceability Matrix** The final engineering output is a **PSPG Traceability Matrix**: $$ \begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|} \hline \textbf{Policy Clause} & \textbf{Standard Req.} & \textbf{Procedure} & \textbf{Guideline} \\ \hline \text{§4.1 Least Privilege} & \text{ST-ACS-007} & \text{PROC-OB-03} & \text{GD-RBAC-02} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ This matrix is the *single most important artifact* in any KTU 14-mark PSPG design answer and in any real-world ISO 27001 audit. --- ### **Step 7 — Validation and Approval Workflow** $$ \text{Draft} \rightarrow \text{Peer Review} \rightarrow \text{Stakeholder Consultation} \rightarrow \text{Legal Review} \rightarrow \text{CISO Sign-off} \rightarrow \text{CEO Approval} \rightarrow \text{Publication} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The PSPG Hierarchical Pyramid The following Mermaid `flowchart` block illustrates the **functional relationship and information flow** between the four PSPG layers. Note the use of purely alphanumeric node identifiers prefixed with letters, as required by the KTU diagram engine safeguards. ```mermaid flowchart TD node1["POLICY LAYER
Strategic Intent
Mandatory | High-Level | Rarely Changed"]:::policyNode node2["STANDARDS LAYER
Technical Specifications
Mandatory | Measurable | Periodically Updated"]:::standardNode node3["PROCEDURES LAYER
Step-by-Step Instructions
Mandatory | Imperative | As-Needed Updates"]:::procedureNode node4["GUIDELINES LAYER
Best-Practice Recommendations
Advisory | Indicative | Continuously Evolving"]:::guidelineNode node5["ASSET INVENTORY
Hardware Software Data People Process"]:::assetNode node6["RISK ASSESSMENT
Threats Vulnerabilities Impact Likelihood"]:::riskNode node7["TRACEABILITY MATRIX
PSPG Cross-Reference"]:::traceNode node8["AUDIT & COMPLIANCE
ISO 27001 NIST 800-53 PCI-DSS"]:::auditNode node5 --> node1 node6 --> node1 node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node1 --> node7 node2 --> node7 node3 --> node7 node4 --> node7 node7 --> node8 classDef policyNode fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1e3a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef standardNode fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef procedureNode fill:#ef6c00,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef guidelineNode fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef assetNode fill:#455a64,stroke:#263238,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef riskNode fill:#c62828,stroke:#8e0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef traceNode fill:#00838f,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef auditNode fill:#37474f,stroke:#1c313a,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff ``` ### Diagram Interpretation - **Blue (Policy)** is the *root*; it draws inputs from the asset and risk analyses on the left. - **Green (Standards)** is *derived* from the policy and *feeds* the procedures. - **Orange (Procedures)** is the *operational layer* that engineers use daily. - **Purple (Guidelines)** is the *wisdom layer* — a parallel advisory stream, not a strict descendant. - **Cyan (Traceability Matrix)** is the *audit glue* that proves the chain of governance. - **Grey-Blue (Audit)** is the *external verification* by ISO/NIST/PCI auditors. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PSPG Engine This second diagram maps the **functional architecture** of how a PSPG suite operates inside a real engineering organization. It is a block-flow schematic suitable for KTU board exam diagrams. ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Executive
Management"]:::execNode nodeB["CISO Office
Governance Lead"]:::cisoNode nodeC["Security
Architects"]:::archNode nodeD["Team Leads
and SMEs"]:::teamNode nodeE["End Users
and Operators"]:::userNode nodeF["Internal Audit
and Compliance"]:::auditNode nodeG["External Auditors
ISO NIST PCI-DSS"]:::extNode nodeA -->|"Approves Policy"| nodeB nodeB -->|"Drafts Policy"| nodeC nodeC -->|"Translates to Standard"| nodeD nodeD -->|"Writes Procedure"| nodeE nodeC -.->|"Provides Guideline"| nodeD nodeD -.->|"Provides Guideline"| nodeE nodeE -->|"Complies With"| nodeF nodeF -->|"Reports to"| nodeA nodeF -->|"Evidence to"| nodeG nodeB -->|"Reviews Quarterly"| nodeF classDef execNode fill:#0d47a1,stroke:#002171,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff classDef cisoNode fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#003300,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef archNode fill:#4a148c,stroke:#12005e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef teamNode fill:#bf360c,stroke:#3e2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef userNode fill:#f9a825,stroke:#6d4c41,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef auditNode fill:#263238,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff classDef extNode fill:#37474f,stroke:#102027,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff ``` ### Architecture Interpretation - The **solid arrows** denote *mandatory flow* (Policy → Standard → Procedure). - The **dotted arrows** denote *advisory flow* (Guideline flow). - The **feedback loop** (Internal Audit → Executive Management) is the *continuous improvement cycle* required by ISO 27001 Clause 9.3. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Conflating Policy and Standard** — Many students write *"use a strong password"* as a policy. That is a *standard*. The policy says *"protect credentials"*. Examiners deduct up to 3 marks. > 2. **Skipping the Traceability** — A 14-mark PSPG design question without a traceability matrix is graded as *incomplete*. Always include the matrix. > 3. **Ignoring "Mandatory vs Advisory"** — If you mark a guideline as mandatory, you lose marks. The examiner looks for the explicit "advisory" disclaimer. > 4. **Writing Technology into a Policy** — Policies must be technology-agnostic. If your policy says *"use Windows Defender"*, that is a standard, not a policy. > 5. **No Examples** — Abstract answers without a sample clause for each layer are marked weak. Always give one concrete example per document. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]** **Differentiate between a Security Policy and a Security Standard with suitable examples. (3 Marks)** ### **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Security Policy** is a high-level, technology-agnostic statement of management intent that defines *what* must be protected and *why*. It is mandatory, organization-wide, and changes infrequently. For example, an Access Control Policy clause states: *"All access to production systems must be granted on a need-to-know basis and authenticated using multi-factor authentication."* A **Security Standard** is a mandatory, technology-specific specification that supports a policy. It defines *how* the policy is implemented using specific tools and metrics. For example, the same policy clause translates to a Standard that mandates: *"MFA must use TOTP-based authenticator applications; password minimum length 12 characters; rotation every 90 days; account lockout after 5 failed attempts."* **Key Distinction:** The policy states the *principle*; the standard specifies the *measurable implementation*. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Paper)]** **What is the role of Guidelines in a security governance framework? Why are they considered advisory and not mandatory? (3 Marks)** ### **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Guidelines serve as **advisory recommendations** that suggest best-practice approaches for achieving compliance with policies, standards, and procedures. They provide professional judgment and contextual advice in situations where the standard cannot dictate a single rigid approach. For example, a secure coding guideline might recommend *"use parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection"* and provide example code patterns, but leaves the developer free to choose the specific ORM framework. Guidelines are advisory because the operational environment is too varied for one-size-fits-all rules. Forcing every "best practice" as mandatory would paralyze engineering agility. Guidelines preserve *engineering judgment* while still steering teams toward proven patterns. The accountability for deviating from a guideline is less severe than for violating a policy. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries 14 marks split into 7 + 7. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]** **A B.Tech project team is developing a cloud-based Hospital Management System (HMS) that stores Electronic Health Records (EHR) of patients. The system is hosted on AWS and accessed by doctors, nurses, receptionists, and patients.** **(a)** Design a comprehensive **Information Security Policy suite** for the HMS, identifying at least four policies with one sample clause each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For the Access Control Policy designed in part (a), derive the corresponding **Security Standard** and write one **Operating Procedure** for the *"Onboarding of a new doctor"* process. Also include one **Guideline** for the same. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** #### **Part (a) — Policy Suite (7 Marks)** The HMS must protect EHR data, comply with the *Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act (DISHA)* and *IT Act 2000/2008 (India)*. Four policies are designed: **Policy 1: Information Classification and Handling Policy** > *"All patient health information shall be classified as 'Restricted' by default and shall not be disclosed, copied, or transmitted outside the HMS without explicit patient consent and authorized clinical justification."* > *(1.5 marks — for stating the policy name and a sample clause)* **Policy 2: Access Control Policy** > *"Access to EHR data shall be granted strictly on a need-to-know, role-based basis. Every access attempt shall be authenticated using multi-factor authentication and logged for a minimum retention period of 365 days."* > *(1.5 marks)* **Policy 3: Incident Response Policy** > *"Any suspected breach, unauthorized access, or loss of patient data shall be reported to the Information Security Officer within 60 minutes of detection. The organization shall preserve all forensic evidence and notify affected patients within 72 hours as mandated by applicable law."* > *(1.5 marks)* **Policy 4: Data Backup and Disaster Recovery Policy** > *"All EHR data shall be backed up daily to a geographically separate region and the organization shall demonstrate the ability to restore critical systems within 4 hours (Recovery Time Objective) with a maximum data loss of 15 minutes (Recovery Point Objective)."* > *(1.5 marks)* **Conclusion Statement (1 mark):** All four policies are technology-agnostic, mandatory, approved by the Hospital Director, and reviewed annually. --- #### **Part (b) — Standard, Procedure, Guideline (7 Marks)** **Security Standard derived from Access Control Policy §2:** > *"As mandated by the Access Control Policy §2, the following technical standards apply to the HMS hosted on AWS:* > - *All user accounts shall be provisioned through AWS IAM Identity Center with role-based access groups: Doctor-Group, Nurse-Group, Receptionist-Group, Patient-Group.* > - *Multi-factor authentication shall use TOTP-based authenticator apps (e.g., Google Authenticator) or hardware tokens. SMS-based MFA is prohibited.* > - *Password policy: minimum 12 characters, 3 of 4 character classes, rotation every 90 days, history of last 5 passwords, lockout after 5 failed attempts.* > - *All access logs shall be forwarded to AWS CloudTrail and retained in an Amazon S3 bucket with object-lock for 365 days.* > - *Privileged accounts (DBAs, System Admins) shall use just-in-time (JIT) access with a maximum session duration of 4 hours."* > *(2.5 marks for the standard)* **Operating Procedure — Onboarding of a New Doctor:** > *"Trigger: HR system creates a new doctor record. Pre-conditions: (1) Medical Council Registration verified, (2) Department Head approval attached in HR ticket.* > *Steps:* > *1. The IT Helpdesk analyst logs into the AWS IAM Identity Center console using the privileged admin account.* > *2. Select 'Add User', enter the doctor's official email ID, first name, and last name.* > *3. Assign the user to the group 'Doctor-Group' as specified in Standard §2.1.* > *4. Under MFA settings, select 'TOTP' and generate a QR code.* > *5. Send the QR code via secure channel to the doctor's verified mobile number; instruct them to scan using Google Authenticator.* > *6. Set an initial temporary password that meets Standard §2.3 and flag for change on first login.* > *7. Verify access by logging in to the HMS test environment as the new doctor.* > *8. Document the access grant in the access register spreadsheet with ticket reference.* > *Escalation: If MFA registration fails after 3 attempts, raise a P1 ServiceNow ticket to the Identity Team on-call engineer."* > *(3 marks for the procedure — 0.5 mark for trigger/preconditions, 2 marks for the steps, 0.5 mark for verification/escalation)* **Guideline — Best Practice for Doctor Access:** > *"It is recommended that the Doctor-Group's IAM role be further scoped using AWS IAM Permission Boundaries to limit access to only the specific hospital department (e.g., Cardiology vs. Oncology). When integrating with third-party diagnostic labs, prefer short-lived OAuth 2.0 tokens over static API keys, and rotate lab-API credentials every 30 days. Developers should consider implementing attribute-based access control (ABAC) using doctor specialization tags for fine-grained access in the future."* > *(1.5 marks for the guideline — 0.5 mark for advisory tone, 0.5 mark for the recommendation, 0.5 mark for the trade-off context)* --- ### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model Paper)]** **A fintech startup in Kerala is launching a mobile payment wallet application. The app processes UPI transactions and stores sensitive KYC data (Aadhaar, PAN) of 5 million users.** **(a)** Explain the **PSPG documentation hierarchy** and design a **comprehensive Security Policy** for the wallet application covering data privacy, access control, and incident response. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** From the policies designed in part (a), derive **one Security Standard** (with at least 4 measurable requirements), **one Procedure** for *"Resetting a User's Forgotten PIN"*, and **one Guideline** for *"Secure Storage of KYC Documents in Cloud Database"*. Present your answer using a **Traceability Matrix** at the end. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B (Outline for Self-Study)** **Part (a) — 7 Marks:** - Explain the PSPG pyramid (2 marks). - Design three policies (Data Privacy, Access Control, Incident Response) with one sample clause each (3 × 1.5 = 4.5 marks). - Conclude with reference to RBI Cyber Security Framework and IT Act compliance (0.5 mark). **Part (b) — 7 Marks:** - Standard for Access Control (e.g., MFA mandatory, encryption AES-256, session timeout 5 min, key rotation 90 days) — 2.5 marks. - Procedure for PIN reset (with trigger, preconditions, numbered steps, verification, escalation) — 3 marks. - Guideline for KYC storage (recommend tokenization over raw Aadhaar, use HSM for encryption keys) — 1 mark. - Traceability Matrix (Policy § → Standard § → Procedure → Guideline) — 0.5 mark. *(Detailed step-by-step is omitted here to avoid redundancy with Question A; the same analytical framework from SECTION 3 applies.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Box Before Exam** - **Policy = WHY & WHAT (Strategic, Mandatory, Rarely changed, Tech-agnostic, Audience: Everyone, Approver: CEO/CISO).** - **Standard = WHAT exactly (Tactical, Mandatory, Measurable, Tech-specific, Audience: Engineers, Approver: Security Architect).** - **Procedure = HOW step-by-step (Operational, Mandatory, Imperative voice, Audience: Operators, Approver: Team Lead).** - **Guideline = Best-practice advice (Advisory, Indicative voice, Audience: Decision-makers, Approver: SME).** - **Hierarchy is non-substitutable** — every lower layer derives authority from the layer above. - **Traceability Matrix is the audit glue** — always include it in 14-mark answers. - **Policy cannot name a technology** — if it does, it is a Standard. - **Guidelines are the ONLY non-mandatory documents** in the PSPG suite. - **Three classic roles** must appear in any Access Control Policy: Data Owner, Data Custodian, Data User. - **Compliance frameworks** to cite: ISO 27001, NIST 800-53, PCI-DSS, RBI Cybersecurity Framework, HIPAA, GDPR. - **Mnemonic:** **PSPG = Purpose, Spec, Process, Guidance.** - **Cycle:** Draft → Peer Review → Stakeholder → Legal → CISO → CEO → Publish → Quarterly Review → Annual Update. - **Consequence Matrix** is required in Policy to give the document enforcement teeth. - **Exception Process** is required in Standard to handle legitimate waivers. - **Pre-conditions + Verification + Escalation** are the three mandatory elements of a Procedure. - **Trade-off discussion** distinguishes a Guideline from a Standard (Standards have no trade-offs — they are absolute).

topics.title: Transferable and Technical Skills of Effective Systems Security Engineers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transferable and Technical Skills of Effective Systems Security Engineers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

A **Systems Security Engineer** is a specialised information technology professional who is responsible for designing, implementing, monitoring, and maintaining security controls, defence mechanisms, and countermeasure architectures that protect an organisation's information systems, networks, applications, and data assets against internal and external threats.

The KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) for **PECCT754 – Engineering of Trustworthy Secure Systems** defines the role of an effective Systems Security Engineer as the convergence of two distinct but complementary competency domains:

1. **Transferable (Soft) Skills** – Interpersonal, cognitive, and managerial capabilities that are domain-agnostic and portable across multiple engineering disciplines.
2. **Technical (Hard) Skills** – Domain-specific, measurable, and tool-driven competencies related to cryptography, secure coding, network security, risk assessment, and systems architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECCT754):** A graduate engineer is expected to possess a balanced portfolio of *transferable* and *technical* skills to engineer, evaluate, and manage trustworthy secure systems in real-world industry environments. The KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) frequently tests the student's ability to *classify*, *justify*, and *map* these skills to industry-grade security engineering roles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Security Architect**
>
> Imagine a senior architect tasked with designing a **hospital**. The hospital must (a) protect patients from intruders, (b) keep medical records confidential, (c) ensure doctors can access critical systems 24/7, and (d) survive natural disasters. The architect needs two types of expertise:
>
> - **Transferable Skills** — Communication with the hospital board, project management, ethical reasoning, leadership during a crisis, analytical thinking to weigh trade-offs. These are the *architect's soft toolkit*.
> - **Technical Skills** — Knowledge of fire-resistant materials, structural load calculations, HVAC design, electrical schematics, and medical gas pipelines. These are the *architect's hard toolkit*.
>
> A **Systems Security Engineer** is the *hospital architect of the digital world*. The **transferable skills** allow them to lead teams, communicate risk to non-technical executives, and ethically reason through complex trade-offs. The **technical skills** allow them to engineer firewalls, audit cryptographic implementations, and design zero-trust architectures.
>
> Without either set, the "hospital" collapses: too many soft skills and you have a charismatic leader who cannot read a network diagram; too many hard skills and you have a brilliant technician who cannot explain a breach to the board of directors.

### 1.3 The Trustworthy Secure Systems Context

The course code **PECCT754** uses the word *"Trustworthy"*, which by KTU/NIST convention refers to systems that exhibit verifiable properties of **security, reliability, safety, resilience, and privacy**. An engineer who can deliver such systems must therefore be both *technically deep* and *professionally versatile* — a duality captured precisely by the transferable-technical skills framework.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Skills Matrix

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Skills — Structured Logic Breakdown

A rigorous analysis of the skills required can be organised into the following hierarchical logic:

#### **A. Transferable Skills (Soft / Interpersonal / Cognitive)**

1. **Analytical & Critical Thinking**
   - *Why:* Security problems are multi-causal; root-cause analysis is non-linear.
   - *How:* Hypothesis-driven investigation, log forensics, threat modelling.
2. **Communication Skills (Verbal & Written)**
   - *Why:* Security findings must be conveyed to executives, regulators, and developers who lack a security vocabulary.
   - *How:* Security advisories, incident reports, executive dashboards.
3. **Problem-Solving under Pressure**
   - *Why:* Incident response is a time-critical, high-stakes activity.
   - *How:* Triage, containment, eradication, and recovery (NIST IR lifecycle).
4. **Teamwork & Collaboration**
   - *Why:* Security is a cross-functional discipline (DevSecOps, Red/Blue/Purple teams).
   - *How:* Joint threat-modelling workshops, shared incident playbooks.
5. **Leadership & Project Management**
   - *Why:* Security programmes require budget, prioritisation, and stakeholder buy-in.
   - *How:* Roadmaps, RACI matrices, risk-based prioritisation.
6. **Ethical Reasoning & Professional Integrity**
   - *Why:* Engineers hold privileged access to sensitive data and systems.
   - *How:* Adherence to codes such as (ISC)² Code of Ethics, ACM/IEEE codes.
7. **Continuous Learning & Adaptability**
   - *Why:* Threat landscape evolves daily; CVE databases grow continuously.
   - *How:* Self-paced labs, CTF competitions, security conference participation.
8. **Legal & Regulatory Awareness**
   - *Why:* Compliance failures invoke GDPR, IT Act 2000, DPDP Act 2023, HIPAA penalties.
   - *How:* Privacy-by-design, DPIA preparation.

#### **B. Technical Skills (Hard / Domain-Specific)**

1. **Systems & Operating System Internals**
   - Linux/Windows kernel, file systems, processes, memory management.
2. **Networking & Network Security**
   - TCP/IP, DNS, BGP, TLS, VPN, firewalls, IDS/IPS, zero-trust networking.
3. **Cryptography & PKI**
   - Symmetric/asymmetric algorithms, hash functions, digital signatures, certificate management.
4. **Secure Software Development (SSDLC)**
   - Threat modelling (STRIDE/DREAD), SAST/DAST, OWASP Top 10, code review.
5. **Identity & Access Management (IAM)**
   - RBAC, ABAC, MFA, SSO, OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, SAML.
6. **Vulnerability Assessment & Penetration Testing (VAPT)**
   - Tools: Nmap, Burp Suite, Metasploit, Wireshark, OWASP ZAP.
7. **Cloud & Container Security**
   - AWS/Azure/GCP security services, Kubernetes security, Docker hardening.
8. **Risk Management & Governance**
   - ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, COBIT, FAIR risk quantification.
9. **Security Operations & Monitoring (SOC)**
   - SIEM (Splunk, QRadar), SOAR, EDR, log analysis.
10. **Digital Forensics & Incident Response (DFIR)**
    - Chain of custody, memory/disk forensics, malware analysis.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Skills Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **golden revision sheet** for the Module 1 KTU 2024 ESE. Memorise the mapping of skill → sub-domain → artefact/evidence.

| # | Skill Category | Skill Name | Sub-Domain | Industry Artefact / Evidence | KTU 2024 CO Mapping |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transferable | Analytical Thinking | Threat Modelling | STRIDE diagram of an e-banking app | CO1 |
| 2 | Transferable | Communication | Risk Reporting | Executive risk dashboard (1-pager) | CO1, CO6 |
| 3 | Transferable | Ethics | Privacy Engineering | DPIA report (GDPR/DPDP aligned) | CO5, CO6 |
| 4 | Transferable | Teamwork | DevSecOps | Joint SAST review notes | CO4, CO5 |
| 5 | Technical | Cryptography | TLS / PKI | Hand-crafted X.509 cert lab | CO2, CO3 |
| 6 | Technical | Network Security | Firewall / IDS | iptables + Snort configuration | CO2, CO4 |
| 7 | Technical | Secure Coding | OWASP Top 10 | Patched vulnerable code repo | CO3, CO4 |
| 8 | Technical | IAM | Zero Trust | RBAC + MFA policy document | CO3, CO4 |
| 9 | Technical | VAPT | Pen-Testing | Nmap + Metasploit report | CO3, CO4 |
| 10 | Technical | Cloud Security | AWS / K8s | CIS benchmark hardening checklist | CO4 |
| 11 | Technical | DFIR | Incident Handling | Forensic timeline + IOC list | CO4, CO5 |
| 12 | Technical | Risk Mgmt | ISO 27001 | Statement of Applicability (SoA) | CO1, CO5 |

**Note on Markdown:** All `|` characters within mathematical set notation or bitwise OR expressions inside tables have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve table syntax.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Banking & FinTech:** Engineers use transferable skills to *negotiate* security SLAs with vendors, and technical skills to engineer *secure transaction pipelines*.
- **Healthcare:** Ethical reasoning (HIPAA/DPDP) prevents unauthorised data exposure; cryptography skills protect patient records.
- **Critical Infrastructure (Power, Water):** Problem-solving under pressure handles SCADA/ICS incidents; technical skills ensure *air-gapped* network integrity.
- **Defence & Aerospace:** Leadership and continuous learning align with classified threat-intelligence lifecycles.

### 2.4 The Skills-Synergy Principle

$$
\text{Trustworthy System} \;\propto\; f(\text{Transferable Skills}, \text{Technical Skills})
$$

A system is *trustworthy* only when both inputs are present. A system engineered by a technically brilliant but ethically compromised engineer is **untrustworthy**. A system engineered by a well-meaning generalist is **insecure**. Thus, the KTU 2024 syllabus demands *concurrent development* of both.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Skill Mapping & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Four-Stage Skill Acquisition Pathway

A Systems Security Engineer's professional maturity progresses through four stages. Each stage is exhaustively described:

---

**Stage 1 — Foundational Awareness (0–6 months)**

- *Transferable:* Develops structured analytical thinking; reads security blogs and threat-intelligence feeds.
- *Technical:* Learns operating-system fundamentals (Linux CLI, Windows PowerShell), basic networking (OSI/TCP-IP model), and an introductory programming language (Python).
- *Outcome:* Can identify basic vulnerabilities in a CTF beginner room.

**Stage 2 — Core Competence (6–18 months)**

- *Transferable:* Writes incident reports; collaborates in a SOC environment; presents findings to a small audience.
- *Technical:* Performs vulnerability scans using Nessus/OpenVAS; writes secure code; configures firewalls; understands OWASP Top 10.
- *Outcome:* Capable of performing a basic web application penetration test.

**Stage 3 — Professional Proficiency (18–36 months)**

- *Transferable:* Leads a small security team; manages a security project budget; negotiates with stakeholders.
- *Technical:* Architects zero-trust networks; conducts threat-modelling workshops; integrates SAST/DAST into CI/CD pipelines; designs IAM policies.
- *Outcome:* Owns a security sub-programme (e.g., Cloud Security Workstream).

**Stage 4 — Expert Mastery (3+ years)**

- *Transferable:* Influences enterprise security strategy; mentors junior engineers; publishes research; speaks at conferences.
- *Technical:* Designs cryptographic protocols; reverse-engineers malware; performs red-team engagements; authors security standards.
- *Outcome:* A trusted advisor to C-suite; recognised SME in a security sub-discipline.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Skills vs Engineering Tasks

| Engineering Task | Required Transferable Skills | Required Technical Skills | KTU CO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conduct a Penetration Test | Analytical thinking, ethics (scope adherence) | Nmap, Metasploit, Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP | CO3, CO4 |
| Respond to a Ransomware Incident | Problem-solving, communication, teamwork | SIEM, EDR, forensic toolkits, network packet analysis | CO4, CO5 |
| Architect a Zero-Trust Network | Leadership, project management, analytical thinking | IAM (RBAC/ABAC), micro-segmentation, MFA, NAC | CO1, CO4 |
| Publish a CVE / Patch | Communication, ethics, continuous learning | Reverse engineering, secure coding, SAST/DAST | CO3 |
| Achieve ISO 27001 Certification | Leadership, legal awareness, communication | ISMS design, risk assessment, SoA, internal audit | CO1, CO5 |
| Implement DPDP Act 2023 Compliance | Ethics, legal awareness, communication | Data classification, encryption, DLP, consent management | CO5, CO6 |

### 3.3 The Skill-Pairing Theorem

For any trustworthy system engineering task $T$, the skill pairing must satisfy:

$$
\text{Proficiency}(T) \;=\; \alpha \cdot T_{trans} \;+\; \beta \cdot T_{tech}
$$

where:
- $T_{trans}$ = aggregate transferable skill score (0–10)
- $T_{tech}$ = aggregate technical skill score (0–10)
- $\alpha, \beta$ = task-dependent weighting constants such that $\alpha + \beta = 1$

**Example Derivation — Penetration Test Task:**

For a standard external web application penetration test, the empirical industry weighting is:
- $\alpha = 0.30$ (soft skills: report writing, scope adherence, ethics)
- $\beta = 0.70$ (hard skills: tooling, exploitation, scripting)

Suppose a junior engineer has $T_{trans} = 6$ and $T_{tech} = 5$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Proficiency}(T_{pentest}) &= 0.30 \times 6 + 0.70 \times 5 \\
&= 1.8 + 3.5 \\
&= 5.3 \;\; \text{(out of 10)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a *below-threshold* score — the engineer is not yet ready to lead a pentest. To reach a *deployment-ready* threshold of 7.0, the engineer must either:
- Raise $T_{tech}$ to 7.0 while keeping $T_{trans}$ at 6.0, **or**
- Raise $T_{trans}$ to 8.0 while keeping $T_{tech}$ at 5.0, **or**
- Pursue balanced growth, e.g., $T_{trans} = 7.0$ and $T_{tech} = 7.0$, yielding:
- $0.30 \times 7 + 0.70 \times 7 = 7.0$ ✓ (threshold met)

This derivation illustrates the KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO1, CO4) that *effective* security engineering requires *balanced* skill development — a single-minded focus on tools alone produces unbalanced engineers.

### 3.4 The Trust Triangle — Why Both Skill Sets Matter

The "Trust Triangle" is a pedagogical device used in PECCT754 to connect skills to outcomes:

- **Confidentiality** $\rightarrow$ preserved by technical skills (cryptography, IAM)
- **Integrity** $\rightarrow$ preserved by technical skills (hashing, digital signatures)
- **Availability** $\rightarrow$ preserved by both (technical: redundancy, DDoS mitigation; transferable: incident response coordination)
- **Accountability** $\rightarrow$ preserved by transferable skills (ethics, audit, governance)
- **Privacy** $\rightarrow$ preserved by both (technical: encryption, anonymisation; transferable: legal compliance, ethical design)

Thus, the **CIA Triad + Accountability + Privacy** (the extended *Trust Pentagon*) can only be achieved by combining both skill domains.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Systems Security Engineer Skills Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SSE["Systems Security Engineer"]
```

### 4.2 Skill Maturity Progression — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR

SME, CISO advisor, researcher"]
```

### 4.3 The Trust Pentagon — Mapping Skills to Security Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CONF["Confidentiality"]:::conf
    INT["Integrity"]:::int
    AVAIL["Availability"]:::avail
    ACC["Accountability"]:::acc
    PRIV["Privacy"]:::priv

    CONF --- INT
    INT --- AVAIL
    AVAIL --- ACC
    ACC --- PRIV
    PRIV --- CONF

    TH1["Technical: Cryptography, IAM"] --> CONF
    TH2["Technical: Hashing, Digital Signatures"] --> INT
    TH3["Technical: Redundancy, DDoS Mitigation"] --> AVAIL
    TS1["Transferable: Audit, Ethics"] --> ACC
    TS2["Transferable: Legal Awareness, Privacy Engineering"] --> PRIV

    classDef conf fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    classDef int fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define Systems Security Engineering. Differentiate between **transferable skills** and **technical skills** with two examples each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 2):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
