- Trigger **re-authorization** when significant changes occur or risk levels shift.
- Update the SSP, SAR, and POA&M continuously.

> [!TIP]
> **Continuous Monitoring Frequency (Typical):**
> - Security control assessments: **At least annually** for moderate-impact systems; **continuously** for high-impact.
> - Vulnerability scans: **Monthly** (minimum) for all federal systems.
> - Configuration management: **Continuous** automated assessment.

---

### 2.2 The RMF–SDLC Integration Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The RMF is **not a separate process** from the SDLC. It is executed **in parallel** with the system's development phases:

| **SDLC Phase**        | **RMF Steps Executed in Parallel**              |
| --------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- |
| Initiation / Planning | **Prepare, Categorize**                         |
| Requirements Analysis | **Select**                                      |
| Design / Development  | **Implement**                                   |
| Integration / Testing | **Assess**                                      |
| Deployment            | **Authorize**                                   |
| Operations & Maintenance | **Monitor** (continuous, throughout life)    |
| Disposal              | **Monitor** (final sanitization, media disposal)|

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates all RMF-critical concepts, formulas, and classifications that you must memorize for the KTU ESE.**

| **Concept / Parameter**            | **Definition / Formula / Value**                                                                                  | **KTU Significance** |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------- |
| Core Risk Formula                  | $R = P \times I$                                                                                                | Foundation of every risk decision. |
| Categorization Levels (FIPS 199)   | **LOW**, **MODERATE**, **HIGH** — applied to C, I, A                                                            | Determines control baseline. |
| System Categorization Rule         | Overall = **Maximum** (high-water mark) of C, I, A ratings                                                       | Step 1 outcome. |
| RMF Steps Count                    | **7** (Prepare, Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor)                                       | Most-asked. |
| Prepare Step Source                | Added in **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2**                                                                              | Differentiator. |
| Control Catalog                    | **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5**                                                                                       | 20 control families. |
| Assessment Standard                | **NIST SP 800-53A**                                                                                             | Examine / Interview / Test. |
| Continuous Monitoring Standard     | **NIST SP 800-137**                                                                                             | ISCM strategy. |
| Risk Assessment Standard           | **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1**                                                                                       | Threat × Vulnerability × Impact × Likelihood. |
| Minimum Assessment Frequency       | **Annually** (Moderate); **Continuous** (High)                                                                  | Auditor focus. |
| Authorization Decisions            | **ATO, DATO, IATO, Common Control Authorization**                                                              | All four are testable. |
| Key Roles                          | **AO** (Authorizing Official), **SCA** (Security Control Assessor), **ISSO** (Info. Sys. Security Officer), **System Owner** | Role-based questions. |
| RMF Tiers                          | **Tier 1** (Organization), **Tier 2** (Mission/Business), **Tier 3** (System)                                  | Governance hierarchy. |
| Impact Levels (FIPS 200 minimum)   | **LOW / MODERATE / HIGH** dictate minimum baseline security requirements                                          | Maps to FIPS 200. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the **Security Control Assessor (SCA)** with the **Authorizing Official (AO)**. The SCA *evaluates* controls and produces the SAR; the AO *accepts residual risk* and signs the ATO. They are **separate, independent roles** to ensure objectivity.

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The RMF is mandated by **U.S. Federal Law (FISMA — Federal Information Security Modernization Act)** for all federal agencies and is widely adopted by:
- **Defense contractors** (via CMMC — Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification).
- **Healthcare** (HIPAA-aligned implementations).
- **Critical infrastructure** (energy, finance, transportation).
- **Cloud service providers** seeking **FedRAMP** authorization.
- **International organizations** adapting the framework for ISO 27001 alignment.

In engineering practice, the RMF ensures that **trustworthiness** (the core theme of this course) is **engineered into** the system from inception, not bolted on as an afterthought.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Decision Logic

### 3.1 Detailed Decision Logic of the RMF Cycle

Below is the complete, exhaustive analytical breakdown of the RMF decision flow — the "how" behind the "what."

---

#### **Phase 1: Prepare (Step 0) — Enterprise & System Preparation**

**Step 0.1 — Identify the Risk Management Roles**
The organization must explicitly assign and document:
- **Authorizing Official (AO)** — accountable for risk acceptance.
- **System Owner** — responsible for system development and operation.
- **Information Owner/Steward** — custodian of data.
- **Security Officer (CISO / ISSM)** — sets security policy.
- **Privacy Officer** — ensures privacy compliance (especially for PII).
- **SCA** — independent control evaluator.
- **System Administrator** — implements and maintains technical controls.

**Step 0.2 — Establish the Risk Tolerance**
- A formal, board-approved **Risk Tolerance Statement** is required.
- Example: *"The organization tolerates a 1% annual probability of a confidentiality breach involving regulated PII."*

**Step 0.3 — Perform a System-Level Risk Assessment (Pre-Categorization)**
- Identify preliminary threats, vulnerabilities, and impacts.
- This **informs** the categorization in Step 1 — it is *not* a substitute for it.

**Step 0.4 — Identify Common Controls**
- Many controls are inherited (e.g., the organization's perimeter firewall, enterprise IAM, physical security at the data center).
- These are **documented once** at the organization or program level and reused.
- Reduces duplication of effort and cost.

---

#### **Phase 2: Categorize (Step 1) — FIPS 199 Categorization**

**Step 1.1 — Identify System Boundaries**
Define what is **inside** the system (in-scope) and what is **outside** (e.g., a shared cloud service that is FedRAMP-authorized). Document in the **System Identification Document**.

**Step 1.2 — Apply FIPS 199 Impact Levels**
For each security objective (C, I, A), determine the worst-case impact if the objective fails:

$$
\text{Impact}_{C}, \text{Impact}_{I}, \text{Impact}_{A} \in \{\text{LOW}, \text{MODERATE}, \text{HIGH}\}
$$

**Step 1.3 — Apply the High-Water-Mark Rule**
The overall system categorization is the **highest** impact level of any individual objective:

$$
\text{Overall Category} = \max(\text{Impact}_{C}, \text{Impact}_{I}, \text{Impact}_{A})
$$

**Worked Example:**
A cloud-based **electronic health record (EHR)** system:
- **Confidentiality:** Patient health data exposure → significant harm → **HIGH**
- **Integrity:** Wrong medication order due to data corruption → severe harm → **HIGH**
- **Availability:** Outage during surgery → life-threatening → **HIGH**
- → **Overall System: HIGH (H, H, H)**

---

#### **Phase 3: Select (Step 2) — Control Selection Logic**

**Step 2.1 — Apply Baseline Controls (per FIPS 200)**
- FIPS 200 maps each impact level to a **baseline** of 17 security control families.
- These baselines are the **minimum** mandatory controls.

**Step 2.2 — Tailor the Baseline**
Tailoring decisions (justified and documented):
- **Compensating Controls:** Substitute one control with an equivalent alternative.
- **Scoping Decisions:** Exclude inapplicable controls (e.g., wireless controls for an air-gapped system).
- **Parameterization:** Define specific values (e.g., password length = 14 characters).
- **Removal/Addition:** Based on mission need, threat environment, or cost-benefit.

**Step 2.3 — Supplement the Controls**
- Add controls from the **broader SP 800-53 catalog** to address **unique risks** not covered by the baseline.
- Common sources: industry regulations, threat intelligence, prior incidents.

**Step 2.4 — Document the Control Set**
The output is a **tailored control set** — the basis for the **System Security Plan (SSP)**.

---

#### **Phase 4: Implement (Step 3) — Operational Deployment**

**Step 3.1 — Deploy Technical, Operational, and Management Controls**
- **Technical:** Firewalls, encryption, access control lists, intrusion detection.
- **Operational:** Patch management, backups, incident response procedures, awareness training.
- **Management:** Policies, risk assessments, security planning documentation.

**Step 3.2 — Develop the System Security Plan (SSP)**
The SSP describes:
- System environment, architecture, and boundaries.
- Implementation status of each control.
- Dependencies and common controls inherited.
- Roles and responsibilities.

**Step 3.3 — Establish Configuration Baselines**
Document approved configurations for OS, applications, network devices.

---

#### **Phase 5: Assess (Step 4) — Independent Verification**

**Step 4.1 — Develop the Security Assessment Plan (SAP)**
- Defines **what** will be assessed, **how**, and **when**.
- Identifies assessment methods (Examine / Interview / Test).
- Maps to selected controls.

**Step 4.2 — Execute the Assessment**
- The **SCA** (independent from the System Owner) executes the plan.
- Findings are categorized:
  - **Satisfied** — control is effective.
  - **Other than Satisfied** — control has weaknesses.

**Step 4.3 — Produce the Security Assessment Report (SAR)**
- Records assessment methodology, results, residual findings, and recommendations.
- This is the **primary input** to the AO's authorization decision.

---

#### **Phase 6: Authorize (Step 5) — Risk Acceptance Decision**

**Step 5.1 — Assemble the Authorization Package**
- **System Security Plan (SSP)**
- **Security Assessment Report (SAR)**
- **Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M)** — a corrective action plan for weaknesses.

**Step 5.2 — AO's Decision Logic**

$$
\text{Decision}_{AO} =
\begin{cases}
\text{ATO} & \text{if residual risk} \leq \text{risk tolerance} \\
\text{IATO} & \text{if risk is acceptable for a defined time period pending remediation} \\
\text{DATO} & \text{if risk exceeds tolerance} \\
\text{Common Control Auth.} & \text{if relying on inherited controls}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5.3 — Document the Authorization Decision**
The AO issues a signed **Authorization Decision Letter**, which is:
- Time-bound (typically **3 years** for federal systems).
- Conditional on ongoing monitoring.
- Revoked if conditions change unfavorably.

---

#### **Phase 7: Monitor (Step 6) — Continuous Vigilance**

**Step 6.1 — Execute Continuous Monitoring (ISCM)**
- **Vulnerability Scanning:** Automated tools (e.g., Nessus, Qualys) run on a defined schedule.
- **Configuration Management:** Real-time detection of unauthorized changes.
- **Patch Monitoring:** Track patching compliance and effectiveness.
- **Log Aggregation & Analysis:** SIEM systems (e.g., Splunk, Sentinel) for anomaly detection.
- **Threat Intelligence Integration:** Ingest feeds (e.g., CISA, MITRE ATT&CK) to update threat models.

**Step 6.2 — Trigger Re-Authorization Events**
Significant changes requiring re-authorization:
- Major system architecture changes.
- Changes in impact level (e.g., introduction of PII).
- New threat vectors discovered.
- Periodic schedule (typically **every 3 years** for full re-authorization).

**Step 6.3 — Maintain Documentation Currency**
- SSP, SAR, and POA&M are **living documents**, updated continuously.
- The POA&M must track every open weakness with owner, severity, and target completion date.

---

### 3.2 The Complete RMF Cycle — Equation of Continuity

The RMF is **not a linear waterfall**; it is a **closed feedback loop**:

$$
\text{Prepare} \to \text{Categorize} \to \text{Select} \to \text{Implement} \to \text{Assess} \to \text{Authorize} \to \text{Monitor} \to \text{Prepare (re-enter)}
$$

Each step's output becomes the next step's input, and the **Monitor** step continuously feeds back into **Prepare**, creating an **adaptive, learning security lifecycle**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The RMF Seven-Step Lifecycle Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete RMF cycle, including the **Prepare** step and the continuous feedback loop back to organizational preparation. All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed to comply with Mermaid parser safety rules.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start0[System Conception and Initiation] --> step0A
    step0A["Step 0 PREPARE - Establish Risk Context and Roles"] --> step1A
    step1A["Step 1 CATEGORIZE - Apply FIPS 199 Impact Levels"] --> step2A
    step2A["Step 2 SELECT - Choose Controls from NIST 800-53"] --> step3A
    step3A["Step 3 IMPLEMENT - Deploy Controls and Draft SSP"] --> step4A
    step4A["Step 4 ASSESS - Independent Control Evaluation by SCA"] --> step5A
    step5A["Step 5 AUTHORIZE - AO Risk Acceptance and ATO Signing"] --> step6A
    step6A["Step 6 MONITOR - Continuous ISCM and POAM Updates"] --> decision1
    decision1{Significant Change or Risk Shift Detected}:::decision -->|Yes| step0A
    decision1 -->|No| monitor2
    monitor2[Continue Operational Monitoring] --> decision1
    step5B{Authorization Decision}:::decision -->|Acceptable Risk| ATO[ATO Authorization to Operate]:::success
    step5B -->|Time Limited Pending Fix| IATO[IATO Interim Authorization]:::warning
    step5B -->|Unacceptable Risk| DATO[DATO Denial of Authorization]:::danger
    classDef decision fill:#FFE08A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef success fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    classDef warning fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef danger fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 RMF Tiers and SDLC Alignment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph tier1["TIER 1 ORGANIZATION LEVEL - Governance and Strategy"]
        t1a[Enterprise Risk Management Strategy]
        t1b[Risk Tolerance and Appetite]
        t1c[Common Control Identification]
    end
    subgraph tier2["TIER 2 MISSION AND BUSINESS PROCESS LEVEL"]
        t2a[Program Risk Management]
        t2b[Shared Services and Common Controls]
        t2c[Privacy and Security Architectures]
    end
    subgraph tier3["TIER 3 SYSTEM LEVEL - RMF Seven Steps"]
        s3a["Prepare and Categorize"]
        s3b["Select and Implement"]
        s3c["Assess and Authorize"]
        s3d["Monitor Continuously"]
    end
    subgraph sdlc["SYSTEM DEVELOPMENT LIFE CYCLE"]
        p1[Initiation] --> p2[Planning]
        p2 --> p3[Design and Development]
        p3 --> p4[Testing and Integration]
        p4 --> p5[Deployment]
        p5 --> p6[Operations and Maintenance]
        p6 --> p7[Disposal]
    end
    tier1 --> tier2
    tier2 --> tier3
    tier3 -.executes in parallel with.-> sdlc
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of RMF Inputs and Outputs

> [!TIP]
> This block diagram maps the **information artifacts (inputs/outputs)** between each RMF step, illustrating the documentary trail that auditors and AOs rely on.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    prep["PREPARE"] --> p1[System Identification]
    prep --> p2[Risk Assessment Preliminary]
    prep --> p3[Common Control Inventory]
    cat["CATEGORIZE"] --> c1[FIPS 199 Category]
    cat --> c2[System Boundary Description]
    sel["SELECT"] --> s1[Tailored Control Set]
    sel --> s2[Control Allocation Matrix]
    imp["IMPLEMENT"] --> i1[System Security Plan SSP]
    imp --> i2[Configuration Baselines]
    ass["ASSESS"] --> a1[Security Assessment Report SAR]
    ass --> a2[Plan of Action and Milestones POAM]
    auth["AUTHORIZE"] --> au1[Authorization Decision Letter]
    auth --> au2[Risk Determination Record]
    mon["MONITOR"] --> m1[Continuous Monitoring Reports]
    mon --> m2[Updated POAM and SSP]
    p1 --> cat
    c1 --> sel
    s1 --> imp
    i1 --> ass
    a1 --> auth
    au1 --> mon
    m2 -.feedback.-> prep
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part A questions test **direct, factual recall** aligned to Bloom's **Remember / Understand** levels. Answers should be concise (3–5 sentences).

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the Risk Management Framework (RMF). List its seven steps in the correct order of execution.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Risk Management Framework (RMF)** is a structured, risk-based methodology developed by **NIST (SP 800-37, Rev. 2)** to manage security and privacy risks across the system development life cycle. It provides a repeatable process for categorizing systems, selecting and implementing controls, assessing their effectiveness, authorizing system operation, and continuously monitoring security posture.

The seven steps in order are:
1. **Prepare**
2. **Categorize**
3. **Select**
4. **Implement**
5. **Assess**
6. **Authorize**
7. **Monitor**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**What is the purpose of the "Prepare" step introduced in RMF Revision 2? List three key activities performed during this step.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Prepare** step was added in **NIST SP 800-37 Revision 2** to embed security and privacy risk management into the **enterprise-wide governance and SDLC** *before* any system-level work begins. It ensures strategic alignment, resource availability, and consistent application of risk management practices.

Three key activities:
1. **Identify and assign risk management roles** (AO, System Owner, CISO, SCA, Privacy Officer).
2. **Establish a documented risk tolerance** for the organization.
3. **Identify common controls** that can be inherited by systems (e.g., enterprise-wide firewalls, IAM).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions feature **internal choice** and sub-parts (typically a 7-mark and a 7-mark sub-question). Answers must demonstrate **Apply / Analyze** level cognitive depth. Length: **2–3 substantial paragraphs per sub-part** plus diagrams.

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model – Module 2 Reference]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven steps of the NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF)**. For each step, clearly state the **purpose** and the **key output artifact** produced. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss how the RMF is **integrated with the System Development Life Cycle (SDLC)**. Explain why continuous monitoring is treated as an ongoing, life-long activity rather than a one-time process. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

| **Step**                | **Purpose**                                                                                                                                  | **Key Output Artifact**                       |
| ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------- |
| **Prepare**             | Establish organizational context, risk tolerance, roles, and identify common controls. Embeds RMF into SDLC.                                | Risk Management Strategy; System Identification |
| **Categorize**          | Assign **FIPS 199** impact levels (LOW/MODERATE/HIGH) for Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. Uses the high-water-mark principle.      | FIPS 199 Categorization Document              |
| **Select**              | Choose and tailor controls from **NIST SP 800-53** baseline based on impact level. Apply tailoring and supplementation.                       | Tailored Control Set; Control Allocation      |
| **Implement**           | Deploy selected controls (technical, operational, management) within the system and its environment.                                          | **System Security Plan (SSP)**                |
| **Assess**              | Independent evaluation of control effectiveness by the **Security Control Assessor (SCA)** using Examine/Interview/Test methods.                | **Security Assessment Report (SAR)**           |
| **Authorize**           | The **Authorizing Official (AO)** reviews the SSP, SAR, and POA&M and formally accepts residual risk by issuing an ATO, IATO, or DATO.        | **Authorization Decision Letter (ATO/IATO/DATO)** |
| **Monitor**             | Establish and execute an **Information Security Continuous Monitoring (ISCM)** strategy per NIST SP 800-137 to detect changes and emerging threats. | Continuous Monitoring Reports; Updated POA&M  |

These artifacts form a **traceable, auditable chain of evidence** that is examined by assessors and regulators.

**Model Solution (b):**

The RMF is designed to be **executed in parallel with the SDLC**, not as a separate, sequential process. During **Initiation and Planning**, the Prepare and Categorize steps are performed. During **Requirements Analysis and Design**, the Select step refines the control set. **Implementation** maps to the development phase. **Testing** maps to the Assess step. **Deployment** is gated by the Authorize step. **Operations and Maintenance** is the long-running Monitor phase, and **Disposal** is finalized with secure media sanitization and a final risk assessment.

**Continuous monitoring is treated as a life-long activity** because threats, vulnerabilities, and operational environments evolve continuously. Controls that were effective at the time of authorization may degrade due to software updates, configuration drift, new attack vectors, or changes in the threat landscape. The **ISCM strategy** (per **NIST SP 800-137**) ensures that risk is **re-evaluated continuously**, not just at the time of initial authorization. This shift from **static, point-in-time authorization** to **ongoing authorization** is a defining improvement of RMF Rev. 2 and is essential for maintaining **trustworthiness** in modern, dynamic systems.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model – Module 2 Reference]**

**(a)** Explain the **FIPS 199 Categorization** process with a suitable example. How does the **high-water-mark principle** work? (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **role of the Authorizing Official (AO)** and the **Security Control Assessor (SCA)** in the RMF. Why are these roles kept **independent** of each other and of the System Owner? (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a):**

**FIPS 199 Categorization** is the process of assigning an impact level (**LOW, MODERATE, or HIGH**) to an information system for each of the three security objectives: **Confidentiality (C)**, **Integrity (I)**, and **Availability (A)**. The impact level reflects the **worst-case, reasonably foreseeable adverse effect** of a loss of that objective on organizational operations, assets, individuals, or the nation.

**The High-Water-Mark Principle:** The overall system security category is equal to the **highest impact level** assigned to any individual security objective. This ensures that the system is protected at a level proportionate to its most critical weakness — it does not "average out" a critical confidentiality risk with a low availability risk.

**Worked Example — University Online Examination System:**
- **Confidentiality (C):** Exposure of student answer scripts could cause **moderate** harm (privacy violation, academic fraud) → **MODERATE**
- **Integrity (I):** Tampering with marks could result in **high** harm (academic injustice, regulatory action) → **HIGH**
- **Availability (A):** Outage during exam window causes **moderate** disruption → **MODERATE**
- → **Overall System Categorization: HIGH** (driven by the Integrity rating)

This HIGH categorization mandates the most stringent control baseline (e.g., strong authentication, cryptographic integrity checks, continuous monitoring).

**Model Solution (b):**

The **Authorizing Official (AO)** is a **senior organizational executive** with the **authority and accountability** to formally accept the residual risk of operating an information system. The AO reviews the complete authorization package (SSP, SAR, POA&M) and issues one of the following decisions: **ATO** (Authorization to Operate), **IATO** (Interim Authorization), **DATO** (Denial of Authorization), or a Common Control Authorization. The AO is **personally accountable** for this risk-acceptance decision and signs the formal authorization letter.

The **Security Control Assessor (SCA)** is a **technically qualified, independent individual or team** responsible for objectively evaluating the implementation and effectiveness of security and privacy controls. The SCA develops the Security Assessment Plan (SAP), executes the assessment using Examine, Interview, and Test methods, and produces the **Security Assessment Report (SAR)**. The SAR documents findings, deviations, residual risks, and recommended corrective actions.

**Independence Rationale:**
The separation of the AO, SCA, and System Owner is a **foundational governance control** rooted in **segregation of duties** principles. Independence:
1. **Prevents conflicts of interest** — the System Owner cannot self-authorize a system they developed.
2. **Ensures objective assessment** — the SCA has no vested interest in declaring controls "satisfied" when they are not.
3. **Enables informed, accountable risk decisions** — the AO receives a candid, unbiased SAR to base the ATO decision upon.
4. **Provides credible, auditable evidence** — third-party independence is required for the ATO to be accepted by regulators, oversight bodies (e.g., GAO, OIG), and external partners.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the SCA with the AO:** Students often attribute the ATO signing to the SCA. **The SCA *assesses*; the AO *authorizes*.** This is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.
> 2. **Listing only 6 RMF steps:** Forgetting the **Prepare** step (added in Rev. 2) is a common error. Always list **7 steps** unless the question specifically cites Rev. 1.
> 3. **Misapplying the High-Water-Mark Rule:** A system with C=LOW, I=LOW, A=HIGH is **categorized as HIGH**, not as "average" or "mixed." Always take the **maximum**.
> 4. **Confusing FIPS 199 with FIPS 200:** FIPS 199 = **categorization**; FIPS 200 = **minimum security requirements**. They are different documents with different purposes.
> 5. **Treating Monitor as a one-time activity:** The Monitor step is **continuous**, not a final audit. Loss of 2 marks if described as a one-time event.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Read this in the final 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **RMF =** NIST's 7-step, risk-based, life-cycle approach to security & privacy risk management (codified in **SP 800-37 Rev. 2**).
- ✅ **The 7 Steps (in order):** **Prepare → Categorize → Select → Implement → Assess → Authorize → Monitor.** Remember "**P-C-S-I-A-A-M**".
- ✅ **Prepare** was added in **Rev. 2** to embed RMF into the SDLC from inception.
- ✅ **Categorize** uses **FIPS 199** with **LOW/MODERATE/HIGH** levels for **C, I, A**; overall = **maximum** (high-water-mark).
- ✅ **Select** uses **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** as the control catalog; baseline is determined by impact level; controls are then **tailored and supplemented**.
- ✅ **Implement** produces the **System Security Plan (SSP)**.
- ✅ **Assess** is done by the **SCA** (independent role) using Examine/Interview/Test; produces the **SAR**.
- ✅ **Authorize** is done by the **AO** (senior official) and produces an **ATO, IATO, DATO, or Common Control Authorization**.
- ✅ **Monitor** is **continuous** and governed by **NIST SP 800-137 (ISCM)**; vulnerability scans typically **monthly**, assessments at least **annually**.
- ✅ **Two-Tier (actually Three-Tier) Hierarchy:** Tier 1 (Organization) → Tier 2 (Mission/Business) → Tier 3 (System).
- ✅ **RMF runs in parallel with the SDLC**, not as a separate waterfall.
- ✅ **Core Risk Equation:** $R = P \times I$ (Probability × Impact).
- ✅ **Key Independent Roles:** **System Owner** (builds/operates), **SCA** (assesses), **AO** (accepts risk). These three must be **segregated**.
- ✅ **ATO is time-bound** (typically 3 years) and requires ongoing POA&M management and re-authorization on significant change.
- ✅ **Common Controls** are inherited from the organization or shared services, reducing duplication of effort.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Prepare before you Categorize. Tailor after you Select. Document while you Implement. Assess Independently. Authorize Accountably. Monitor Continuously."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Management Framework (RMF) Activities, Tasks, and Outcomes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Risk Management Framework (RMF) Activities, Tasks, and Outcomes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Aligned Definition

The **Risk Management Framework (RMF)** is the unified, organization-wide, and continuous process established by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** under **Special Publication 800-37, Revision 2**, designed to integrate information security, privacy, and cyber supply chain risk management activities into the system development life cycle (SDLC). It provides a structured, seven-step lifecycle that translates executive-level risk decisions into operational, technical, and procedural controls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PECCT754):** The RMF links *risk determination* (organization/mission level) to *risk response* (system level) through seven sequential activities. Each activity decomposes into **Tasks** (the actions performed) and **Outcomes** (the artifacts/proof produced). The framework is mandatory for U.S. federal information systems under **FISMA** and is the de-facto global benchmark.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Inspection" View

Imagine a hospital that wants to open a new ICU wing.

| Hospital Analogy | RMF Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Deciding the type of ICU (cardiac, trauma, pediatric) — its *importance* | **Categorize** the system |
| Choosing what fire alarms, oxygen lines, and locks to install | **Select** controls |
| Actually installing the alarms and locks | **Implement** controls |
| Inspection by a fire marshal to verify they work | **Assess** controls |
| Hospital board signing the occupancy permit | **Authorize** the system |
| Annual safety audits after opening | **Monitor** controls |
| Pre-project budgeting, assigning teams, defining rules | **Prepare** (Step 0/Step 1 in Rev. 2) |

Just as a hospital does not declare itself "safe" once and forget it, the RMF insists on a **continuous monitoring** loop — security is a living process, not a checkbox.

### 1.3 Key Standard Identifiers

- **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2** — defines the seven RMF steps.
- **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** — catalog of security & privacy controls.
- **NIST SP 800-53A** — assessment procedures for those controls.
- **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** — risk assessment methodology.
- **FIPS 199** — system categorization standard.
- **FIPS 200** — minimum security requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant in Framework Use:** There are exactly **7 activities**, **approximately 85+ tasks** (across roles), and a defined set of **outcomes** per activity in NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2. RMF does not define a numeric "risk score" formula; risk is expressed as a **categorical determination** (Low / Moderate / High) combined with a **risk response decision** (Authorize, Mitigate, Transfer, Avoid, Accept).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven RMF Activities — Logical Decomposition

The RMF operates as a **prepare-then-execute-then-monitor** pipeline. Each activity produces *outcomes* (deliverables) that become *inputs* to the next activity.

#### Step 0 / Step 1 — **Prepare**
- **Why:** Establishes *organizational context* and *risk management strategy* before any system is touched. Without this, the rest of the framework is unanchored.
- **How it works:**
  1. Identify key risk management roles (Authorizing Official, System Owner, ISSO, Privacy Officer).
  2. Establish a risk tolerance and strategy at the **organization level** and **mission/business process level**.
  3. Publish a common control provider identification list.
  4. Perform system-level preparation: identify stakeholders, assets, types of information processed.
- **Key Terms:** **Organization-Level Risk Management Strategy**, **System-Level Risk Management Strategy**, **Common Controls**, **System Stakeholders**.

#### Step 2 — **Categorize**
- **Why:** Drives *everything downstream* — the rigor of controls selected, the depth of assessment, and the authorization level required.
- **How it works:** Use **FIPS 199** to assign impact values (**Low, Moderate, High**) across the three security objectives:
  - **C** — Confidentiality
  - **I** — Integrity
  - **A** — Availability
- The overall system categorization is the **high-water mark** (the maximum of C, I, A).

> [!TIP]
> **High-Water Mark Rule:** If Confidentiality = Moderate, Integrity = High, Availability = Low, the system is categorized as **HIGH**. This is the most-asked conceptual point in KTU viva.

#### Step 3 — **Select**
- **Why:** Translates the categorization into a tailored set of safeguards.
- **How it works:** Use the **NIST SP 800-53 control catalog**, apply **baselines** (Low / Moderate / High), then **tailor** by applying:
  - **Scoping** (applicability to system components)
  - **Compensating controls** (when a baseline control cannot be met as stated)
  - **Parameterization** (filling in assignment values)
  - **Supplementing** (adding organization-specific controls)
- Document the result in the **System Security Plan (SSP)**.

#### Step 4 — **Implement**
- **Why:** The selected controls must be realized in hardware, software, policies, and procedures.
- **How it works:** Deploy controls per the SSP; capture implementation details (who, what, when, how). Output feeds the security and privacy control assessment.

#### Step 5 — **Assess**
- **Why:** Independently verifies that the controls are *correct, effective, and producing the intended outcome*.
- **How it works:** A **Security Control Assessor (SCA)** uses **NIST SP 800-53A** assessment procedures to test, examine, and interview. Findings are documented in a **Security Assessment Report (SAR)** with a recommendation to the Authorizing Official.

#### Step 6 — **Authorize**
- **Why:** A senior accountable official must accept the residual risk in writing.
- **How it works:** The **Authorizing Official (AO)** reviews the SAR, SSP, and any Plan of Action & Milestones (POA&M), then issues an **Authorization to Operate (ATO)**, **Denial**, or **Interim Authorization**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Authorization Decisions:** ATO, Denial of Authorization, Interim Authorization to Operate (IATO), Authorization with Conditions. The AO is *not* the same person as the System Owner — this is **separation of duties**.

#### Step 7 — **Monitor**
- **Why:** Threats, vulnerabilities, and the operational environment evolve; controls degrade over time.
- **How it works:** Continuous monitoring strategy, ongoing control assessments, change management, incident response, and re-authorization triggers. Outputs feed back into the **Prepare** activity, closing the loop.

### 2.2 Roles and Responsibilities (KTU High-Yield)

| Role | Primary Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Authorizing Official (AO)** | Accepts risk; signs ATO |
| **System Owner** | Owns the system; develops SSP |
| **Information System Security Officer (ISSO)** | Day-to-day security operations |
| **Security Control Assessor (SCA)** | Independently assesses controls |
| **Common Control Provider** | Implements organization-wide controls (e.g., network firewall) |
| **Privacy Officer / Privacy Engineer** | Manages privacy risks under NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2 |

### 2.3 Tasks vs. Outcomes — The Output of Each Activity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tasks** = *what is done*; **Outcomes** = *what is produced*. Examiners frequently ask for the **naming of key documents** (e.g., "What document is produced in the Assess step?" — Answer: **Security Assessment Report / SAR**).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet Table

| Activity | Primary Input(s) | Tasks (Key Actions) | Outcome / Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prepare** | Organizational strategy; system context | Assign roles, define risk tolerance, identify common controls, perform system prep | **System-Level Risk Management Strategy**; **System Registration** |
| **Categorize** | System info types, FIPS 199 | Categorize system & information; describe the system | **System Categorization** (Low/Moderate/High); **System Description** |
| **Select** | Categorization; control baselines | Apply baseline; tailor; supplement; document | **System Security Plan (SSP)**; **Security & Privacy Requirements** |
| **Implement** | SSP | Deploy controls; record implementation details | **Updated SSP** (with implementation statements) |
| **Assess** | Implemented controls | Test, examine, interview per 800-53A | **Security Assessment Report (SAR)**; **POA&M (initial)** |
| **Authorize** | SAR, SSP, POA&M | AO reviews; accepts residual risk | **Authorization Decision (ATO/ATO w/Conditions/Denial)** |
| **Monitor** | Authorization; change data | Continuous monitoring; assess changes; report | **Continuous Monitoring Strategy/Reports; Updated POA&M; Re-authorization decisions** |

> [!TIP]
> For exam recall, memorize the **three "A" documents**: **SSP** (Select), **SAR** (Assess), **ATO** (Authorize) — these are the most-tested artifacts.

### 2.5 Categorical Risk Determination (FIPS 199)

$$
\text{Security Category} = \{ (C_{impact}, I_{impact}, A_{impact}) \}
$$

Where each $X_{impact} \in \{\text{Low}, \text{Moderate}, \text{High}\}$.

$$
\text{System Security Category} = \max_{X \in \{C,I,A\}} (X_{impact})
$$

The "max" is taken using the order $\text{Low} < \text{Moderate} < \text{High}$ (i.e., the **high-water mark**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** RMF and FIPS 199 drive procurement decisions in U.S. federal agencies. Cloud providers (AWS GovCloud, Azure Government) operate under RMF-authorized environments specifically because their customers must meet these standards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Mapping Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Categorizing a Hospital EHR System

We will exhaustively walk through a real-world RMF categorization so the "high-water mark" computation is unambiguous.

**Scenario:** A hospital is deploying an Electronic Health Record (EHR) system. The information includes patient diagnoses, prescriptions, lab results, and appointment data. The system is internet-facing for patient portal access.

#### Step 1 — Identify Information Types

Using **NIST SP 800-60 Vol. II**, *Medical Records* is identified as the dominant information type. We focus on the **three security objectives**.

#### Step 2 — Apply FIPS 199 Logic

| Objective | Question Asked | Determination | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Could unauthorized disclosure cause serious harm? | **High** | Patient diagnoses are protected health information (PHI); disclosure violates HIPAA and causes severe personal harm. |
| **Integrity** | Could unauthorized modification cause serious harm? | **High** | Modifying prescriptions or allergies could directly cause patient death. |
| **Availability** | Could disruption cause serious harm? | **Moderate** | A multi-hour outage degrades care but rarely causes immediate severe harm; hospitals maintain paper-fallback procedures. |

#### Step 3 — High-Water Mark Computation

$$
\text{SC} = \{ (C = \text{High},\ I = \text{High},\ A = \text{Moderate}) \}
$$

$$
\text{System Security Categorization} = \max(\text{High},\ \text{High},\ \text{Moderate}) = \text{HIGH}
$$

Therefore, the **High baseline** of **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** must be used as the starting point in the **Select** activity.

#### Step 4 — Downstream Consequences

- **Select step:** The *High* baseline of 800-53 contains ~350+ controls (vs. ~100 for *Low*).
- **Assess step:** Assessment depth, independence, and frequency all increase.
- **Authorize step:** Typically requires a **Senior Agency Information Security Officer (SAISO)** or **Agency Head**-level AO sign-off, not a mid-level AO.

### 3.2 Mapping Matrix: Tasks to Outcomes (RMF Rev. 2 — Selected Highlights)

| Activity | Sample Task | Sample Outcome | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prepare (Org) | Define risk tolerance | Risk Management Strategy document | Senior Leader |
| Prepare (System) | Conduct system registration | System registered in inventory | System Owner |
| Categorize | Categorize system per FIPS 199 | Approved categorization memo | System Owner, AO |
| Select | Tailor control baseline | Approved SSP | System Owner |
| Implement | Deploy control per SSP | Implementation evidence | System Owner / ISSO |
| Assess | Execute assessment procedures | SAR with findings | SCA |
| Authorize | Determine residual risk | ATO / Denial | AO |
| Monitor | Report control status | Monthly/quarterly metrics | ISSO |

### 3.3 Continuous Feedback Loop

After the **Monitor** step, if significant changes occur (e.g., new threat, major architectural change, new privacy law), the system re-enters **Categorize → Select → Implement → Assess → Authorize** while still operating — this is **ongoing authorization**.

$$
\text{Trigger Re-Authorization} \iff \text{Significant Change Detected in Monitor}
$$

$$
\text{Significant Change} = \text{Net Risk} > \text{AO-Accepted Residual Risk}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This continuous cycle is what makes RMF a *framework* rather than a *checklist* — risk is **re-evaluated**, not assessed once and forgotten.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid — RMF Seven-Step Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    prep0[Prepare Organization Level] --> prep1[Prepare System Level]
    prep1 --> cat[Categorize]
    cat --> sel[Select]
    sel --> impl[Implement]
    impl --> assess[Assess]
    assess --> auth[Authorize]
    auth --> mon[Monitor]
    mon --> prep0
    assess -.->|Findings| poam[POA and M]
    poam --> auth
    mon -->|Significant Change| cat
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Inputs, Tasks, and Outcomes per Activity (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph prepA[Prepare]
        pIn[Inputs: Org Strategy, Risk Tolerance] --> pTask[Tasks: Assign Roles, Identify Common Controls, Register System]
        pTask --> pOut[Outcome: System Level Risk Strategy, Registration]
    end
    subgraph catA[Categorize]
        cIn[Inputs: System Description, FIPS 199] --> cTask[Task: Assign C I A Impact Values]
        cTask --> cOut[Outcome: System Security Categorization]
    end
    subgraph selA[Select]
        sIn[Inputs: Categorization, Baselines] --> sTask[Task: Tailor and Supplement Controls]
        sTask --> sOut[Outcome: System Security Plan SSP]
    end
    subgraph impA[Implement]
        iIn[Inputs: SSP] --> iTask[Task: Deploy Controls]
        iTask --> iOut[Outcome: Implemented Controls Evidence]
    end
    subgraph assA[Assess]
        aIn[Inputs: Implemented Controls] --> aTask[Task: Test Examine Interview per 800-53A]
        aTask --> aOut[Outcome: Security Assessment Report SAR]
    end
    subgraph autA[Authorize]
        zIn[Inputs: SSP, SAR, POA and M] --> zTask[Task: AO Risk Determination]
        zTask --> zOut[Outcome: Authorization Decision ATO]
    end
    subgraph monA[Monitor]
        mIn[Inputs: ATO, Change Data] --> mTask[Task: Continuous Monitoring and Change Control]
        mTask --> mOut[Outcome: Ongoing Authorization Reports]
    end
    pOut --> cIn
    cOut --> sIn
    sOut --> iIn
    iOut --> aIn
    aOut --> zIn
    zOut --> mIn
    mOut -.->|Re Categorize| cIn
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Role-to-Activity Responsibility Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AO[Authorizing Official] --> authR[Authorize]
    SO[System Owner] --> catR[Categorize]
    SO --> selR[Select]
    SO --> implR[Implement]
    ISSO[ISSO] --> implR
    ISSO --> monR[Monitor]
    SCA[Security Control Assessor] --> assR[Assess]
    CCP[Common Control Provider] --> prepR[Prepare]
    PO[Privacy Officer] --> catR
    PO --> selR
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the Risk Management Framework. Name the standard that defines it and list its seven activities in order.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **RMF** is a unified, organization-wide, continuous process developed by **NIST** to integrate information security and risk management into the SDLC.
- It is defined in **NIST SP 800-37 Revision 2**.
- The **seven activities** in order are:
  1. **Prepare** (organization & system)
  2. **Categorize**
  3. **Select**
  4. **Implement**
  5. **Assess**
  6. **Authorize**
  7. **Monitor**

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for standard, 1 mark for the 7 activities.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected]**
*What is the "high-water mark" principle used in FIPS 199 system categorization? Illustrate with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **high-water mark** principle states that the overall system security category equals the **highest impact level** assigned to any of the three security objectives (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability).
- **Example:** A system is rated as Confidentiality = Moderate, Integrity = High, Availability = Low. By the high-water mark rule, the system is categorized as **HIGH** (the maximum of the three).
- This ensures the system is protected to the level of its most sensitive security objective.

*(1 mark principle, 1 mark example, 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) **confusing SSP with SAR** (SSP = Select step; SAR = Assess step), (2) **forgetting the "Prepare" step** when listing RMF activities, and (3) **mixing up roles** (e.g., the System Owner *does not* authorize — only the AO does). Always use the **exact terms** in **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2**; KTU examiners reward precise terminology.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]

**(a)** Explain the seven activities of the NIST Risk Management Framework as defined in **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2**. For each activity, state its **primary task(s)** and **outcome document(s)**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A government agency is deploying a tax-processing system that handles citizens' PAN, Aadhaar-like identifiers, and bank account details. Apply **FIPS 199** to determine the system's security categorization using the high-water mark principle. Justify each of the C, I, A values and show the categorization computation explicitly. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The **Risk Management Framework** in **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2** consists of the following seven activities:

1. **Prepare**
   - *Task:* Identify risk management roles, define organization- and system-level risk strategies, identify common controls, register the system.
   - *Outcome:* **System-Level Risk Management Strategy**, **System Registration** in the organizational inventory.

2. **Categorize**
   - *Task:* Apply **FIPS 199** to assign impact values to C, I, A; document the system description.
   - *Outcome:* **System Security Categorization** (Low / Moderate / High) and **System Description**.

3. **Select**
   - *Task:* Choose a baseline (Low / Moderate / High) from **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5**; tailor (scoping, compensating, parameterization); supplement with organization-specific controls.
   - *Outcome:* **System Security Plan (SSP)** containing the tailored control set and allocation (system / hybrid / common).

4. **Implement**
   - *Task:* Deploy controls as specified in the SSP; record implementation details (responsible party, schedule, evidence).
   - *Outcome:* **Updated SSP with implementation statements** and supporting evidence artifacts.

5. **Assess**
   - *Task:* An independent **Security Control Assessor (SCA)** uses **NIST SP 800-53A** assessment procedures (test, examine, interview) to evaluate each control.
   - *Outcome:* **Security Assessment Report (SAR)** with findings (satisfied / other-than-satisfied) and recommendations.

6. **Authorize**
   - *Task:* The **Authorizing Official (AO)** reviews the SAR, SSP, and **POA&M**, evaluates residual risk, and decides.
   - *Outcome:* **Authorization Decision** — ATO, ATO with Conditions, Interim Authorization (IATO), or Denial.

7. **Monitor**
   - *Task:* Execute the **Continuous Monitoring Strategy**; perform ongoing assessments, track changes, respond to incidents, and re-evaluate risk.
   - *Outcome:* **Continuous Monitoring Reports**, **Updated POA&M**, and **Re-authorization** events when triggers occur.

The framework is **iterative** — Monitor feeds back into Prepare, and a *significant change* may re-initiate Categorize.

*(Allocation: 1 mark per activity × 7 = 7 marks. Examiners may split 0.5/0.5 for task and outcome.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify security objectives** (per FIPS 199):

- **Confidentiality (C):** PAN and bank account numbers, if disclosed, could cause serious financial and identity harm. → **High**
- **Integrity (I):** Unauthorized modification of tax records could lead to wrongful assessments, fraud, and significant financial/legal impact on citizens and the state. → **High**
- **Availability (A):** Tax filing windows are time-bounded; an outage longer than typical downtime could cause significant but recoverable harm. → **Moderate**

**Step 2 — Construct the security category tuple:**

$$
SC = \{ C = \text{High},\ I = \text{High},\ A = \text{Moderate} \}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the high-water mark rule:**

$$
\text{System Security Categorization} = \max(\text{High},\ \text{High},\ \text{Moderate}) = \text{High}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The tax-processing system is categorized as **HIGH**. The agency must use the **High baseline** of **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** for control selection, and authorization must be granted by a senior AO.

*(Allocation: 2 marks C/I/A values + 1 mark tuple + 2 marks high-water-mark calculation + 2 marks conclusion/baseline linkage.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Differentiate between the **System Security Plan (SSP)** and the **Security Assessment Report (SAR)**. State the activity in which each is produced, the owner of each document, and the typical structure / sections of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the roles of **Authorizing Official (AO)**, **System Owner (SO)**, **ISSO**, **Security Control Assessor (SCA)**, and **Common Control Provider (CCP)**. State the principle of **separation of duties** and explain how it is enforced in the RMF. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Aspect | System Security Plan (SSP) | Security Assessment Report (SAR) |
|---|---|---|
| **RMF Activity** | Produced in the **Select** step (and updated in Implement) | Produced in the **Assess** step |
| **Owner** | **System Owner** | **Security Control Assessor (SCA)** |
| **Purpose** | Documents *what* controls are selected and *how* they will be implemented | Documents *how well* those controls were implemented and whether they are effective |
| **Audience** | AO, SCA, ISSO, auditors | AO (decision input), System Owner |
| **Typical Sections** | (1) System Identification, (2) System Description/Environment, (3) System Interconnections, (4) System Security Categorization (FIPS 199), (5) Control Allocation, (6) Tailored Controls, (7) Implementation Plan, (8) Continuous Monitoring Plan | (1) Executive Summary, (2) Assessment Scope, (3) Methodology, (4) Assessment Findings (satisfied / other-than-satisfied per control), (5) Risk Determination per finding, (6) Recommendations, (7) Appendices with evidence |

The SSP is a *forward-looking design and planning* document; the SAR is a *backward-looking evidence and findings* document.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for activity, 1 mark for owner, 1 mark for purpose, 1 mark for audience, 3 marks for sections — 7 marks total.)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

| Role | Core Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Authorizing Official (AO)** | Senior accountable executive; **accepts residual risk** by issuing the ATO. Operates *independently* of the system owner. |
| **System Owner (SO)** | Owns the system throughout its lifecycle; develops and maintains the **SSP**; ensures controls are implemented. |
| **ISSO** | Operational security officer; day-to-day implementation of the SSP; reports to the SO; performs continuous monitoring activities. |
| **Security Control Assessor (SCA)** | Independent assessor (organizational or third-party); conducts the **assessment**; produces the **SAR**. **Must not be the SO or ISSO** of the system under assessment. |
| **Common Control Provider (CCP)** | Implements **organization-wide (inherited) controls** such as boundary protection, physical security, or identity management that multiple systems share. |

**Principle of Separation of Duties (SoD):**
- The RMF enforces **SoD** by ensuring that the *implementer* (System Owner + ISSO) is **different from** the *assessor* (SCA), who is in turn **different from** the *risk acceptor* (AO).
- This prevents a single individual from both **committing** a system to security claims and **certifying** those claims as true.
- Formally, the AO cannot be the System Owner of the same system they authorize; and the SCA must be organizationally or contractually independent from the system team.

This mirrors the broader cybersecurity principle that **trust must be distributed**, not concentrated.

*(Allocation: 1 mark per role × 5 = 5 marks; 2 marks for SoD principle and enforcement — 7 marks total.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Risk Management Framework**

- **RMF = NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2.** Know the **seven activities** in order: **Prepare → Categorize → Select → Implement → Assess → Authorize → Monitor**.
- **Prepare** is the *new* step in Rev. 2 — do not omit it in exam answers.
- **FIPS 199** drives **Categorize**; the **high-water mark** of (C, I, A) is the system security category.
- **NIST SP 800-53** drives **Select**; baselines are **Low / Moderate / High**; controls are then **tailored**.
- **Three "A" documents**:
  - **SSP** (System Security Plan) — produced in **Select** by the **System Owner**.
  - **SAR** (Security Assessment Report) — produced in **Assess** by the **SCA**.
  - **ATO** (Authorization to Operate) — produced in **Authorize** by the **AO**.
- **AO ≠ System Owner** — separation of duties is mandatory.
- **Monitor** is a *continuous*, not a one-time, activity; it feeds back into **Prepare**.
- **Risk response** vocabulary: **Accept, Avoid, Mitigate, Transfer, Share**.
- **Continuous authorization** is a modern RMF concept where the system operates under *ongoing* ATO conditions rather than point-in-time re-authorizations.
- **FISMA** compliance for U.S. federal systems requires RMF use; many private-sector organizations adopt it as a best-practice baseline.
- **Common Controls** (inherited from the organization) reduce the per-system control burden — know what they are.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to *"explain RMF,"* always start with: *"RMF is a seven-step, continuous, organization-wide risk management process defined in NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2."* Anchoring the answer to the standard and revision number signals examiner-favored precision and typically earns full credit for the opening statement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of a Risk Management Assessment Using the Risk Management Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Development of a Risk Management Assessment Using the Risk Management Framework # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Risk Management Framework (RMF)** — A structured, multi-disciplinary process (formalized by **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2**) that integrates security, privacy, and cyber supply chain risk management activities into the system development life cycle (SDLC). It provides a **seven-step lifecycle** approach for managing risks to organizational operations, assets, individuals, and other organizations resulting from the operation and use of information systems. In the context of KTU's *Engineering of Trustworthy Secure Systems* course, the **Risk Management Assessment (RMA)** is the practical deliverable produced when an organization executes the RMF on a target system. The assessment produces: - A **System Security Plan (SSP)** documenting controls. - A **Security Assessment Report (SAR)** detailing residual risk. - An **Authority to Operate (ATO)** — the formal acceptance letter. - A continuous **monitoring strategy** for ongoing risk visibility. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Building a House in Monsoon Country** Imagine you are building a house in Kerala during monsoon season. Before pouring the foundation, you ask five questions: 1. **What kind of house do I need?** (Categorize — small cottage vs. high-rise) 2. **What hazards must I plan for?** Floods, cyclones, termites, theft. (Select Controls) 3. **Did I actually install the waterproofing, locks, and pillars?** (Implement) 4. **Will it survive the first storm?** (Assess — stress test) 5. **Should I move in, or fix cracks first?** (Authorize) 6. **Will I re-check every year before next monsoon?** (Monitor) The RMF does **exactly this** for an information system. Instead of rain, the "monsoons" are **cyber threats** (malware, insider attacks, zero-days). Instead of waterproofing, we install **security controls** (firewalls, encryption, access control). The "engineer inspecting the house" is the **assessor**, and the "occupancy certificate" is the **ATO**. ## 1.3 Why the RMF Matters for Trustworthy Systems Engineering > [!TIP] > A *trustworthy* system is one that not only **functions correctly** but can **demonstrate** that it does so — under adversarial conditions. The RMF is the **evidence-gathering engine** that produces that demonstration. Without RMF, security is a feeling; with RMF, security is a **documented, auditable artifact**. **Key Standardized Metrics (Bold-Coded):** - **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2** — The canonical RMF document. - **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** — Catalog of 1000+ security/privacy controls. - **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** — Risk assessment methodology. - **FIPS 199** — Categorization standard for confidentiality, integrity, availability. - **FIPS 200** — Minimum security requirements baseline. - **NIST SP 800-137** — Information Security Continuous Monitoring (ISCM) strategy. - **CISA / OMB A-130** — U.S. federal governance overlays. - **ISO/IEC 27005:2022** — International risk management standard aligned with RMF philosophy. ## 1.4 The Seven RMF Steps at a Glance | Step # | Step Name | Core Question Answered | |---|---|---| | 0 | **Prepare** | Are governance, resources, and risk tolerance in place? | | 1 | **Categorize** | What is the system's impact level? | | 2 | **Select** | Which controls are required? | | 3 | **Implement** | Have controls been deployed and configured? | | 4 | **Assess** | Do controls actually work? | | 5 | **Authorize** | Is residual risk acceptable? | | 6 | **Monitor** | Does the system remain trustworthy over time? | > [!WARNING] > **Common Misconception:** RMF is *not* a one-time checklist. It is a **lifecycle**. Skipping the "Monitor" step turns a trustworthy system into a legacy liability within months. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Seven Steps — Operational Breakdown ### Step 0 — Prepare (Governance Foundation) **Goal:** Establish the organizational context *before* touching any system. **Why it matters:** Without preparation, the RMF becomes ad-hoc and produces non-reproducible assessments. **How it works:** - Identify **risk tolerance levels** at the organizational tier. - Assign roles: **Authorizing Official (AO)**, **System Owner**, **Information System Security Officer (ISSO)**, **Security Control Assessor (SCA)**. - Develop a **system-level risk assessment strategy**. - Establish common controls inventory (services that multiple systems inherit, e.g., a centralized SOC). ### Step 1 — Categorize System **Goal:** Classify the system by impact level. **Standard (FIPS 199):** For each security objective (**C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability), assign one of: - **LOW** — limited adverse effect. - **MODERATE** — serious adverse effect. - **HIGH** — catastrophic adverse effect. The **overall system categorization** is the **highest watermark** of the three (e.g., if Confidentiality = HIGH and others = MODERATE, system is HIGH). ### Step 2 — Select Controls **Goal:** Tailor the baseline (from **NIST SP 800-53B**) and produce the **System Security Plan (SSP)**. **Process:** - Start with the baseline corresponding to categorization (Low / Moderate / High baseline). - **Tailor** — apply scoping, parameterization, supplementation, and compensating controls. - Document each control in the SSP with responsible roles, implementation status, and dependencies. ### Step 3 — Implement Controls **Goal:** Translate the SSP into deployed technical, operational, and management controls. **Key artifacts:** - Configuration baselines (e.g., CIS Benchmarks). - Hardening scripts. - Network architecture diagrams showing control placement. - Training records (awareness controls). ### Step 4 — Assess Controls **Goal:** Verify that the controls are **implemented correctly, operating as intended, and producing the desired outcome**. **Methodology (NIST SP 800-53A):** - **Examine** — review documentation, interview personnel. - **Test** — hands-on technical verification (pen-tests, vulnerability scans). - **Interview** — collect process-level evidence. **Output:** **Security Assessment Report (SAR)** — list of satisfied controls, partially satisfied controls, and failed controls, each with remediation guidance. ### Step 5 — Authorize System **Goal:** The **Authorizing Official (AO)** makes a risk-based decision. **Possible decisions:** - **Authorization to Operate (ATO)** — risk acceptable. - **Denial of Authorization to Operate (DATO)** — risk unacceptable. - **Interim Authorization to Operate (IATO)** — short-term conditional approval. The AO signs a formal **Authorization Decision Letter**. ### Step 6 — Monitor Controls **Goal:** Maintain ongoing situational awareness of security and risk posture. **Activities:** - Continuous vulnerability scanning. - Configuration management and change control. - Security control re-assessments (typically annually for HIGH systems). - **Information Security Continuous Monitoring (ISCM)** strategy per **NIST SP 800-137**. - Threat intelligence feeds integrated into monitoring dashboards. ## 2.2 Risk Assessment Math — KTU Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas and metrics are part of the **NIST SP 800-30** quantitative vocabulary. KTU examiners frequently test the conceptual understanding of these expressions. **Table 2.1 — KTU High-Yield Risk Formulas** | Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning | |---|---|---| | Impact Level | $I \in \{\text{LOW}, \text{MODERATE}, \text{HIGH}\}$ | Categorical severity if threat succeeds | | Likelihood | $L \in \{\text{LOW}, \text{MODERATE}, \text{HIGH}\}$ | Probability of threat occurrence | | Risk Magnitude | $R = f(I, L)$ | Determined via Risk Matrix lookup | | Risk Matrix Cell | $R_{i,j} = \text{Cell}(I_i, L_j)$ | Pre-mapped (LOW,MOD)→MOD, etc. | | Adjusted Risk | $R' = f(I, L, \text{controls})$ | Residual risk after mitigation | | ALE (Annual Loss Expectancy) | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Expected monetary loss per year | | SLE (Single Loss Expectancy) | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | Loss from one incident | | ARO (Annualized Rate of Occurrence) | $\text{ARO} \in \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ | Expected incidents per year | | Risk Reduction ROI | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} - \text{Cost}_{\text{control}}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{control}}}$ | Cost-benefit ratio | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary conditions:** Categorical values for $I$ and $L$ map through a *deterministic* $3 \times 3$ matrix. Continuous extensions use fuzzy logic, but KTU accepts the categorical form. ## 2.3 The 3×3 Risk Matrix (KTU Gold) | | L = LOW | L = MOD | L = HIGH | |---|---|---|---| | I = HIGH | MODERATE | HIGH | HIGH | | I = MOD | LOW | MODERATE | HIGH | | I = LOW | LOW | LOW | MODERATE | > [!TIP] > **Memory trick:** "Square it down" — matrix entry is the higher of $I$ and $L$ *unless both are LOW* (then stay LOW), *unless both are HIGH* (then stay HIGH). This matches the formula $R = \max(I, L)$ in qualitative scale. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | RMF Application | |---|---| | **Banking (PCI-DSS-aligned)** | Continuous monitoring of cardholder data environments. | | **Healthcare (HIPAA-aligned)** | Categorization of PHI systems; baseline = HIPAA Security Rule mapped to NIST 800-66. | | **Government (US Federal)** | Mandatory per **OMB Circular A-130** and **FISMA**. | | **Critical Infrastructure (Kerala State)** | KSEB / KSWRY/SCADA categorization follows RMF-derived impact tiering. | | **Cloud (AWS GovCloud, Azure Gov)** | FedRAMP authorizations are RMF-based. | > [!WARNING] > **RMF is mandatory, not optional**, for U.S. federal systems, FedRAMP cloud services, and many regulated industries. A KTU graduate entering cybersecurity consulting in 2025/26 will use RMF weekly. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Risk Management Assessment for a Hospital Patient Records System > [!NOTE] > **Scenario:** A hospital deploys a web-based Electronic Health Record (EHR) system. The CISO must produce an RMA following the RMF. We will walk through **all seven steps** with full derivations and numerical justifications. ### Step 0 — Prepare The CISO establishes: - Risk tolerance: *Low tolerance for patient data exposure, moderate tolerance for availability disruption under 4 hours.* - Roles: - **AO** = Hospital CEO. - **System Owner** = Chief Medical Information Officer. - **ISSO** = Hospital IT Security Lead. - **SCA** = External auditor (per independence requirement). - Common controls inherited: enterprise firewall, SOC services, physical datacenter security. ### Step 1 — Categorize (Using FIPS 199) For each security objective, evaluate worst-case impact: **Confidentiality (C):** Disclosure of patient records → identity theft, legal liability, HIPAA violation. $$ I_C = \text{HIGH} \quad \text{(catastrophic: mass patient harm, regulatory fines)} $$ **Integrity (I):** Unauthorized modification of prescriptions or diagnoses → direct patient injury. $$ I_I = \text{HIGH} \quad \text{(catastrophic: loss of life possible)} $$ **Availability (A):** 24-hour outage of EHR during emergency → inability to access records. $$ I_A = \text{MODERATE} \quad \text{(serious, but paper fallback exists)} $$ **Overall Categorization (Watermark Rule):** $$ I_{\text{system}} = \max(I_C, I_I, I_A) = \max(\text{HIGH}, \text{HIGH}, \text{MODERATE}) = \text{HIGH} $$ The system is therefore categorized as **HIGH-Impact**. ### Step 2 — Select Controls Starting baseline: **NIST SP 800-53 HIGH baseline** (≈ 421 controls across 20 control families). Tailoring decisions: | Tailoring Type | Decision | Justification | |---|---|---| | Scoping | Exclude controls applicable only to law-enforcement systems (e.g., AC-7 Unsuccessful Logon Attempts at the federal-investigation tier) | Hospital is civilian | | Parameterization | AC-2 account management — set review frequency to 90 days | Industry standard for healthcare | | Supplementation | Add cloud-specific controls (SC-7(9)) | EHR runs on vendor cloud | | Compensating | MP-6 media sanitization → use cryptographic erase | SSDs in use | Documented in **System Security Plan (SSP)**. ### Step 3 — Implement Controls Example implementation stack: - **AC (Access Control):** RBAC with mandatory MFA for all clinicians. - **AU (Audit):** Centralized SIEM with 1-year log retention. - **SC (System & Communications Protection):** TLS 1.3 for all data in transit, AES-256 for data at rest. - **IR (Incident Response):** 24/7 on-call rotation with 1-hour containment SLA. - **CP (Contingency Planning):** Hot-site failover with 4-hour RTO, 1-hour RPO. ### Step 4 — Assess Controls (with Risk Calculation) **Threat:** *External attacker exploits unpatched web server → exfiltrates 50,000 patient records.* | Parameter | Value | Derivation | |---|---|---| | Asset Value (records) | $50{,}000 \times \$200 = \$10{,}000{,}000$ | Avg HIPAA fine + remediation per record | | Exposure Factor (EF) | $0.80$ | 80% of records compromised in a breach | | SLE | $\text{Asset Value} \times \text{EF} = \$10{,}000{,}000 \times 0.80 = \$8{,}000{,}000$ | Direct loss per single event | | ARO (pre-control) | $0.5$ | Industry data: significant web breach every ~2 years | | ALE (pre-control) | $\text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = \$8{,}000{,}000 \times 0.5 = \$4{,}000{,}000$ | Annual expected loss | **Apply controls:** Deploy WAF + patch management + EDR. - New ARO = **0.05** (controls reduce likelihood 10×). - New EF = **0.50** (faster detection limits spread). - New SLE = $\$10{,}000{,}000 \times 0.50 = \$5{,}000{,}000$. - **ALE (post-control):** $$ \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = \$5{,}000{,}000 \times 0.05 = \$250{,}000 $$ **Cost of controls:** WAF licensing, EDR, SOC staffing ≈ **\$600,000/year**. **Annual Savings:** $$ \Delta\text{ALE} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = \$4{,}000{,}000 - \$250{,}000 = \$3{,}750{,}000 $$ **Net Benefit:** $$ \text{Net Benefit} = \Delta\text{ALE} - \text{Cost}_{\text{control}} = \$3{,}750{,}000 - \$600{,}000 = \$3{,}150{,}000 $$ **ROI:** $$ \text{ROI} = \dfrac{\$3{,}750{,}000 - \$600{,}000}{\$600{,}000} = 5.25 = 525\% $$ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** For every \$1 spent on controls, the hospital saves \$5.25 in expected loss reduction. The AO can now justify authorization on quantitative + qualitative grounds. ### Step 5 — Authorize The AO reviews the SAR and the cost-benefit analysis: - Residual risk = **MODERATE** (post-control, ARO reduced 10×). - AO accepts residual risk → issues **ATO** valid for **1 year** (typical for HIGH systems). - Authorization Decision Letter archived with SSP and SAR. ### Step 6 — Monitor Ongoing activities: - **Daily:** SIEM correlation, vulnerability scans of web servers. - **Weekly:** Patch status reports. - **Monthly:** Configuration drift audits against baseline. - **Annually:** Full security re-assessment, ATO re-issuance. - **Ad-hoc:** Re-authorize after major change (e.g., move to new cloud region). ## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which RMF Step Is Most Often Skipped? | Step | Common Real-World Skip Rate | Business Reason for Skipping | Compliance Consequence | |---|---|---|---| | 0 — Prepare | 30% | "We have no time for governance" | Inconsistent risk acceptance | | 1 — Categorize | 15% | "We are not federal" | Mis-aligned baseline | | 2 — Select | 20% | "We will use a checklist" | Non-tailored controls | | 3 — Implement | 10% | Least-skipped | Direct security gap | | 4 — Assess | 35% | Cost of independent assessors | Undetected control failures | | 5 — Authorize | 25% | "We trust our team" | No accountability | | 6 — Monitor | **60%** | "We are too busy operating" | Systemic decay over 12 months | > [!WARNING] > Step 6 has the highest skip rate in industry. This is *exactly* why the RMF places it as a peer of the other six steps — it is non-negotiable for trustworthiness. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid — RMF Seven-Step Lifecycle (with Feedback Loops) ```mermaid flowchart TD prepA[Step 0 Prepare
Governance Roles and Risk Tolerance] catB[Step 1 Categorize System
FIPS 199 Impact Analysis] selC[Step 2 Select Controls
NIST 800-53 Baseline plus Tailoring] impD[Step 3 Implement Controls
Deploy Technical and Procedural Safeguards] assE[Step 4 Assess Controls
Examine Test Interview] autF[Step 5 Authorize System
ATO or DATO Decision] monG[Step 6 Monitor Controls
Continuous Assessment and Change] prepA --> catB catB --> selC selC --> impD impD --> assE assE --> autF autF --> monG monG --> assE monG --> catB monG --> selC autF -. Denial .-> impD autF -. Interim ATO .-> monG ``` > [!NOTE] > The two **dashed feedback loops** from Step 6 back to Step 4 (Assess) and Step 1 (Categorize) capture the **continuous monitoring** mandate. The dashed return from Step 5 to Step 3 captures the **DATO remediation** path. ## 4.2 Mermaid — Risk Assessment Quantitative Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR aA[Asset Identification] bB[Threat Identification] cC[Vulnerability Identification] dD[Impact Analysis I] eE[Likelihood Analysis L] fF[Risk Determination R equals f of I and L] gG[Control Recommendation] hH[Adjusted Risk R Prime] iI[Authorize or Mitigate Decision] aA --> bB bB --> cC cD --> fF dD --> fF aA --> dD bB --> eE cC --> eE eE --> fF fF --> gG gG --> hH hH --> iI aA --> cD cC --> cD ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid — Block-Level Architecture of an RMA Deliverable Set ```mermaid flowchart TB org[Organizational Tier
Risk Tolerance and Governance] sys[System Tier
Target Information System] mission[Mission and Business Process Tier] org --> sys mission --> org subgraph RMA[RMA Deliverable Set] ssp1[System Security Plan SSP] sar2[Security Assessment Report SAR] ato3[Authority to Operate ATO] iscm4[ISCM Strategy] poam5[Plan of Action and Milestones POAM] end sys --> ssp1 sys --> sar2 sys --> ato3 sys --> iscm4 sys --> poam5 ssp1 --> sar2 sar2 --> ato3 ato3 --> iscm4 poam5 --> sar2 ``` > [!TIP] > The **POA&M (Plan of Action and Milestones)** is the bridge between the SAR (findings) and the ISCM strategy (tracking). It lists every weakness, owner, scheduled completion date, and current status. KTU students should remember POA&M as the **"to-do list"** of the RMF. ## 4.4 Mermaid — Authority and Responsibility RACI Overview ```mermaid flowchart LR ao1[Authorizing Official AO] so2[System Owner SO] isso3[ISSO] sca4[Security Control Assessor SCA] scmo5[Security Continuous Monitoring Manager] users6[Common Control Provider] privacy7[Privacy Officer] ao1 -->|Accountable for ATO| so2 so2 -->|Responsible for SSP| isso3 isso3 -->|Coordinates| sca4 isso3 -->|Reports to| scmo5 users6 -->|Provides inherited controls| isso3 privacy7 -->|Advises on PII controls| so2 sca4 -->|Produces SAR| ao1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define the Risk Management Framework (RMF). Mention its source document. > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Risk Management Framework (RMF)** is a structured, seven-step process that integrates security, privacy, and cyber supply chain risk management into the system development life cycle. It is defined in **NIST Special Publication 800-37 Revision 2**. The seven steps are: (1) Prepare, (2) Categorize System, (3) Select Controls, (4) Implement Controls, (5) Assess Controls, (6) Authorize System, and (7) Monitor Controls. RMF transforms the traditional *certify once and forget* approach into a *continuous authorization* paradigm. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. What is the role of an Authorizing Official (AO) in the RMF? > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Authorizing Official (AO)** is a senior organizational official with the authority to formally accept the residual risk of operating an information system. The AO reviews the **Security Assessment Report (SAR)**, the **System Security Plan (SSP)**, and the **Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M)**, and then issues one of three decisions: **Authorization to Operate (ATO)**, **Denial of Authorization to Operate (DATO)**, or **Interim Authorization to Operate (IATO)**. The AO bears ultimate accountability for the security posture of the system. **(3 marks)** --- ## Part B — Full ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, each module contains *internal choice* questions. Two fully independent alternatives are provided below. Both choices carry **7 + 7 = 14 marks** across sub-parts. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the seven steps of the **NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF)** as defined in **SP 800-37 Rev. 2**. For each step, identify the primary deliverable artifact. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mid-sized engineering firm hosts a **CAD design repository** worth ₹50 crore. Management estimates that a successful ransomware attack would compromise **70%** of files and would occur approximately **once every 3 years** if no controls are applied. After deploying EDR + immutable backups + MFA, the likelihood drops to **once every 30 years** and exposure factor drops to **0.30**. Calculate SLE, ARO (pre and post), ALE (pre and post), and the **annual savings in expected loss**.
