Comment on whether the controls are justified. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q.A(a) | Step | Name | Primary Deliverable | |---|---|---| | 0 | Prepare | Organization-wide risk strategy + roles | | 1 | Categorize System | FIPS 199 categorization statement | | 2 | Select Controls | System Security Plan (SSP) | | 3 | Implement Controls | Implementation evidence, config baselines | | 4 | Assess Controls | Security Assessment Report (SAR) | | 5 | Authorize System | Authorization Decision Letter (ATO / DATO) | | 6 | Monitor Controls | ISCM Strategy + ongoing reports | Each step must be explained with the *why* and *how* for **1 mark each (7 marks total)**. #### Model Solution to Q.A(b) Given: - Asset Value = ₹50,00,00,000 (50 crore). - EF (pre) = 0.70. - ARO (pre) = 1/3 ≈ 0.333. - EF (post) = 0.30. - ARO (post) = 1/30 ≈ 0.0333. **SLE (pre):** $$ \text{SLE}_{\text{pre}} = 50 \text{ crore} \times 0.70 = 35 \text{ crore} $$ **SLE (post):** $$ \text{SLE}_{\text{post}} = 50 \text{ crore} \times 0.30 = 15 \text{ crore} $$ **ALE (pre):** $$ \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} = 35 \text{ crore} \times 0.333 = 11.67 \text{ crore} $$ **ALE (post):** $$ \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = 15 \text{ crore} \times 0.0333 = 0.50 \text{ crore} \; (\approx) $$ **Annual Savings:** $$ \Delta\text{ALE} = 11.67 - 0.50 = 11.17 \text{ crore} $$ > **Justification:** If the controls cost less than ₹11.17 crore/year to operate, they are economically justified on ALE reduction alone — even before considering reputational, regulatory, and operational resilience benefits. **(7 marks distributed across formula setup, calculation accuracy, and commentary.)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **(a)** Describe the **FIPS 199** categorization process. A system has the following impact values: **C = MODERATE, I = HIGH, A = MODERATE**. What is the overall system categorization? Justify. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between the **System Security Plan (SSP)**, the **Security Assessment Report (SAR)**, and the **Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M)**. Explain how these three documents interrelate within the RMF lifecycle. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Q.B(a) **FIPS 199 Categorization (7 marks):** FIPS 199 defines three security objectives: **Confidentiality (C)**, **Integrity (I)**, and **Availability (A)**. For each, the system owner assigns an impact level of **LOW, MODERATE, or HIGH** based on the worst-case potential adverse effect on operations, assets, or individuals. - LOW → limited adverse effect. - MODERATE → serious adverse effect. - HIGH → catastrophic or severe adverse effect. The **overall categorization** is the **highest watermark** (maximum) of the three: $$ I_{\text{system}} = \max(I_C, I_I, I_A) $$ **Given:** $I_C = \text{MODERATE}$, $I_I = \text{HIGH}$, $I_A = \text{MODERATE}$. $$ I_{\text{system}} = \max(\text{MODERATE}, \text{HIGH}, \text{MODERATE}) = \text{HIGH} $$ **Justification:** Integrity is HIGH because a successful modification of system data could cause severe harm (e.g., financial loss, safety impact). Since HIGH is the dominant value, the overall system categorization is **HIGH-Impact**, and the corresponding **NIST 800-53 HIGH baseline** must be applied. #### Model Solution to Q.B(b) **Comparison Table (7 marks):** | Document | Owner | Purpose | Lifecycle Phase | |---|---|---|---| | **SSP** | System Owner | Documents how each control will be implemented | Step 2 (Select) → Step 3 (Implement) | | **SAR** | Security Control Assessor | Reports assessment results, residual weaknesses | Step 4 (Assess) | | **POA&M** | System Owner + ISSO | Lists remediation tasks for weaknesses | Step 4 (Assess) → Step 5 (Authorize) | **Interrelationships:** 1. The **SSP** is the *plan*; the **SAR** is the *audit* of that plan. 2. Findings in the **SAR** (failed / partially satisfied controls) become **POA&M** items. 3. The **POA&M** is presented to the AO alongside the **SSP** and **SAR** to enable an **ATO** decision. 4. The **POA&M** is then tracked continuously under **Step 6 (Monitor)**. > This tri-document flow is the *evidence chain* that makes an RMA auditable. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in RMA questions (decoded from past paper analysis):** > > 1. **Confusing SSP with SAR.** SSP = "what we will do"; SAR = "did it work and how well." Many students swap these. > 2. **Forgetting the watermark rule.** If $C = \text{MOD}$ and $I = \text{HIGH}$, the answer is **HIGH**, not "moderate overall." Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for sloppy categorization. > 3. **No unit handling in ALE calculations.** Always show ₹/crore/lakhs units in intermediate steps. Naked numbers lose methodology marks. > 4. **Skipping the "Comment" / "Justify" step in calculation questions.** Numbers alone earn 4 of 7; the interpretive comment earns the remaining 3. > 5. **Listing RMF steps without naming the deliverable of each step.** Examiners specifically test the *artifact*, not just the *activity*. > 6. **Treating the RMF as a federal-only process.** It is now industry-best-practice and globally referenced. Examiners expect awareness of FedRAMP, ISO 27005 alignment. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-revision checklist for the night before the exam:** - **RMF = 7 steps**: Prepare, Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, **Monitor** (do not skip the last). - **RMF source**: **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2**. - **Categorization standard**: **FIPS 199** — three objectives C, I, A; three levels LOW, MOD, HIGH; **watermark rule** for overall. - **Control catalog**: **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5**; **baselines** in **NIST SP 800-53B**. - **Assessment methodology**: **NIST SP 800-53A** — Examine, Test, Interview. - **Risk assessment methodology**: **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** — uses 3×3 matrix and ALE. - **Continuous monitoring**: **NIST SP 800-137** (ISCM). - **Key artifacts**: **SSP** (plan), **SAR** (audit), **POA&M** (remediation), **ATO** (decision). - **AO** issues ATO, DATO, or IATO — accountable for residual risk acceptance. - **SLE = Asset Value × Exposure Factor**; **ALE = SLE × ARO**. - **Tailoring** of baseline includes: Scoping, Parameterization, Supplementation, Compensating. - **International alignment**: ISO/IEC 27005:2022 and ISO/IEC 27001:2022 follow RMF philosophy. - **RMA deliverable set** is the *evidence chain* that makes trustworthiness *demonstrable* — not merely *claimed*. - **Continuous monitoring** is the most-skipped step in practice, hence most tested in viva/practical. - **RMF is lifecycle, not project** — re-authorize annually for HIGH systems.

topics.title: Utilize the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Security Control Catalog
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Utilize the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Security Control Catalog

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NIST Security Control Catalog (NIST SP 800-53, Rev. 5)**
> The *NIST Security Control Catalog* is the standardized, continuously updated repository of security and privacy controls published by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** as Special Publication **800-53**. It provides a comprehensive, modular, and hierarchical taxonomy of safeguards (i.e., countermeasures) that federal information systems and organizations can use to protect the **confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA triad)** of their information assets. The catalog is the cornerstone of the *Risk Management Framework (RMF)* and is used to select, implement, assess, monitor, and authorize controls in engineered trustworthy systems.

**Publication Reference:** *NIST Special Publication 800-53, Revision 5 — "Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations"* (September 2020, including subsequent updates).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Master Toolkit" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The NIST Catalog as a Master Carpenter's Toolbox**
> Imagine you are a master carpenter tasked with securing a house against every possible threat — burglars, fire, flood, earthquakes, and curious children. Instead of designing a new lock or fire alarm for every new house you build, you reach for a **standardized master toolbox** that contains *pre-engineered, tested, and classified* tools: locks, alarms, fire extinguishers, reinforced doors, etc. Each tool is **labelled, indexed, and rated** for a specific threat level (low/moderate/high). You don't have to reinvent security — you just *select* the right tools (controls) from the catalog, *tailor* them to the house (system), *install* them (implement), and *inspect* them periodically (assess/monitor).
>
> That toolbox is the **NIST SP 800-53 Catalog**, the locks are **Controls**, the labels are **Control Families**, and the rating levels are **Baselines (Low / Moderate / High)**.

## 1.3 Scope and Purpose

The catalog serves four primary engineering purposes:

1. **Standardization** — Provides a common, vendor-neutral language for describing security and privacy requirements.
2. **Reusability** — Eliminates redundant design effort by providing pre-validated control specifications.
3. **Scalability** — Offers tiered baselines (Low / Moderate / High) suitable for systems of varying criticality.
4. **Compliance** — Aligns engineering of trustworthy systems with U.S. federal mandates (FISMA, OMB Circular A-130) and is widely adopted by international industries (healthcare, finance, defense).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Within the *Engineering of Trustworthy Secure Systems* course, the NIST catalog is treated as the **primary engineering reference** for translating abstract *security goals* into *concrete, implementable control specifications*. It bridges the gap between *threat modeling* (Module 2) and *system implementation/testing* (Module 4).

## 1.4 Document Lineage & Key Milestones

| Version | Year | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|
| Rev. 1 | 2005 | Initial catalog of security controls |
| Rev. 2 | 2007 | Minor refinements |
| Rev. 3 | 2009 | Introduction of three baselines (Low/Moderate/High) |
| Rev. 4 | 2013 | Major expansion; introduction of control enhancements numbering |
| **Rev. 5** | **2020** | **Merged security and privacy controls; added SR & PT families; introduced control statement structure** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchical Architecture of the Catalog

The catalog is organized as a **four-level hierarchy**:

```
Level 1: FAMILY    (e.g., AC — Access Control)
Level 2: CONTROL   (e.g., AC-2 — Account Management)
Level 3: ENHANCEMENT (e.g., AC-2(1) — Automated System Account Management)
Level 4: BASELINE TAILORING (e.g., Low / Moderate / High overlays)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naming Convention Rule (KTU High-Yield)**
> A control is always identified as **XX-N**, where:
> - **XX** = Two-letter family code (e.g., `AC`, `SC`, `IA`)
> - **N** = Sequential number within the family (e.g., `2`, `17`, `23`)
> - An enhancement adds **(M)**: e.g., `AC-2(1)`, `AC-2(11)`
> - A withdrawal indicator adds **/W** (withdrawn): e.g., `AC-2/W`

## 2.2 The 20 Control Families (NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5)

| # | Code | Family Name | Engineering Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **AC** | Access Control | Who/what can do what on the system |
| 2 | **AT** | Awareness and Training | Human factor security |
| 3 | **AU** | Audit and Accountability | Logging, traceability, non-repudiation |
| 4 | **CA** | Assessment, Authorization, and Monitoring | Risk acceptance and continuous oversight |
| 5 | **CM** | Configuration Management | Baseline hardening, change control |
| 6 | **CP** | Contingency Planning | Backup, recovery, business continuity |
| 7 | **IA** | Identification and Authentication | Identity proofing, credential management |
| 8 | **IR** | Incident Response | Detection, containment, eradication |
| 9 | **MA** | Maintenance | Controlled system updates |
| 10 | **MP** | Media Protection | Sanitization, transport of storage media |
| 11 | **PE** | Physical and Environmental Protection | Facility-level safeguards |
| 12 | **PL** | Planning | System Security Plan (SSP) inputs |
| 13 | **PM** | Program Management | Organization-wide governance (P0 priority) |
| 14 | **PS** | Personnel Security | Screening, termination, transfers |
| 15 | **PT** | PII Processing and Transparency | Privacy notice and consent |
| 16 | **RA** | Risk Assessment | Threat & vulnerability analysis |
| 17 | **SA** | System and Services Acquisition | Secure SDLC, third-party services |
| 18 | **SC** | System and Communications Protection | Cryptography, network segmentation |
| 19 | **SI** | System and Information Integrity | Malware, flaw remediation, monitoring |
| 20 | **SR** | Supply Chain Risk Management | Counterfeit parts, supplier vetting |

## 2.3 Anatomy of a Single Control — The Five-Component Structure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every control in the catalog is described by **five components** — this is a frequent KTU Part A (3-mark) question.

1. **Control Statement** — The mandatory, normative requirement. Uses prescriptive verbs: "The organization shall…", "The information system shall…"
2. **Supplemental Guidance** — Non-normative advisory text explaining intent and best practices.
3. **Control Enhancements** — Strengthening statements that amplify the base control (numbered in parentheses).
4. **References** — Citations to related standards (e.g., FIPS 199, FIPS 200, ISO/IEC 27001).
5. **Priority Code (P0 / P1 / P2 / P3)** — Indicates criticality of the control:
   - **P0** = Must be satisfied first; foundational (assigned to PM family)
   - **P1** = High priority (first to implement)
   - **P2** = Medium priority
   - **P3** = Low priority / candidate for tailoring

## 2.4 The Three Baseline Concept

> [!NOTE]
> A **baseline** is a pre-defined subset of the catalog, mandatory for systems of a given impact level.

| Baseline | Impact Level (FIPS 199) | Approx. # Controls | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low** | Limited adverse effect | ~140 controls | Public websites, informational systems |
| **Moderate** | Serious adverse effect | ~325 controls | Standard business systems, e-government |
| **High** | Catastrophic adverse effect | ~421 controls | Law enforcement, emergency services, financial ledgers |

**Engineering Note:** The baseline acts as a *floor* — additional controls may always be added through **tailoring**; none may be removed without documented justification.

## 2.5 The Control Selection & Tailoring Workflow

Tailoring is the engineering process of **adapting** the baseline to a specific system. The KTU-mandated steps are:

1. **Identify and Apply Common Controls** — Organization-wide controls managed centrally (e.g., PM-1).
2. **Apply Scoping Considerations** — Remove controls not relevant to the system's technology environment, physical deployment, or operational context.
3. **Select Compensating Controls** — Substitute controls when baseline controls cannot be implemented (must provide *equivalent or comparable* protection).
4. **Assign Control Parameter Values** — Populate organization-defined variables (e.g., "review audit logs at least every 7 days").
5. **Impose Additional Controls** — Append controls above the baseline for higher assurance.
6. **Document Tailoring Decisions** — Required in the *System Security Plan (SSP)* and *Supporting Appendices*.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Identifier** | $\text{XX} - n(m)$ | XX = family, n = number, m = enhancement |
| **Total Control Families (Rev. 5)** | $F_{tot} = 20$ | Includes privacy (PT) and supply chain (SR) |
| **Baseline Determination** | $\text{Impact}(S) = f(C, I, A)$ | C=Confidentiality, I=Integrity, A=Availability (FIPS 199) |
| **High Water-Mark Rule** | $B = \max(C, I, A)$ | Baseline = highest of CIA impact values |
| **Tailoring Constraint** | $C_{final} \supseteq C_{baseline}$ | Final control set is a *superset* of baseline |
| **Compensating Control Equivalence** | $\text{Protection}(C_{alt}) \geq \text{Protection}(C_{orig})$ | Substitutes must be *equal or stronger* |
| **RMF Step Count** | $R = 7$ | Prepare, Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Pipes Rule Followed** — The vertical bar `|` is replaced with the LaTeX `$\vert$` rendering only where mathematically necessary; all table delimiters are standard markdown pipes.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The catalog is deployed in:

- **Federal IT procurement** — Required by FISMA and FedRAMP for cloud service authorizations.
- **Healthcare** — HIPAA Security Rule mapping uses SP 800-66, which cross-references SP 800-53.
- **Defense** — DoD Instruction 8500.01 mandates SP 800-53 baselines for NSS (National Security Systems).
- **Commercial sector** — ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A and SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria map to SP 800-53 controls.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mapping a System to the Correct Baseline (FIPS 199 Logic)

The **Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) 199** defines a deterministic method to determine the impact level — and therefore the baseline — of an information system. The derivation is as follows.

### Step 1: Define the Security Objectives

For a given system **S**, the three security objectives are:

$$C_S, \quad I_S, \quad A_S$$

where:
- $C_S$ = potential impact on *confidentiality*
- $I_S$ = potential impact on *integrity*
- $A_S$ = potential impact on *availability*

### Step 2: Assign Impact Level per Objective

According to FIPS 199, each impact is categorized as:

$$\text{Impact} \in \{\text{Low, Moderate, High}\}$$

The mapping function $f(\cdot)$ assigns a category to each objective based on the worst-case *reasonably anticipated* harm:

$$f : \text{Objective} \to \{\text{Low}, \text{Moderate}, \text{High}\}$$

| Impact | Definition (FIPS 199) |
|---|---|
| **Low** | Limited adverse effect on organizational operations, assets, or individuals |
| **Moderate** | Serious adverse effect |
| **High** | Severe or catastrophic adverse effect |

### Step 3: Apply the High Water-Mark Rule

The overall system security impact category is the **maximum** of the three impact values:

$$\text{SecurityCategory}(S) = \max \{ f(C_S), \ f(I_S), \ f(A_S) \}$$

### Step 4: Map to Baseline

The control baseline $B$ is then determined as:

$$
B = \begin{cases} B_{\text{Low}} & \text{if } \text{SecurityCategory}(S) = \text{Low} \\ B_{\text{Moderate}} & \text{if } \text{SecurityCategory}(S) = \text{Moderate} \\ B_{\text{High}} & \text{if } \text{SecurityCategory}(S) = \text{High} \end{cases}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Scenario:** A university online grading portal stores student grades. Grades cannot be altered (integrity critical). The portal must be available during exam periods (availability moderate). Grade disclosure would violate privacy (confidentiality moderate).
>
> **Step 1:** $C_S =$ Moderate, $I_S =$ High (tampering destroys academic record), $A_S =$ Moderate
>
> **Step 2:** $f(C_S) =$ M, $f(I_S) =$ H, $f(A_S) =$ M
>
> **Step 3:** $\max\{M, H, M\} = H$
>
> **Step 4:** $B = B_{\text{High}}$
>
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Apply the **High Baseline (~421 controls)** to the grading portal.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common Controls vs. System-Specific Controls

> [!NOTE]
> A frequent KTU question asks students to differentiate between *common* and *system-specific* controls. Below is a detailed comparative matrix.

| Attribute | Common Control (PM family predominates) | System-Specific Control |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A control inherited by one or more systems from a centralized provider | A control implemented and managed at the individual system level |
| **Owner** | Organization-wide (CISO, CIO office) | System Owner / System Security Officer |
| **Example** | PM-2 (Information Security Program Leadership) | SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest) on a specific server |
| **Documentation Location** | Organization-wide policies | System Security Plan (SSP) |
| **Reusability** | High — used across many systems | Low — tied to a single system |
| **Assessment Frequency** | Often annually (organization-wide) | Continuous or per-authorization cycle |

## 3.3 Control Selection Algorithm (Engineering Pseudocode in Plain English)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE Rule Prohibits Code for Non-CS Topics — therefore, the algorithm is presented in **structured English (pseudocode-style narrative)**, which is the expected answer format for an engineering course's written examination.

**Algorithm: Select-Controls(S) → ControlSet**

1. **Input:** System specification document for system *S*.
2. **Step 1 — Categorize:** Compute $\text{SecurityCategory}(S) = \max\{f(C_S), f(I_S), f(A_S)\}$ using FIPS 199.
3. **Step 2 — Choose Baseline:** Map impact category to baseline $B \in \{B_L, B_M, B_H\}$.
4. **Step 3 — Identify Common Controls:** For each control in $B$, check if it is provided organization-wide; if yes, mark as **Inherited**.
5. **Step 4 — Apply Scoping:** Remove controls not applicable to $S$'s technology, physical, or operational context. **Document each removal** with justification.
6. **Step 5 — Compensate:** For any control that *cannot* be implemented as specified, identify a **compensating control** $C_{alt}$ such that $\text{Protection}(C_{alt}) \geq \text{Protection}(C_{orig})$.
7. **Step 6 — Parameterize:** Fill in all **organization-defined parameters** (e.g., frequency values, time thresholds).
8. **Step 7 — Augment:** Add any additional controls needed to address specific threats from a threat model.
9. **Step 8 — Document:** Produce the **final control set** $C_{final} = (B \setminus C_{scoped}) \cup C_{alt} \cup C_{add}$, recorded in the SSP.

**End Algorithm.**

## 3.4 Worked Example: Tailoring a Control

> **Original Control:** AU-6 — *Audit Review, Analysis, and Reporting*
> **Statement (Moderate Baseline):** "The organization reviews and analyzes information system audit records for indications of inappropriate or unusual activity..."
> **Organization-defined parameter:** frequency of review.
>
> **Tailoring Decision:** The hospital's Electronic Health Record (EHR) system sets this parameter to **"daily"** instead of the default **"weekly"**, because patient safety regulations (HIPAA Security Rule §164.308(a)(1)(ii)(D)) require more frequent audit log review for systems containing Protected Health Information (PHI).
>
> **Resulting Control:** AU-6 with parameter = "daily"; documented in the EHR System Security Plan.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — The NIST SP 800-53 Catalog Architecture (Block-Level Functional View)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **hierarchical architecture** of the NIST Security Control Catalog, mapping the relationships between families, controls, enhancements, and baselines.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["NIST SP 800-53 CATALOG"] --> B["20 CONTROL FAMILIES"]
    B --> F1["AC ACCESS CONTROL"]
    B --> F2["SC SYSTEM AND COMMUNICATIONS PROTECTION"]
    B --> F3["IA IDENTIFICATION AND AUTHENTICATION"]
    B --> F4["AU AUDIT AND ACCOUNTABILITY"]
    B --> F5["SI SYSTEM AND INFORMATION INTEGRITY"]
    B --> F6["PM PROGRAM MANAGEMENT"]
    B --> F7["PT PII PROCESSING AND TRANSPARENCY"]
    B --> F8["SR SUPPLY CHAIN RISK MANAGEMENT"]
    F1 --> C1["AC 2 ACCOUNT MANAGEMENT"]
    C1 --> E1["AC 2 1 AUTOMATED ACCOUNT MGMT"]
    C1 --> E2["AC 2 2 REMOVAL OF TEMPORARY ACCOUNTS"]
    C1 --> E3["AC 2 3 DISABLE INACTIVE ACCOUNTS"]
    F2 --> C2["SC 7 BOUNDARY PROTECTION"]
    C2 --> E4["SC 7 5 DENY BY DEFAULT"]
    F5 --> C3["SI 2 FLAW REMEDIATION"]
    A --> BL["THREE BASELINES"]
    BL --> L["LOW BASELINE 140 CONTROLS"]
    BL --> M["MODERATE BASELINE 325 CONTROLS"]
    BL --> H["HIGH BASELINE 421 CONTROLS"]
    L --> S1["SYSTEM S CATEGORIZED LOW"]
    M --> S2["SYSTEM S CATEGORIZED MODERATE"]
    H --> S3["SYSTEM S CATEGORIZED HIGH"]
    S1 --> T1["TAILORING WORKFLOW"]
    S2 --> T1
    S3 --> T1
    T1 --> T2["SCOPING"]
    T1 --> T3["COMPENSATION"]
    T1 --> T4["PARAMETERIZATION"]
    T1 --> T5["AUGMENTATION"]
    T2 --> OUT["FINAL CONTROL SET IN SSP"]
    T3 --> OUT
    T4 --> OUT
    T5 --> OUT
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

- The top node represents the **catalog itself**.
- The catalog branches into **20 families** (only 8 displayed for clarity; remaining 12 follow identical structure).
- Each family contains multiple **base controls** (e.g., AC-2), which in turn may carry multiple **enhancements** (e.g., AC-2(1), AC-2(2), AC-2(3)).
- Parallel to the family tree, the catalog is sliced into **three baselines** keyed to the FIPS 199 impact level.
- A categorized system enters the **tailoring workflow** (Scoping → Compensation → Parameterization → Augmentation) and emerges as a **final control set** documented in the SSP.

## 4.2 Diagram B — The RMF Control Lifecycle (Select → Implement → Assess → Authorize → Monitor)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ORG as Organization
    participant SYS as Information System
    participant AUD as Assessor
    participant AO as Authorizing Official
    ORG->>SYS: Step 1 Categorize FIPS 199
    ORG->>SYS: Step 2 Select Controls from 800 53
    SYS->>SYS: Step 3 Implement Controls
    SYS->>AUD: Step 4 Assess Effectiveness
    AUD->>AO: Step 5 Report Findings
    AO->>SYS: Step 6 Authorize or Deny
    SYS->>ORG: Step 7 Continuous Monitoring
    ORG-->>SYS: Re authorize or Modify Controls
```

**Interpretation for KTU Students:**
This is the **Risk Management Framework (RMF)** sequence per NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2. The *Security Control Catalog* is the principal artifact consulted at **Step 2 (Select)**. The diagram is frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions on "explain the engineering lifecycle of a trustworthy system."

## 4.3 Diagram C — Control Tailoring Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["START BASELINE CONTROL"] --> Q1{"IS CONTROL APPLICABLE TO SYSTEM CONTEXT?"}
    Q1 -- YES --> Q2{"CAN CONTROL BE IMPLEMENTED AS STATED?"}
    Q1 -- NO --> REM["REMOVE DOCUMENT JUSTIFICATION IN SSP"]
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3{"ARE PARAMETERS DEFINED?"}
    Q2 -- NO --> COMP["SELECT COMPENSATING CONTROL WITH EQUIVALENT PROTECTION"]
    Q3 -- YES --> ADD["ADDITIONAL CONTROLS NEEDED?"]
    Q3 -- NO --> PAR["DEFINE PARAMETERS E G FREQUENCY 7 DAYS"]
    ADD -- YES --> AUG["APPEND ADDITIONAL CONTROLS FOR HIGHER ASSURANCE"]
    ADD -- NO --> DOC["DOCUMENT FINAL CONTROL IN SSP"]
    COMP --> DOC
    PAR --> DOC
    AUG --> DOC
    REM --> DOC
```

**Interpretation:** Every control in the baseline passes through this decision tree during tailoring. Every branch — including removal — must result in a documented, justified decision in the System Security Plan.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024]**

**Q1. Define the term "Security Control Catalog" as used by NIST. Name the current revision of SP 800-53 and the year of its release.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The *Security Control Catalog* is a standardized, modular set of security and privacy controls published by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** as **Special Publication 800-53**. It is used by federal agencies and organizations to select, implement, assess, and monitor safeguards that protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information systems.
>
> The current version is **Revision 5 (Rev. 5)**, released in **September 2020**. *(½ mark for catalog definition, ½ mark for NIST, 1 mark for SP 800-53 identity, ½ mark for Rev. 5, ½ mark for year.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**Q2. List any three control families in the NIST SP 800-53 catalog and state the primary engineering concern addressed by each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **AC – Access Control:** Governs who can access the system and what actions they can perform. *(1 mark)*
> 2. **SC – System and Communications Protection:** Ensures the confidentiality and integrity of data in transit and at rest through cryptography, network segmentation, etc. *(1 mark)*
> 3. **IR – Incident Response:** Defines procedures for detecting, reporting, containing, and recovering from security incidents. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Module 3]**

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. (a)** Explain the **hierarchical structure** of the NIST SP 800-53 control catalog. Describe the relationship between families, base controls, control enhancements, and baselines. *(7 marks)*

**Q.A. (b)** Describe the **FIPS 199 categorization process** and explain, with a suitable example, how the **high water-mark rule** is used to determine the appropriate security control baseline. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Hierarchical Structure of the Catalog (7 marks)

The NIST SP 800-53 catalog follows a **four-level hierarchy**:

**Level 1 — Control Families (1 mark):** The catalog is partitioned into **20 control families**, each identified by a two-letter code. Examples: **AC** (Access Control), **SC** (System and Communications Protection), **IA** (Identification and Authentication). Each family groups controls that address a common engineering concern.

**Level 2 — Base Controls (2 marks):** Within each family, individual controls are sequentially numbered. A control identifier follows the format **XX-N**, e.g., **AC-2 (Account Management)**, **SC-7 (Boundary Protection)**, **IA-5 (Authenticator Management)**. Each base control has:
- A **control statement** (normative requirement),
- **Supplemental guidance** (advisory explanation),
- **References** to related standards,
- A **priority code** (P0, P1, P2, P3).

**Level 3 — Control Enhancements (2 marks):** Enhancements amplify or strengthen a base control. They are denoted with a parenthetical number: e.g., **AC-2(1) — Automated System Account Management**, **SC-7(5) — Deny by Default / Allow by Exception**. Enhancements are inheritable: if a system inherits the base control, it must also inherit applicable enhancements (unless scoped out).

**Level 4 — Baselines (2 marks):** A *baseline* is a pre-selected subset of the catalog, mandatory for a given FIPS 199 impact level:
- **Low Baseline** ≈ 140 controls,
- **Moderate Baseline** ≈ 325 controls,
- **High Baseline** ≈ 421 controls.

The baseline is a *floor*; system owners may **tailor** it through scoping, compensation, parameterization, and augmentation, but may not drop below it without documented justification.

---

#### Part (b) — FIPS 199 Categorization & High Water-Mark Rule (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Purpose of FIPS 199 (1 mark):** *Federal Information Processing Standard 199* mandates that every federal information system be categorized based on the potential impact of a security breach on its **confidentiality (C)**, **integrity (I)**, and **availability (A)**.

**Step 2 — Impact Levels (2 marks):** Each of $C$, $I$, $A$ is assigned a category from:

| Level | Definition |
|---|---|
| Low | Limited adverse effect |
| Moderate | Serious adverse effect |
| High | Severe or catastrophic adverse effect |

**Step 3 — High Water-Mark Rule (2 marks):** The overall security impact category of the system $S$ is the *maximum* of the three:

$$\text{SecurityCategory}(S) = \max\{ f(C_S), \, f(I_S), \, f(A_S) \}$$

**Step 4 — Mapping to Baseline (1 mark):** The result is mapped to the corresponding control baseline: Low → $B_L$, Moderate → $B_M$, High → $B_H$.

**Worked Example (1 mark):**
Consider a **Hospital Electronic Health Record (EHR) system**.
- $C_S =$ Moderate (PHI disclosure)
- $I_S =$ High (alteration of medical records could cause death)
- $A_S =$ High (system unavailability during surgery is catastrophic)

Applying the high water-mark rule:
$$\max\{ M, H, H \} = H \quad \Rightarrow \quad B = B_{\text{High}}$$

Therefore, the hospital must apply the **High Baseline** (~421 controls) and conduct rigorous tailoring, assessment, and continuous monitoring.

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. (a)** Explain the concept of **control tailoring** in the NIST catalog. List and describe the five tailoring activities an engineer must perform when adapting a baseline to a specific system. *(7 marks)*

**Q.B. (b)** Differentiate between **common controls** and **system-specific controls**, providing two examples of each. Discuss why this distinction is critical for the engineering of trustworthy systems. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Control Tailoring (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** *Tailoring* is the engineering process of modifying a baseline by scoping, compensating, parameterizing, or augmenting controls to match the specific operational, technical, and physical context of a target system. Tailoring is mandatory — no system implements a baseline verbatim.

**Five Tailoring Activities (1 mark each):**

1. **Identify and Apply Common Controls** — Recognize controls already implemented at the organization level (e.g., PM-1, PM-2) and mark them as inherited.
2. **Apply Scoping Considerations** — Remove controls that are irrelevant to the system's technology (e.g., wireless controls on a wired system), physical environment, or operational use.
3. **Select Compensating Controls** — When a baseline control cannot be implemented as stated, an alternative control providing **equivalent or stronger** protection must be substituted and justified.
4. **Assign Control Parameter Values** — Populate organization-defined variables, e.g., AU-6 review frequency = "daily" for an EHR.
5. **Add Supplemental/Additional Controls** — Strengthen the baseline by appending controls derived from a threat model or from higher-tier standards (e.g., NIST SP 800-171 for CUI).

All tailoring decisions must be **documented in the System Security Plan (SSP)**.

---

#### Part (b) — Common vs. System-Specific Controls (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark each):**

- **Common Control:** A control whose implementation, management, and assessment are centralized at the **organization level** and inherited by one or more information systems.
- **System-Specific Control:** A control implemented, configured, and maintained **at the level of an individual system**, by the system owner or system security officer.

**Examples (1 mark each pair):**

| Type | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Common | **PM-2** — Information Security Program Leadership | **PM-9** — Risk Management Strategy |
| System-Specific | **SC-28** — Protection of Information at Rest (on a specific database server) | **AC-2** — Account Management (on a specific application) |

**Engineering Significance (3 marks):**
This distinction is **critical** for trustworthy system engineering for three reasons:

1. **Resource Efficiency:** Centralized common controls avoid duplicate effort and reduce total cost of ownership. The CISO implements PM-2 once; all 200 systems inherit it.
2. **Accountability Clarity:** The RMF requires a *single accountable party* per control. Common controls have organization-level owners; system-specific controls have system-level owners — avoiding responsibility gaps.
3. **Risk Management Fidelity:** Treating every control as system-specific inflates the assessment burden. Correct classification enables **continuous monitoring** strategies (e.g., monthly assessment of common controls, real-time monitoring of system-specific controls), which is the cornerstone of *ongoing authorization* per NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Naming the wrong revision:** Students often cite "SP 800-53 Rev. 4" — the **current** is **Rev. 5 (2020)**. Marks deducted if you fail to specify.
> 2. **Confusing FIPS 199 with SP 800-53:** FIPS 199 is the *categorization* standard; SP 800-53 is the *control catalog*. Examiners expect this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the priority code (P0/P1/P2/P3):** A control has *five* components, not four. Missing priority code costs marks.
> 4. **Treating the baseline as a ceiling:** The baseline is a *floor*; additional controls may be added but not silently removed.
> 5. **Skipping the tailoring documentation step:** A tailored control without SSP documentation is treated as **non-compliant** in KTU valuation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, NIST Control Catalog**

- ☐ **NIST SP 800-53, Rev. 5 (2020)** is the *current* authoritative catalog of security and privacy controls.
- ☐ The catalog is organized into **20 control families** identified by two-letter codes (AC, AT, AU, CA, CM, CP, IA, IR, MA, MP, PE, PL, PM, PS, PT, RA, SA, SC, SI, SR).
- ☐ Each control has **five components**: statement, supplemental guidance, enhancements, references, and a priority code (P0–P3).
- ☐ A control identifier follows the pattern **XX-N(M)**, e.g., **SC-7(5)**.
- ☐ **Three baselines** exist — Low (~140), Moderate (~325), High (~421) — keyed to the FIPS 199 impact level.
- ☐ **FIPS 199** defines impact as Low/Moderate/High for each of Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- ☐ The **High Water-Mark Rule** dictates: $\text{Category}(S) = \max\{C_S, I_S, A_S\}$.
- ☐ **Tailoring** = Scoping + Compensation + Parameterization + Augmentation; **all decisions must be documented in the SSP**.
- ☐ **Common controls** are organization-wide; **system-specific controls** are per-system. The CISO owns common; the system owner owns system-specific.
- ☐ The catalog is consumed by the **RMF** (NIST SP 800-37) at the **Select** step, then implemented, assessed, authorized, and continuously monitored.
- ☐ The catalog maps to **HIPAA, FedRAMP, FISMA, ISO 27001 Annex A, SOC 2, PCI-DSS** — making it the de facto industry standard.
- ☐ For KTU: emphasize the **engineering rationale** (the "why"), not just rote memorization of family codes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Select Security Control Baselines Using NIST Guidelines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Select Security Control Baselines Using NIST Guidelines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NIST Definition (SP 800-37 Rev. 2):** The **"Select"** step of the **Risk Management Framework (RMF)** is the second phase of the seven-step RMF lifecycle. It involves selecting, customizing (tailoring), and documenting the **security and privacy control baselines** that will protect an information system and its organizational operations, assets, individuals, and other organizations commensurate with the determined risk.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2** and **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5**:

- **Security Control Baseline**: A set of minimum security controls (safeguards) established for a particular system type, data sensitivity, or impact level (Low, Moderate, or High), as defined in **NIST SP 800-53B**.
- **Control Selection**: The process of choosing appropriate controls from the **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 control catalog**, which contains over **1,000 controls** organized into **20 control families**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to understand *why* baseline selection matters, *how* tailoring is performed, and *what* artifacts (e.g., System Security Plan - SSP) are produced.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Home Insurance Locker" Model:**
>
> Imagine you are a homeowner. Before you install a security system, the insurance company first asks you:
>
> 1. **What is the value of your assets inside the house?** (Impact Level: Low / Moderate / High)
> 2. **Based on that value, they offer a "starter package"** (the **Baseline**): maybe a lock and a CCTV for Low; add motion sensors and a guard for Moderate; add biometrics, vault, and 24/7 patrol for High.
> 3. **But your house has a unique feature** — say, a basement with a hidden entry. So you **tailor** the package: add a basement sensor.
> 4. **You document everything** in a security plan, get approval, and install.
>
> This is **exactly** what NIST does with information systems. The *baseline* is the starter kit; *tailoring* is customization to your environment; *documentation* is the System Security Plan (SSP).

### Plain-English Summary

| Term | Plain English Meaning |
|------|----------------------|
| **Baseline** | A pre-packaged set of security controls for Low/Moderate/High systems |
| **Tailoring** | Adjusting the baseline to fit the specific mission and environment |
| **Overlay** | A set of controls tailored for a specific sector/community (e.g., Healthcare) |
| **Allocation** | Assigning controls to specific system components (hardware, software, facilities) |
| **SSP** | The official document that records all selected and tailored controls |

---

## 1.3 NIST Mandatory Standards Referenced

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **NIST publications** govern this topic (no fabrication — all are real and current as of the knowledge cutoff):
>
> 1. **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2** — Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations (RMF)
> 2. **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** — Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
> 3. **NIST SP 800-53B** — Control Baselines for Information Systems and Organizations
> 4. **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** — Guide for Conducting Risk Assessments
> 5. **NIST SP 800-60 Vol. I & II Rev. 1** — Guide for Mapping Types of Information and Information Systems to Security Categories

The three **mandatory impact levels** defined by FIPS 199 are:
- **LOW** — Limited adverse effect
- **MODERATE** — Serious adverse effect
- **HIGH** — Catastrophic or severe adverse effect

Each impact level triggers a corresponding **control baseline** with progressively stronger safeguards.

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment

> [!NOTE]
> This topic primarily maps to the following Course Outcomes (COs) under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme:
> - **CO3:** Apply the NIST RMF to design trustworthy secure systems.
> - **CO4:** Evaluate control baselines and tailor them for enterprise environments.

The cognitive level target is **Apply (Bloom Level 3)** and **Analyze (Bloom Level 4)**, consistent with the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy** adopted by KTU 2024.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Select Step — Detailed Operational Breakdown

The "Select" step (RMF Step 2) consists of **four major tasks**, each with sub-tasks, as defined in NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2.

### Task 1: **Control Selection**
- Identify and select the appropriate **control baseline** (Low / Moderate / High) based on the **system security categorization** done in Step 1 (Categorize).
- Use **FIPS 199** categorization of **CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** for the system.
- Use **NIST SP 800-60** to map information types to CIA impact levels.

### Task 2: **Control Tailoring**
- Modify the baseline to align with the organization's specific mission, environment, and risks.
- Apply **tailoring actions**: **scoping, parameterization, compensating controls, and supplementation**.

### Task 3: **Control Allocation**
- Assign each selected/tailored control to the **specific system element** responsible for its implementation (e.g., hardware, software, facility, personnel, policy).

### Task 4: **Documentation**
- Produce the **System Security Plan (SSP)** — a formal document listing all selected controls, tailoring decisions, allocation, and planned implementation.
- The SSP serves as the authoritative baseline for the **Implement** (Step 3) and **Assess** (Step 4) phases.

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Sub-Step

| Sub-Step | Why It Matters (Engineering Rationale) |
|----------|----------------------------------------|
| **Baseline Selection** | Provides a defensible, standards-based starting point; avoids reinventing security from scratch. |
| **Tailoring** | A flat baseline cannot fit all systems (e.g., SCADA vs. Cloud). Tailoring prevents over- or under-securing. |
| **Allocation** | Controls must be **traceable** to components; without allocation, you cannot assess or monitor. |
| **Documentation (SSP)** | Auditors, AOs (Authorizing Officials), and continuous monitoring depend on a canonical reference. |

---

## 2.3 The 20 Control Families in NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5

> [!NOTE]
> The complete control catalog is organized into **20 control families**, each with a two-character identifier (e.g., **AC** = Access Control, **AU** = Audit and Accountability).

| Family ID | Family Name | Engineering Purpose |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| AC | Access Control | Restrict who can do what |
| AT | Awareness and Training | Educate users and admins |
| AU | Audit and Accountability | Log and trace actions |
| CA | Assessment, Authorization, and Monitoring | Formal certification |
| CM | Configuration Management | Maintain system integrity |
| CP | Contingency Planning | Ensure availability after disaster |
| IA | Identification and Authentication | Verify user identity |
| IR | Incident Response | React to security events |
| MA | Maintenance | Secure system upkeep |
| MP | Media Protection | Protect data at rest |
| PE | Physical and Environmental Protection | Protect the physical layer |
| PL | Planning | Document system security plan |
| PM | Program Management | Organization-wide governance |
| PS | Personnel Security | Vet and manage staff |
| PT | PII Processing and Transparency | Privacy controls |
| RA | Risk Assessment | Identify and evaluate risks |
| SA | System and Services Acquisition | Secure SDLC |
| SC | System and Communications Protection | Network/communications security |
| SI | System and Information Integrity | Detect and correct flaws |
| SR | Supply Chain Risk Management | Vendor/3rd party risk |

---

## 2.4 Tailoring Decisions — The Core Sub-Process

NIST SP 800-53B defines a **tailoring decision matrix**. There are **three possible tailoring outcomes** for each control:

1. **Retain** — Keep the control as-is in the baseline.
2. **Modify** — Adjust using scoping, parameterization, or compensating controls.
3. **Remove** — Eliminate because it does not apply (with justification).

### Tailoring Actions Defined by NIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tailoring is NOT a free-form process.** It must follow four documented actions:

| Tailoring Action | Definition | Example |
|------------------|------------|---------|
| **Scoping** | Decide if a control applies based on system characteristics (e.g., wireless, mobile, cloud). | "SC-28 (Protection of Information at Rest) does not apply because system has no persistent storage." |
| **Parameterization** | Set specific values for control parameters (e.g., password length = 12). | "IA-5(1)(a) — minimum password length = 12 characters." |
| **Compensating Controls** | Substitute alternate control(s) that achieve equivalent protection. | "PE-3 (Physical Access Control) is not feasible at remote site; substituted with encryption + CCTV." |
| **Supplementation** | Add new controls not in the baseline because of unique risks. | "Adding custom IoT-specific control for medical device telemetry." |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The table below contains the **core engineering relationships** you must memorize for the KTU ESE. All values are derived directly from NIST SP 800-53B.

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Value / Standard |
|---------------|------------|------------------|
| $CIA_{system}$ | System security categorization (High Water Mark) | $\max(C, I, A)$ |
| $C$ | Confidentiality impact | $\in \{L, M, H\}$ |
| $I$ | Integrity impact | $\in \{L, M, H\}$ |
| $A$ | Availability impact | $\in \{L, M, H\}$ |
| $N_{controls}^{Low}$ | Number of controls in Low baseline | **≈ 156** |
| $N_{controls}^{Mod}$ | Number of controls in Moderate baseline | **≈ 325** |
| $N_{controls}^{High}$ | Number of controls in High baseline | **≈ 421** |
| $F$ | Number of control families | **20** |
| $T_{tailor}$ | Number of tailoring actions | **4** |
| $\text{RMF Steps}$ | RMF lifecycle phases | **7** (Prepare, Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Water-Mark Rule (FIPS 199):** The system categorization equals the **maximum** of the three CIA impact values. This single value drives baseline selection.

$$\text{System\_Security\_Category} = \max(C, I, A)$$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practice, NIST baseline selection is used by:

- **U.S. Federal Agencies** (mandatory per FISMA / OMB A-130)
- **Defense contractors** (CMMC / RMF for DoD systems)
- **Healthcare** (HIPAA Security Rule maps closely to NIST)
- **Critical infrastructure** (Energy, Finance — via sectoral overlays)
- **Cloud Service Providers** (FedRAMP uses NIST baselines)
- **Auditors** (to verify control coverage and SSP completeness)

> A well-selected baseline reduces audit failures, accelerates ATO (Authority to Operate), and provides measurable cybersecurity posture improvement.

---

## 2.7 Baselines vs. Overlays vs. Profiles

| Concept | Definition | Example |
|---------|------------|---------|
| **Baseline** | Starting set of controls for an impact level (from SP 800-53B) | Moderate Baseline |
| **Overlay** | A sector/community-specific set of tailoring decisions (e.g., DoD, FedRAMP) | DoD Cloud Computing Overlay |
| **Profile** | A tailored baseline for a specific organization's mission | "NIST CSF Profile for XYZ Hospital" |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU may ask you to **distinguish** between these three — they are often confused in exam answers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Procedure: From Categorization to Baseline Selection

The following is the **exhaustive engineering workflow** for moving from Step 1 (Categorize) to Step 2 (Select).

### Step 1 — Determine System Impact Level

Given a system with the following FIPS 199 impact assignments:

$$
C = \text{Moderate}, \quad I = \text{Low}, \quad A = \text{High}
$$

**Apply the High-Water-Mark Rule:**

$$
\text{System\_Security\_Category} = \max(\text{Moderate}, \text{Low}, \text{High})
$$

$$
= \text{High}
$$

**Explanation of conversion logic:**
- The function $\max(\cdot)$ selects the dominant (highest) impact level.
- In this case, "High" > "Moderate" > "Low" on the ordinal scale, so the system is classified as **High-impact**.

---

### Step 2 — Select the Baseline

Using the categorization result, the corresponding baseline from **NIST SP 800-53B** is selected.

$$
B_{system} =
\begin{cases}
B_{Low} & \text{if } \max(C,I,A) = \text{Low} \\
B_{Moderate} & \text{if } \max(C,I,A) = \text{Moderate} \\
B_{High} & \text{if } \max(C,I,A) = \text{High}
\end{cases}
$$

For the example above:

$$
B_{system} = B_{High} \quad (\text{High Baseline})
$$

**Explanation of conversion logic:**
- The categorical lookup function maps the high-water-mark value to the corresponding baseline.
- NIST SP 800-53B defines the **High Baseline** as having **~421 controls**, the largest set.

---

### Step 3 — Tailor the Baseline

Apply each of the four tailoring actions to refine the baseline.

#### 3a. Scoping

$$
B_{tailored} = B_{system} - \{ \text{controls not applicable due to scoping} \}
$$

Example: If the system is a **public-facing read-only web portal**, then control **PE-3 (Physical Access Control)** for the data center may be **scoped out** because the system has no sensitive physical boundary that the agency can control. Each scoped-out control must be documented with justification in the SSP.

#### 3b. Parameterization

Each control may have parameters (assignments) to be filled in.

$$
\forall c \in B_{tailored}: c_{param} \leftarrow \text{org-specific value}
$$

Example: For control **AC-7 (Unsuccessful Logon Attempts)**, NIST's default parameter is "limit = 3." The organization may parameterize to **5** if business requirements demand it.

#### 3c. Compensating Controls

$$
\forall c \in B_{tailored} \text{ where } c \text{ cannot be implemented: } c \leftarrow c_{comp}
$$

The compensating control must provide **equivalent or comparable** protection:

$$
\text{Protection}(c_{comp}) \geq \text{Protection}(c)
$$

Example: Replacing biometric access (PE-3 enhancement) with a **mantrap + dual-person integrity** arrangement.

#### 3d. Supplementation

$$
B_{tailored} \leftarrow B_{tailored} \cup \{ \text{new controls} \}
$$

Example: Adding **custom control** for an **IoT medical device** that is not covered by standard baselines.

---

### Step 4 — Allocate Controls

Each tailored control is allocated to a **system element** $e$:

$$
\text{Alloc}: C \rightarrow E, \quad \text{Alloc}(c) = e
$$

where $C$ is the set of controls and $E$ is the set of system elements (hardware, software, firmware, facilities, personnel, policies/procedures).

Example allocation:

| Control | Allocated To |
|---------|--------------|
| AC-2 (Account Management) | Personnel / Identity Provider |
| SC-7 (Boundary Protection) | Firewall Appliance |
| PE-2 (Physical Access Authorizations) | Facility Security |
| SI-2 (Flaw Remediation) | Patch Management Process |
| IR-4 (Incident Handling) | SOC / IR Team |

---

### Step 5 — Documentation (System Security Plan)

The SSP must include, at minimum:

$$
\text{SSP} = \{ B_{system}, \text{Tailoring\_Justifications}, \text{Allocations}, \text{Implementation\_Plan}, \text{Continuous\_Monitoring\_Strategy} \}
$$

The SSP becomes the **authoritative artifact** for the **Implement** (Step 3) and **Assess** (Step 4) phases.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Complete End-to-End Selection

**Scenario:** A university research system processes student health records. The CIA assessment is:

$$
C = \text{Moderate}, \quad I = \text{Moderate}, \quad A = \text{Low}
$$

**Step 1:** High-Water-Mark

$$
\max(\text{Mod}, \text{Mod}, \text{Low}) = \text{Moderate}
$$

**Step 2:** Baseline Lookup

$$
B_{system} = B_{Moderate}
$$

**Step 3:** Tailoring Decisions

- **Scoping:** Remove **MP-6 (Media Sanitization)** because no removable media is used.
- **Parameterization:** Set **IA-5(1)(b)** password complexity to "8 characters minimum, 2 special."
- **Compensating:** **PE-13 (Fire Protection)** → wet-sprinkler not allowed; substitute with **VESDA + 24/7 monitoring**.
- **Supplementation:** Add custom control for **GDPR Article 32 compliance** (data minimization).

**Step 4:** Allocation

- **AU-2 (Event Logging)** → Application log manager
- **SC-13 (Cryptographic Protection)** → HSM module
- **RA-5 (Vulnerability Monitoring)** → Tenable Nessus scanner

**Step 5:** SSP generated → ready for **Implement** step.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Count Comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{controls}^{Low} &\approx 156 \\
N_{controls}^{Moderate} &\approx 325 \\
N_{controls}^{High} &\approx 421 \\
\text{Ratio } (High:L) &\approx 2.7:1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** The High baseline has roughly **2.7×** more controls than Low. This reflects the **non-linear** increase in security complexity when moving up impact levels.

---

## 3.4 Control Type Differentiation (Rev. 5 Refinement)

NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 introduced **control types** for proper allocation:

| Control Type | Definition | Where Allocated |
|--------------|------------|-----------------|
| **Common** | Inherited from the organization (e.g., PM family) | Enterprise level |
| **System-Specific** | Implemented within the system boundary | System level |
| **Hybrid** | Partially common, partially system-specific | Mixed |

> This allocation affects **assessment scope** — a critical concept for KTU.

---

## 3.5 Decision Tree for Baseline Selection

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input:} \quad & (C, I, A) \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Compute } S = \max(C, I, A) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Map } S \to B_{system} \in \{B_L, B_M, B_H\} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Apply Scoping, Parameterization, Compensating, Supplementation} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Allocate } c \in B_{tailored} \to e \in E \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Generate SSP, hand off to Implement step}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RMF Lifecycle — Position of the Select Step

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Prepare]
    C[Categorize Step 1]
    S[Select Step 2]
    I[Implement Step 3]
    A[Assess Step 4]
    AU[Authorize Step 5]
    M[Monitor Step 6]

    P --> C --> S --> I --> A --> AU --> M
    M -.Continuous Feedback.-> P
    M -.Continuous Feedback.-> S
```

**Caption:** NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2 RMF lifecycle showing the **Select** step (Step 2) between **Categorize** and **Implement**, with continuous feedback from **Monitor**.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Sub-Process: The Select Step

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input: System Categorization from FIPS 199] --> B1[Identify Information Types via SP 800-60]
    B1 --> C1[Determine CIA Impact for Each Type]
    C1 --> D1[Apply High-Water-Mark to Get System Impact]
    D1 --> E1[Look Up Baseline in SP 800-53B]
    E1 --> F1[Select Baseline: Low / Moderate / High]
    F1 --> G1[Apply Tailoring: Scoping]
    G1 --> G2[Apply Tailoring: Parameterization]
    G2 --> G3[Apply Tailoring: Compensating Controls]
    G3 --> G4[Apply Tailoring: Supplementation]
    G4 --> H1[Allocate Controls to System Elements]
    H1 --> I1[Document in System Security Plan SSP]
    I1 --> J1[Authorizing Official Reviews and Approves]
    J1 --> K1[Output: Approved Tailored Baseline]
    K1 --> L1[Hand off to Implement Step 3]
```

**Caption:** The complete sub-process flow inside the **Select** step, from FIPS 199 input to approved tailored baseline output.

---

## 4.3 Tailoring Decision Matrix (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "INPUT"
        BS[Baseline Control]
    end
    subgraph "TAILORING ACTIONS"
        T1[Scoping]
        T2[Parameterization]
        T3[Compensating]
        T4[Supplementation]
    end
    subgraph "POSSIBLE OUTCOMES"
        O1[Retain Control As-Is]
        O2[Modify Control with Justification]
        O3[Remove Control with Justification]
        O4[Add New Control]
    end
    BS --> T1
    BS --> T2
    BS --> T3
    BS --> T4
    T1 --> O1
    T1 --> O2
    T1 --> O3
    T2 --> O2
    T3 --> O2
    T4 --> O4
```

**Caption:** How each baseline control flows through the four tailoring actions and the possible outcomes (retain, modify, remove, add).

---

## 4.4 Baseline-to-Allocation Mapping Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Baseline SP 800-53B"
        B[High or Moderate or Low Baseline]
    end
    subgraph "Tailored Control Set"
        TC[Tailored Controls with Justifications]
    end
    subgraph "System Elements"
        E1[Hardware]
        E2[Software]
        E3[Firmware]
        E4[Facility]
        E5[Personnel]
        E6[Policy Procedure]
    end
    B --> TC
    TC --> E1
    TC --> E2
    TC --> E3
    TC --> E4
    TC --> E5
    TC --> E6
```

**Caption:** Block-level architecture showing how a tailored control set is allocated to the six categories of system elements defined by NIST.

---

## 4.5 NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 Control Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Control Family e.g. AC] --> CL[Control e.g. AC-2]
    CL --> CS[Control Statement]
    CS --> PR[Parameter Assignments]
    CS --> EN[Enhancements e.g. AC-2 1]
    CS --> SR[Supplemental Guidance]
    CS --> RE[Related Controls]
    CS --> RT[References]
```

**Caption:** Internal anatomy of a single NIST control. Each control has a statement, optional parameters, optional enhancements, supplemental guidance, related controls, and references.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Define **security control baseline** as per **NIST SP 800-53B**. List the **three baseline levels** and the number of control families in the catalog.

**Model Answer:**

A **security control baseline** is a pre-defined, minimum set of security controls selected from **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** that represents, for a given impact level, the starting point for securing an information system.

The **three baseline levels** defined in **NIST SP 800-53B** are:

1. **Low-Impact Baseline** — for systems where loss of CIA has limited adverse effect.
2. **Moderate-Impact Baseline** — for systems where loss has serious adverse effect.
3. **High-Impact Baseline** — for systems where loss has catastrophic adverse effect.

The control catalog is organized into **20 control families** (e.g., AC, AU, SC, SI).

**Marks Distribution (per KTU valuation key):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Three baseline levels: 1.5 marks
- Number of families: 0.5 mark

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> List and briefly explain the **four tailoring actions** defined by NIST SP 800-53B.

**Model Answer:**

The four tailoring actions are:

1. **Scoping** — Deciding whether a control applies to the system, based on its characteristics (e.g., system is not wireless, so wireless-specific controls may be scoped out).
2. **Parameterization** — Assigning specific values to control parameters (e.g., setting minimum password length to 12).
3. **Compensating Controls** — Substituting an alternative control that provides equivalent protection when the original cannot be implemented.
4. **Supplementation** — Adding new controls beyond the baseline to address unique risks (e.g., AI-specific or IoT-specific threats).

**Marks Distribution:**
- Listing all 4 correctly: 1.5 marks
- Brief explanation: 1.5 marks

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **"Select" step** of the NIST **Risk Management Framework (RMF)** as defined in **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2**. List all four sub-tasks and the primary outputs of this step.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A system has the following FIPS 199 categorization: **C = Moderate, I = High, A = Low**. Determine the **system security category**, identify the correct baseline, and demonstrate the **tailoring** process with **one example** for each of the four tailoring actions.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**The Select Step (RMF Step 2) — Detailed Explanation:**

The **Select** step is the second phase of the RMF lifecycle defined in **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2**. It transforms the categorization result from Step 1 into a **concrete, justified, and traceable set of controls** that will be implemented, assessed, authorized, and monitored.

**Four Sub-Tasks:**

1. **Control Selection**
   - Selects the appropriate baseline (Low, Moderate, or High) from **NIST SP 800-53B**.
   - Based on the **high-water-mark impact level** determined in Step 1 (Categorize).
   - Uses **NIST SP 800-60** for information-type-to-impact mapping.

2. **Control Tailoring**
   - Applies the four tailoring actions: **scoping, parameterization, compensating controls, and supplementation**.
   - Customizes the baseline to fit the organization's mission, environment, and risk profile.
   - All tailoring decisions must be **justified and documented**.

3. **Control Allocation**
   - Assigns each tailored control to a specific system element (hardware, software, firmware, facilities, personnel, or policy/procedure).
   - Determines whether the control is **common, system-specific, or hybrid** (a Rev. 5 enhancement).

4. **Documentation**
   - Produces the **System Security Plan (SSP)** — the authoritative artifact describing selected controls, tailoring rationale, allocation, and planned implementation.

**Primary Outputs of the Select Step:**
- Tailored control baseline
- System Security Plan (SSP)
- Continuous monitoring strategy (initial draft)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $C = \text{Moderate}, I = \text{High}, A = \text{Low}$

**Step 1 — High-Water-Mark Calculation:**

$$
\text{SSC} = \max(\text{Moderate}, \text{High}, \text{Low}) = \text{High}
$$

**Step 2 — Baseline Lookup:** System is **High-Impact**, so apply $B_{High}$ from NIST SP 800-53B (~421 controls).

**Step 3 — Tailoring Examples (one per action):**

1. **Scoping:** Control **AC-19 (Access Control for Mobile Devices)** is **scoped out** because the system does not include any mobile components.
2. **Parameterization:** Control **IA-5(1)(a)** (Password Length) is parameterized to **minimum 12 characters** (upgraded from baseline default of 8).
3. **Compensating Control:** Control **PE-3 (Physical Access Control)** — biometric readers cannot be installed; substitute with **dual-factor badge + mantrap** providing equivalent protection.
4. **Supplementation:** Add a **custom supply-chain control** for verifying firmware integrity of imported hardware (addresses unique vendor risk not in baseline).

**Step 4 — Allocation Examples:**
- **SC-7 (Boundary Protection)** → Firewall appliance
- **AU-2 (Event Logging)** → SIEM platform
- **SI-2 (Flaw Remediation)** → Patch management process

**Step 5 — Output:** All decisions documented in the SSP and approved by the **Authorizing Official (AO)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **baseline, overlay, and profile** in the context of NIST control selection. Provide one real-world example of each.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **20 control families** of NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 in a structured manner, grouping them under the four RMF control type classifications: **Technical, Operational, Management**, and the new **Privacy** category (added in Rev. 5).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

| Concept | Definition | Real-World Example |
|---------|------------|--------------------|
| **Baseline** | A starting set of controls defined in NIST SP 800-53B for an impact level (Low/Moderate/High). | The Moderate Baseline applied to a federal HR system. |
| **Overlay** | A sector- or community-specific set of tailoring decisions layered onto a baseline. | The **DoD Cloud Computing Overlay** applied to an AWS GovCloud deployment; the **FedRAMP Moderate Overlay**. |
| **Profile** | A tailored baseline reflecting a specific organization's mission, risks, and priorities (popularized by the NIST CSF). | A **hospital's HIPAA-aligned NIST CSF Profile** for protecting electronic health records. |

**Key Distinctions:**
- **Baseline** is generic and published by NIST.
- **Overlay** is sector-specific and may be published by a community of interest.
- **Profile** is organization-specific and reflects the org's unique risk tolerance.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Control Families of NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 (grouped by control type):**

**Technical Controls (4 families):**
- **AC** — Access Control
- **IA** — Identification and Authentication
- **SC** — System and Communications Protection
- **SI** — System and Information Integrity

**Operational Controls (9 families):**
- **AT** — Awareness and Training
- **AU** — Audit and Accountability
- **CM** — Configuration Management
- **CP** — Contingency Planning
- **IR** — Incident Response
- **MA** — Maintenance
- **MP** — Media Protection
- **PE** — Physical and Environmental Protection
- **PS** — Personnel Security

**Management Controls (6 families):**
- **CA** — Assessment, Authorization, and Monitoring
- **PL** — Planning
- **PM** — Program Management
- **RA** — Risk Assessment
- **SA** — System and Services Acquisition
- **SR** — Supply Chain Risk Management

**Privacy Controls (1 family — added in Rev. 5):**
- **PT** — PII Processing and Transparency

> [!NOTE]
> **Total = 4 + 9 + 6 + 1 = 20 families** ✓

**Count Verification:**

$$
N_{families}^{Total} = 4 + 9 + 6 + 1 = 20
$$

This matches the canonical NIST count.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing baseline with overlay or profile** — Examiners deduct 2–3 marks when students use these terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Skipping the high-water-mark calculation** — A 14-mark question often requires *showing* the $\max(C,I,A)$ formula. Writing only the answer without derivation loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Listing fewer than 4 tailoring actions** — All four (Scoping, Parameterization, Compensating, Supplementation) must be named and exemplified.
> 4. **Forgetting the SSP** — The System Security Plan is the **mandatory output** of the Select step. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up control type classification** — Rev. 5 explicitly groups families into Technical, Operational, Management, and Privacy. Using the older 3-category-only scheme (e.g., Pre-Rev. 5) costs marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Select Step in NIST RMF**

- The **Select step is RMF Step 2**, between Categorize (Step 1) and Implement (Step 3).
- Governed by **NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2** (process) and **NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5** (catalog), with baselines in **NIST SP 800-53B**.
- Categorization uses **FIPS 199** (CIA), mapping via **NIST SP 800-60**.
- **High-Water-Mark Rule:** $\text{SSC} = \max(C, I, A)$ — this single value drives baseline selection.
- **Three baselines:** Low (~156 controls), Moderate (~325), High (~421).
- The control catalog has **20 families** and **1,000+ controls** in Rev. 5.
- **Four tailoring actions:** Scoping, Parameterization, Compensating, Supplementation.
- Tailoring outcomes: Retain, Modify, Remove (with justification); Supplementation adds new controls.
- **Allocation** maps each control to one of: Hardware, Software, Firmware, Facility, Personnel, Policy.
- Rev. 5 control types: **Common, System-Specific, Hybrid** — affects assessment scope.
- **Mandatory output:** System Security Plan (SSP).
- **Do not confuse** Baseline vs. Overlay vs. Profile.
- Privacy is a **first-class citizen** in Rev. 5 (PT family).
- Continuous monitoring (Step 6) **feeds back** into the Select step whenever risks change.
- The Select step is a **defensible, auditable, traceable** engineering process — not ad-hoc.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customize and Create Overlays of Security Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Customize and Create Overlays of Security Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Customize and Create Overlays of Security Controls

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (Aligned to KTU 2024 / NIST SP 800-53 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Control (NIST SP 800-53):** A *safeguard or countermeasure* prescribed for an information system or an organization to protect the **confidentiality (C)**, **integrity (I)**, and **availability (A)** of the system and its information.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is rooted in the **NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF)** and **NIST SP 800-53 Revision 5** — *Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations*. Two key engineering activities are defined:

| Term | Rigorous Academic Definition |
|---|---|
| **Tailoring (Customization)** | The process by which the security control baseline is modified to align with the organization's *mission, business functions, risks, technologies, and operational environment*. |
| **Overlay** | A *fully specified set of security controls, control enhancements, and supplemental guidance* derived from applying tailoring guidance to a specific *community of interest, technology, sector, or operational context*. |

> [!NOTE]
> **A Tailoring Operation** is the atomic unit of customization. NIST recognizes four canonical tailoring operations: **Scoping, Compensating, Parameter Assignment, and Supplementing.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Recipe Book

Imagine a **master recipe book** for a culinary school — it contains thousands of standard recipes (this is the **NIST SP 800-53 catalog**).

* The **baseline controls** are the *standard house recipes* — three pre-set menus labeled "Simple", "Medium", and "Complex" (these are the **Low, Moderate, and High baselines**).
* **Customization (Tailoring)** is when a *specific restaurant* picks the "Medium" menu but removes the spicy chili pepper because its customers are children, and substitutes a milder paprika (this is **scoping** + **parameter assignment**).
* An **Overlay** is a *specialized cuisine cookbook* — say, a "Japanese Cuisine Cookbook" — that takes the master recipes, modifies them, removes soy allergens, adds chopstick etiquette rules, and adds raw-fish safety checks. **Anyone operating a Japanese restaurant in the school can adopt this overlay wholesale.**

> **Key Insight:** An overlay is essentially a *reusable, community-specific tailoring* that another organization can adopt in entirety, saving it the work of re-customizing the baseline from scratch.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants to Remember

* **FIPS 199 Impact Levels:** **Low**, **Moderate**, **High** — applied independently to **C, I, A**.
* **Security Categorization Formula (FIPS 199):** $SC_{\text{system}} = \{(C, I, A)_{\max}\}$.
* **Tailoring Categories (per NIST SP 800-53B):** **Common Controls, System-Specific Controls, Hybrid Controls**.
* **Control Family Identifier Format:** `[Family]-[Number]` (e.g., **AC-2**, **AU-6**, **SC-7**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF) Context

Customization and overlays are executed primarily in **RMF Step 7 (Implement Controls)** and refined continuously in **RMF Step 8 (Assess Controls)** and **Step 9 (Authorize Information System)**. The tailoring lifecycle is an iterative closed-loop:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU-Mandated Tailoring Equation:**
> $$\text{Final Control Set} = \text{Baseline} - \text{Scoped Out} + \text{Compensating} + \text{Supplements} + \text{Parameterized}$$

Every control in the final set must be traceable to one of these five operations. If a control cannot be justified by an operation, it is *non-compliant* per NIST methodology.

## 2.2 The Four Canonical Tailoring Operations

### Operation 1 — **Scoping (Elimination)**
Removes parts of a baseline control that do not apply to the system.

> **Example:** If the system has no wireless components, the wireless-specific portions of **AC-18 (Wireless Access)** can be scoped out.

### Operation 2 — **Compensating Control Substitution**
Replaces a baseline control with an *alternative* that provides equivalent or greater protection.

* The substitute must address the **same threat** or satisfy the **same intent**.
* Must be documented with a **formal risk acceptance** by the Authorizing Official (AO).

### Operation 3 — **Parameter Assignment**
Replaces a placeholder value in a control statement with an *organization-defined value*.

> **Example:** Control **AC-2(1)** says *"automated audit mechanisms"*. The organization **assigns the parameter** to *"Splunk Enterprise 9.x"*.

### Operation 4 — **Supplementation (Addition)**
Adds **new controls or enhancements** not present in the baseline when the baseline is insufficient for the system's risk profile.

> **Example:** Adding **SC-30(5) — Concealment and Misdirection** to a high-value e-commerce site that is not in the standard High baseline.

## 2.3 What Exactly is an Overlay? (Engineering Perspective)

An overlay is a **reusable tailoring specification**. It binds together:

1. A **scope statement** — defines who the overlay applies to (sector, technology, community).
2. A **set of tailoring decisions** — which baseline elements to keep, scope, substitute, parameterize, or supplement.
3. **Supplemental guidance** — explains the *why* behind each decision.
4. **Mappings** — links to external standards (HIPAA, FedRAMP, PCI-DSS, ISO 27001).

> **Public Examples of Real Overlays:**
> * **NIST SP 800-53B Appendix F** — Industrial Control Systems (ICS) Overlay
> * **NIST SP 800-66 Rev 2** — HIPAA Security Rule Overlay
> * **DOD Cloud Computing Security Requirements Guide (CC SRG)** — Cloud Overlay
> * **FedRAMP baselines** — Cloud Service Provider Overlay

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet (No-Pipe Markdown Table)

| Concept | Notation / Formula | Boundary / Threshold | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Security Categorization | $SC = \{(C, I, A)_{\max}\}$ | Each axis independently $\in \lbrace \text{L, M, H} \rbrace$ | Categorical (no units) |
| Risk Magnitude | $R = T \times V \times I$ | $R \in \lbrace \text{L, M, H} \rbrace$ | Logarithmic, qualitative |
| Baseline Selection Rule | $B = \max(C, I, A)$ | Take the *highest* of the three axes | "High Water Mark" |
| Tailoring Completeness | $T_{\text{complete}} = \frac{N_{\text{justified}}}{N_{\text{total controls}}}$ | Must equal **1.0 (100%)** before authorization | Dimensionless ratio |
| Control Family Count | **20 families** in SP 800-53 Rev 5 | AC, AT, AU, CM, CP, IA, IR, MA, MP, PE, PL, PM, PS, PT, RA, SA, SC, SI, SR, PM | Identified by 2-letter code |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Healthcare Sector** — HIPAA overlay ensures *PHI confidentiality* controls (e.g., encryption at rest via **SC-28**) are mandatory.
* **Cloud Computing (FedRAMP)** — Overlay enforces *tenant isolation* via **SC-7(11)** and continuous monitoring via **CA-7**.
* **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)** — Overlay relaxes *accountability controls* (because shared logins are operationally required) and tightens *physical and safety controls* (via **PE** family).
* **IoT Devices** — Overlay tightens *supply chain* (SR family) and *firmware integrity* (SI-7) due to embedded constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The topic is *methodological* rather than purely numerical, so the "derivations" below are **explicit algorithmic walkthroughs** of the NIST overlay construction pipeline. Every step is shown without abbreviation.

## 3.1 The NIST SP 800-53 Overlay Construction Pipeline — Exhaustive Derivation

### **Step 1: Identify the Community of Interest**

The first step is to clearly define the *applicability boundary* of the overlay. This requires answering three fundamental questions:

1. *Who* will use this overlay? (e.g., healthcare providers, cloud customers, ICS operators)
2. *What* technology, sector, or mission is in scope?
