| 6 | **Determine, Analyze, and Prioritize Gaps** | Cybersecurity Action Plan with priorities and resource estimates |
| 7 | **Implement the Action Plan** | Executed controls, continuous monitoring, feedback into Step 3 |

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Step 1: Scope]  →  [Step 2: Orient]  →  [Step 3: Current Profile]
                                                        ↓
[Step 7: Implement]  ←  [Step 6: Gap Analysis]  ←  [Step 5: Target Profile]
       ↑                                                       ↑
       └────────────── Continuous Feedback ──────────────────────┘
                                            ↑
                                  [Step 4: Risk Assessment feeds Steps 5 & 6]
```

**Key exam emphasis (1 mark):** The process is **iterative and cyclical** — the output of Step 7 re-feeds Step 3, enabling **continuous improvement** in line with the evolving threat landscape.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] 
> **Common mistakes KTU students make in CSF questions (and where marks are lost):**
> 
> 1. **Treating CSF as a checklist or certification** — It is a *risk management lifecycle*, not a compliance audit. Students who write *"Step 1: comply with control X"* lose framework-understanding marks.
> 2. **Confusing Tiers with Profiles** — Tiers are *maturity levels*; Profiles are *as-is/to-be snapshots*. Examiners penalize the swap.
> 3. **Forgetting GOVERN** — Many students answer with only the old five functions (ID, PR, DE, RS, RC). Since the KTU 2024 syllabus references CSF 2.0, **omitting GOVERN costs 1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Using outdated "Identify → Protect → Detect → Respond → Recover" without noting the GOVERN-centric architecture of CSF 2.0** is a common error.
> 5. **Conflating CSF with ISO 27001** — CSF is *framework*; ISO 27001 is *certifiable management system*. Examiners check for clarity on this distinction.
> 6. **Writing profiles as "Tier 1, 2, 3, 4"** — Profiles and Tiers are different things. Profiles describe *which* subcategories are achieved; Tiers describe *how* the program is managed.
> 7. **Skipping risk formula** — When asked about prioritization, students often write vague terms. Use the explicit relationship $R = L \times I$ to anchor the answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **High-density revision list for last-day KTU prep:**

- ✅ CSF = **NIST Cybersecurity Framework**, currently at **v2.0 (Feb 2024)**.
- ✅ Three components: **Core**, **Tiers**, **Profiles**.
- ✅ Six Functions: **GOVERN (GV), IDENTIFY (ID), PROTECT (PR), DETECT (DE), RESPOND (RS), RECOVER (RC)** — acronym **G-I-P-D-R-R**.
- ✅ **GOVERN is central** in CSF 2.0; provides oversight, policy, roles, supply chain risk management.
- ✅ Six sub-elements of GOVERN: GV.OC, GV.RM, GV.RR, GV.PO, GV.OV, GV.SC.
- ✅ Tiers = maturity levels (1: Partial → 4: Adaptive); **descriptive, not prescriptive**.
- ✅ Profiles = Current vs. Target; **gap** = Target − Current.
- ✅ CSF is **voluntary, risk-based, prioritized, technology-agnostic**.
- ✅ Seven-step application: **Scope → Orient → Current Profile → Risk Assessment → Target Profile → Gap → Implement → (loop back)**.
- ✅ Risk equation: $R = L \times I$ (Likelihood × Impact).
- ✅ CSF **complements** ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, COBIT 2019 — does not replace them.
- ✅ CSF is **NOT a checklist** — examiners penalize this misconception.
- ✅ Real-world adopters: USA critical infrastructure, banks, hospitals, power grids, manufacturing (OT/IT), 5G/6G telecom.
- ✅ Each Function has **Categories**, each Category has **Subcategories** — learn the 2–3 most cited ones per function.
- ✅ Action Priority: prioritize by **residual risk / cost**.
- ✅ CSF supports **continuous improvement**, not one-time implementation.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Overview and Application of the Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigate the Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Navigate the Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)** refers to a structured, risk-based, voluntary compilation of guidelines, standards, and best practices that organizations across various sectors can use to manage and mitigate cybersecurity risks. The most widely adopted CSF is the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (NIST CSF)**, originally developed by the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) under Executive Order 13636 in **2014**, and updated to **NIST CSF 2.0** in **2024**.

The **NIST CSF 2.0** provides a common language and systematic methodology for understanding, managing, expressing, and assessing cybersecurity risk. It is **industry-agnostic**, **technology-agnostic**, and designed to be customized to an organization's specific risk profile, size, and complexity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECCT754 Module 3):** The CSF is studied under the broader umbrella of *Engineering Trustworthy Systems*, where it serves as the **blueprint for building, evaluating, and continuously improving** the security posture of software-intensive and enterprise systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are planning a **long road trip across India** from Kerala to Kashmir. Before you start:

- You check the **route map** (where you are vs. where you want to be).
- You service the **car** (engine, brakes, tires) to prevent breakdowns.
- You install a **GPS tracker and dashcam** so you know if something goes wrong on the way.
- You keep a **first-aid kit and emergency numbers** ready to respond to accidents.
- You have a **backup plan and tow service** to recover if the car breaks down.

The **Cybersecurity Framework works exactly like this travel plan** for an organization's digital assets. Instead of roads, you are navigating **networks, data, applications, and users**. The framework gives you five clear "checkpoints" so you never lose sight of security, no matter how complex the journey (system) becomes.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line takeaway for exam:** *CSF = A voluntary, risk-based roadmap to identify, protect against, detect, respond to, and recover from cyber threats.*

## 1.3 The Six Core Components of NIST CSF 2.0

| # | Component | Purpose |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Govern (NEW in 2.0)** | Establish and monitor the organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy, expectations, and policies. |
| 2 | **Identify** | Develop an organizational understanding of systems, assets, data, and risks. |
| 3 | **Protect** | Implement appropriate safeguards to ensure delivery of critical services. |
| 4 | **Detect** | Define and implement activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event. |
| 5 | **Respond** | Take action regarding a detected cybersecurity incident. |
| 6 | **Recover** | Maintain plans for resilience and restore capabilities/services impaired due to a cybersecurity incident. |

> [!NOTE]
> The acronym **"G-I-P-D-R-R"** (Govern added in 2.0) is the most frequently tested memory aid in KTU exams. The original CSF 1.1 had only five functions: **IDPRR**.

## 1.4 Key Framework Terminology

- **Tiers** — Describe the degree to which an organization's cybersecurity risk management practices exhibit the characteristics defined in the framework (Partial → Risk-Informed → Repeatable → Adaptive).
- **Profiles** — A customized alignment of the framework's functions, categories, and subcategories with the business requirements, risk tolerance, and resources of the organization.
- **Current Profile** — Snapshot of existing cybersecurity outcomes.
- **Target Profile** — Desired state of cybersecurity outcomes.
- **Gap Analysis** — Comparison of *Current Profile* vs. *Target Profile* to identify priorities.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five (now Six) Functions — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 GOVERN (GV) — *NEW in CSF 2.0*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Govern** is the *newest* function introduced in **NIST CSF 2.0 (February 2024)**. It addresses the fact that cybersecurity is fundamentally a governance, risk, and compliance (GRC) issue — not just a technical one.

**Categories under Govern:**
- Organizational Context (GV.OC)
- Risk Management Strategy (GV.RM)
- Roles, Responsibilities, and Authorities (GV.RR)
- Policies, Processes, and Procedures (GV.PO)
- Oversight (GV.OV)
- Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management (GV.SC)

### 2.1.2 IDENTIFY (ID)

**Goal:** Build an *inventory* of what you have and what could go wrong.

**Categories:**
- Asset Management (ID.AM)
- Risk Assessment (ID.RA)
- Improvement (ID.IM)
- *Business Environment (ID.BE) — in older versions*

**Sample Subcategories:**
- ID.AM-1: Physical devices and systems within the organization are inventoried.
- ID.AM-2: Software platforms and applications within the organization are inventoried.
- ID.RA-1: Asset vulnerabilities are identified, validated, and recorded.

### 2.1.3 PROTECT (PR)

**Goal:** Implement *safeguards* to prevent or limit the impact of a cyber event.

**Categories:**
- Identity Management, Authentication, and Access Control (PR.AA)
- Awareness and Training (PR.AT)
- Data Security (PR.DS)
- Platform Security (PR.PS)
- Technology Infrastructure Resilience (PR.IR)

### 2.1.4 DETECT (DE)

**Goal:** Define and implement activities to *identify* the occurrence of a cybersecurity event **in a timely manner**.

**Categories:**
- Continuous Monitoring (DE.CM)
- Adverse Event Analysis (DE.AE)
- *Anomalies and Events (DE.AE)*

### 2.1.5 RESPOND (RS)

**Goal:** Take action regarding a detected cybersecurity incident. **Contain the impact**.

**Categories:**
- Incident Management (RS.MA)
- Incident Analysis (RS.AN)
- Response Communications (RS.CO)
- Mitigation (RS.MI)

### 2.1.6 RECOVER (RC)

**Goal:** Restore capabilities and services that were impaired due to a cybersecurity incident. **Maintain resilience**.

**Categories:**
- Incident Recovery Plan Execution (RC.RP)
- Incident Recovery Communication (RC.CO)

## 2.2 Implementation Tiers (Maturity Levels)

NIST CSF defines **4 Tiers** to describe the sophistication of an organization's risk management practices.

| Tier | Name | Description | KTU Mnemonic |
|------|------|-------------|--------------|
| **Tier 1** | **Partial** | Ad hoc, reactive, no organizational awareness of risk. | "**P**assive" |
| **Tier 2** | **Risk-Informed** | Risk management is approved by management but not enterprise-wide. | "**R**isk-aware" |
| **Tier 3** | **Repeatable** | Risk management is formally approved and expressed as policy. | "**R**outine" |
| **Tier 4** | **Adaptive** | Organization adapts its practices based on lessons learned and predictive indicators. | "**A**gile" |

> [!NOTE]
> Tiers are **descriptive** (not prescriptive) — they describe *current state*, not a required target. The organization decides which tier matches its risk tolerance.

## 2.3 Framework Profile Equation (KTU High-Yield)

The **Framework Profile** is the cornerstone of CSF navigation. It is expressed as:

$$
\text{Profile} = f(\text{Current State}, \text{Target State}, \text{Risk Tolerance}, \text{Resource Constraints})
$$

Formally:

$$
P_{\text{target}} = P_{\text{current}} + \Delta_{\text{priorities}}
$$

Where:

- $P_{\text{current}}$ = Subcategories currently implemented.
- $P_{\text{target}}$ = Subcategories the organization aims to implement.
- $\Delta_{\text{priorities}}$ = The **gap**, expressed as a roadmap of prioritized improvements.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Notation | Definition | Exam Use |
|---------|-------------------|------------|----------|
| Function | $F_i$ | One of six high-level outcomes (GV, ID, PR, DE, RS, RC). | Naming & explaining. |
| Category | $C_{i,j}$ | Subdivision of a function (e.g., ID.AM). | Mapping controls. |
| Subcategory | $S_{i,j,k}$ | Specific outcome (e.g., ID.AM-1). | Gap analysis. |
| Tier | $T_n, \; n \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ | Maturity level of risk management. | Scoring maturity. |
| Profile | $P$ | Alignment of function outcomes with business needs. | Roadmap creation. |
| Gap | $\Delta$ | $P_{\text{target}} - P_{\text{current}}$ | Prioritization. |
| Risk | $R$ | $R = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Risk analysis. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **Categories** (e.g., ID.AM) with **Subcategories** (e.g., ID.AM-1). The notation differs: categories use *letters*; subcategories use *numbers*.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the CSF

- **Critical Infrastructure**: Used by U.S. power grids, water systems, and financial sectors.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA alignment)**: Hospitals use CSF to comply with regulatory audits.
- **Banking (PCI-DSS mapping)**: Maps to payment security standards.
- **Software Engineering**: Guides the integration of security in **SDLC** (Secure SDLC, DevSecOps).
- **Supply Chain Risk Management (SCRM)**: Addresses third-party vendor risk post-SolarWinds attack.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the CSF Navigation Workflow (Step-by-Step)

Navigating the CSF is a **seven-step iterative process**. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation:

### Step 1: **Prioritize and Scope**
The organization first **defines the boundaries** of the cybersecurity initiative. This is analogous to setting the *search domain* in an optimization problem.

$$
\text{Scope} = \{\text{Systems}, \text{Assets}, \text{Business Units}\} \subseteq \text{Organization}
$$

**Logic:** Without a defined scope, the framework cannot be applied meaningfully. The scope may be the entire enterprise, a specific business unit, or a single critical system.

### Step 2: **Orient**
The organization identifies *related systems*, *assets*, *regulations*, and *risk threats* relevant to the scope.

$$
\text{Orientation Set} = \{S_{\text{related}}, A_{\text{relevant}}, R_{\text{regulatory}}, T_{\text{threats}}\}
$$

**Logic:** This step collects the inputs (similar to gathering boundary conditions) required for the next steps.

### Step 3: **Create a Current Profile**
Map the existing cybersecurity outcomes to the relevant subcategories.

$$
P_{\text{current}} = \{S_{i,j,k} \mid \text{Implemented subcategory}\}
$$

**Logic:** This is a *baseline* — a snapshot of "where we are today".

### Step 4: **Conduct a Risk Assessment**
For each gap, evaluate the **likelihood** $L$ and **impact** $I$.

$$
R = L \times I, \quad L \in [0,1], \quad I \in [0,1]
$$

Resulting risk values are categorized:

$$
\text{Risk Level} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Low} & \text{if } R \in [0, 0.2] \\
\text{Medium} & \text{if } R \in (0.2, 0.6] \\
\text{High} & \text{if } R \in (0.6, 1.0]
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 5: **Create a Target Profile**
The organization defines its **desired** state aligned with business goals, risk tolerance, and compliance.

$$
P_{\text{target}} = \{S_{i,j,k} \mid \text{Desired subcategory}\}
$$

### Step 6: **Determine, Analyze, and Prioritize Gaps**
Compare current vs. target:

$$
\Delta = P_{\text{target}} \setminus P_{\text{current}}
$$

Each gap is then weighted by risk:

$$
\text{Priority Score}_i = R_i \times W_i
$$

Where $W_i$ is a business weighting factor (cost, regulatory impact, etc.).

### Step 7: **Implement the Action Plan**
The organization executes actions to close the highest-priority gaps, then **returns to Step 1** to re-evaluate in a continuous improvement loop:

$$
P_{\text{current}}^{(n+1)} = P_{\text{current}}^{(n)} + \bigcup_{i} \text{Actions}_i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CSF is **NOT a one-time exercise**. It is a **continuous feedback loop** — a *Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)* cycle applied to cybersecurity.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Function ↔ Purpose ↔ Key Question

| Function | Core Verb | Key Question Answered | Failure Mode if Missing |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| **Govern** | Establish | "Who is accountable, and what is our policy?" | Inconsistent security across the org. |
| **Identify** | Inventory | "What do we have, and what are the risks?" | Unknown attack surface. |
| **Protect** | Safeguard | "What safeguards keep attacks from succeeding?" | Successful breaches go unnoticed until late. |
| **Detect** | Discover | "How do we know when an attack occurs?" | Dwell time of attackers increases. |
| **Respond** | Contain | "What do we do once an attack is detected?" | Incident escalates uncontrollably. |
| **Recover** | Restore | "How do we get back to normal?" | Prolonged downtime, lost customer trust. |

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Scenario:** A B.Tech student runs a small web-hosting startup in Kerala with 5 servers and 200 customers. A ransomware attack encrypts customer data.

**Step-by-step CSF Navigation:**

1. **Scope:** Web hosting infrastructure.
2. **Orient:** Threats = ransomware, DDoS, phishing; Regulations = IT Act 2000 (India), DPDP Act 2023.
3. **Current Profile (excerpt):**
   - ID.AM-1 (Inventory of devices) — **Implemented**.
   - PR.PS-1 (Configuration management) — **Not implemented**.
4. **Risk Assessment:**
   - Likelihood of ransomware re-attack = 0.7.
   - Impact = 0.9.
   - $R = 0.7 \times 0.9 = 0.63 \rightarrow$ **High**.
5. **Target Profile:** Add PR.PS-1, add DE.CM (continuous monitoring).
6. **Gap:** $\Delta = \{\text{PR.PS-1}, \text{DE.CM-1}, \text{DE.CM-3}\}$.
7. **Action Plan:**
   - Deploy automated configuration management (Ansible/Puppet — for IT roles).
   - Enable SIEM (Security Information & Event Management).
   - Train staff on phishing awareness.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam tip:** When a KTU question gives a *scenario*, walk the examiner through these **7 steps explicitly** in your answer — this guarantees full marks under the structured marking scheme.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NIST CSF 2.0 Core Functions — Wheel Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GOV["GOVERN\nGV - Strategy, Policy, Roles"]
    ID["IDENTIFY\nID - Asset and Risk Inventory"]
    PR["PROTECT\nPR - Safeguards and Access Control"]
    DE["DETECT\nDE - Continuous Monitoring"]
    RS["RESPOND\nRS - Incident Containment"]
    RC["RECOVER\nRC - Restoration and Resilience"]

    GOV --> ID
    ID --> PR
    PR --> DE
    DE --> RS
    RS --> RC
    RC -.Feedback Loop.-> GOV
    GOV -.Oversight.-> PR
    GOV -.Oversight.-> DE
    GOV -.Oversight.-> RS
    GOV -.Oversight.-> RC
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Govern** function is the *center* of the wheel in CSF 2.0, overseeing all other functions. The dotted lines represent continuous governance feedback.

## 4.2 CSF Navigation Workflow (7 Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Prioritize and Scope"] --> B["Step 2: Orient"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Create Current Profile"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Conduct Risk Assessment"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Create Target Profile"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Determine, Analyze, Prioritize Gaps"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Implement Action Plan"]
    G --> A
```

## 4.3 Framework Tiers (Maturity Ladder)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1["Tier 1\nPartial\nAd-hoc, Reactive"]
    T2["Tier 2\nRisk-Informed\nManager-approved"]
    T3["Tier 3\nRepeatable\nPolicy-defined"]
    T4["Tier 4\nAdaptive\nPredictive, Continuous"]

    T1 -->|Mature| T2
    T2 -->|Mature| T3
    T3 -->|Mature| T4
```

## 4.4 Profile Mapping (Current vs. Target)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CurrentProfile["CURRENT PROFILE - Where We Are"]
        S1["ID.AM-1: Implemented"]
        S2["PR.AA-1: Implemented"]
        S3["DE.CM-1: NOT Implemented"]
        S4["RS.MA-1: Partially Implemented"]
    end

    subgraph TargetProfile["TARGET PROFILE - Where We Want to Be"]
        T1a["ID.AM-1: Maintained"]
        T2a["PR.AA-1: Enhanced"]
        T3a["DE.CM-1: Must Implement"]
        T4a["RS.MA-1: Full Implementation"]
    end

    S1 -.Gap Zero.-> T1a
    S2 -.Small Gap.-> T2a
    S3 -.Large Gap.-> T3a
    S4 -.Medium Gap.-> T4a
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU):** When asked to "illustrate CSF navigation", draw the **wheel + the 7-step loop + the tier ladder**. This is the gold-standard diagram for full marks in design/illustration questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) and list its six core functions as per NIST CSF 2.0.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)** is a voluntary, risk-based set of guidelines, standards, and best practices developed by **NIST** to help organizations manage and reduce cybersecurity risk. It provides a common language for understanding, managing, and communicating cybersecurity risk.

The **six core functions** of NIST CSF 2.0 are:

1. **Govern (GV):** Establish and monitor risk management strategy, policies, and roles.
2. **Identify (ID):** Develop an understanding of systems, assets, data, and risks.
3. **Protect (PR):** Implement safeguards to ensure delivery of critical services.
4. **Detect (DE):** Identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event.
5. **Respond (RS):** Take action to contain the impact of a detected incident.
6. **Recover (RC):** Restore capabilities and services impaired by an incident.

---

### **Question 2**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the Current Profile and Target Profile in the NIST CSF.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Current Profile | Target Profile |
|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Definition** | Snapshot of *existing* cybersecurity outcomes achieved by the organization. | Desired *future* state of cybersecurity outcomes aligned with business goals. |
| **Purpose** | Baseline for comparison. | Goal-state for roadmap planning. |
| **Basis** | Subcategories currently implemented. | Subcategories that should be implemented based on risk tolerance. |
| **Use** | Identifies gaps when compared to Target Profile. | Drives prioritization and resource allocation. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **ONE full question** from a choice of two. Each question is 14 marks, typically split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the seven-step process of navigating the NIST Cybersecurity Framework with a suitable diagram.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The seven-step process of navigating the CSF is as follows:

1. **Prioritize and Scope:** Define the boundaries of the cybersecurity initiative — which systems, assets, and business units are in scope.
2. **Orient:** Identify related systems, regulatory requirements, and threat sources relevant to the scope.
3. **Create a Current Profile:** Map existing cybersecurity outcomes to the relevant subcategories in the framework.
4. **Conduct a Risk Assessment:** Evaluate threats, vulnerabilities, likelihoods, and impacts to derive the risk level.
5. **Create a Target Profile:** Define the desired state of cybersecurity outcomes aligned with business goals and risk tolerance.
6. **Determine, Analyze, and Prioritize Gaps:** Compare the current and target profiles; rank gaps by risk and business priority.
7. **Implement the Action Plan:** Execute the prioritized actions, then loop back to Step 1 for continuous improvement.

*(+3 marks for the diagram showing the 7-step iterative loop.)*

> Full marks = 7: 1 mark per step (6 steps fully explained) + 1 mark for the diagram.

---

**(b) Describe the four Implementation Tiers of the NIST CSF. How do they help in assessing an organization's cybersecurity maturity?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four Implementation Tiers describe the degree to which an organization's cybersecurity risk management practices exhibit the characteristics defined in the framework. They are:

1. **Tier 1 — Partial:** Cybersecurity risk management is *ad hoc* and reactive. The organization lacks awareness of risk and has no formal processes.
2. **Tier 2 — Risk-Informed:** Risk management practices are approved by management but are *not* established as organization-wide policy. Awareness exists but is limited.
3. **Tier 3 — Repeatable:** The organization's risk management practices are *formally approved* and expressed as policy. They are reviewed and updated regularly.
4. **Tier 4 — Adaptive:** The organization *adapts* its cybersecurity practices based on lessons learned, predictive indicators, and continuous improvement.

**Role in Maturity Assessment:**
- Provide a **structured vocabulary** to describe an organization's posture.
- Help **benchmark** current vs. desired maturity.
- Enable **gap analysis** between tiers.
- Support **roadmap planning** for moving from lower to higher tiers.
- Facilitate **communication** with stakeholders, regulators, and partners.

> Full marks = 7: 1 mark per tier description (4) + 3 marks for maturity assessment role.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the six core functions of NIST CSF 2.0. Why was the 'Govern' function added in version 2.0?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*(Diagram: The CSF Core Functions Wheel — see Section 4.1 above.)*

**Explanation of the six functions:**

1. **Govern:** Central function that establishes the organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy, policies, roles, and supply chain risk governance.
2. **Identify:** Develops an organizational understanding of systems, people, assets, data, and capabilities to manage cybersecurity risk.
3. **Protect:** Implements appropriate safeguards (access control, training, encryption, secure configuration) to ensure delivery of critical services.
4. **Detect:** Defines activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event through continuous monitoring and anomaly detection.
5. **Respond:** Includes actions to take regarding a detected cybersecurity incident — analysis, mitigation, communication, and improvement.
6. **Recover:** Maintains plans for resilience and restores capabilities/services impaired due to a cybersecurity incident.

**Why 'Govern' was added in CSF 2.0:**
- Earlier versions (1.0, 1.1) focused predominantly on **technical** controls, leaving governance implicit.
- Modern threats (e.g., **SolarWinds supply chain attack**, **Colonial Pipeline ransomware**) revealed that *governance failures* (lack of accountability, weak policy, poor oversight) are root causes of major breaches.
- The Govern function makes **cybersecurity risk a board-level and executive concern**, not just an IT concern.
- It addresses **supply chain risk management (SCRM)**, **roles & responsibilities**, and **oversight** — gaps in 1.1.
- It aligns CSF with modern **GRC (Governance, Risk, Compliance)** standards like ISO 27001 and NIST SP 800-39.

> Full marks = 7: 4 marks for diagram and function explanation, 3 marks for Govern rationale.

---

**(b) A mid-sized IT company in Kerala stores customer payment data. They have suffered a ransomware attack. Using the NIST CSF, propose a step-by-step recovery plan covering Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover functions.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Function | Action |
|----------|--------|
| **Identify** | (1) Inventory all affected servers and data stores. (2) Document the scope of the ransomware infection. (3) Identify which customer records were encrypted. |
| **Protect** | (1) Isolate infected systems from the network immediately. (2) Apply the latest security patches. (3) Enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA) on all admin accounts. (4) Implement offline, immutable backups. |
| **Detect** | (1) Deploy Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) tools. (2) Enable Security Information and Event Management (SIEM). (3) Set alerts for unusual file encryption patterns. |
| **Respond** | (1) Activate the Incident Response Team (IRT). (2) Notify the **CERT-In** (Indian Computer Emergency Response Team) within 6 hours as mandated. (3) Inform affected customers per **DPDP Act 2023**. (4) Conduct forensic analysis to identify the attack vector. |
| **Recover** | (1) Restore systems from clean backups. (2) Validate integrity of restored data. (3) Conduct post-incident review. (4) Update the Incident Response Plan (IRP) with lessons learned. (5) Communicate recovery timeline to stakeholders. |

> Full marks = 7: 1 mark per function applied (5) + 2 marks for contextual relevance (Kerala, DPDP Act, CERT-In, payment data).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing *only* the function names (Govern, Identify…) without explaining *how* each applies to the scenario → **lose up to 50% marks**.
> - **Mistake 2:** Confusing **Category** (e.g., ID.AM) with **Subcategory** (e.g., ID.AM-1). Use correct notation in all answers.
> - **Mistake 3:** Omitting the **Govern** function in answers for questions on **CSF 2.0** specifically. The 2024 KTU paper expects 6 functions, not 5.
> - **Mistake 4:** Forgetting **Indian context** (CERT-In, IT Act 2000/2008, DPDP Act 2023) in scenario questions. Examiners reward regional/contextual awareness.
> - **Mistake 5:** Skipping the **diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts. Always include at least the wheel diagram or the 7-step loop.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CSF Navigation**

- ✅ **CSF = NIST Cybersecurity Framework** — voluntary, risk-based, industry-agnostic.
- ✅ **Origin:** 2014 (CSF 1.0) → 2018 (1.1) → **February 2024 (2.0)**.
- ✅ **Six Functions (in order):** **G**overn → **I**dentify → **P**rotect → **D**etect → **R**espond → **R**ecover → **G-I-P-D-R-R**.
- ✅ **Govern is NEW in 2.0** — it sits at the center, overseeing all other functions.
- ✅ **Four Tiers:** Partial → Risk-Informed → Repeatable → Adaptive (P-R-R-A).
- ✅ **Tiers are descriptive, not prescriptive** — they describe maturity, not enforce it.
- ✅ **Profile = Current vs. Target.** Gap = Target − Current.
- ✅ **7-Step Navigation:** Prioritize → Orient → Current Profile → Risk Assessment → Target Profile → Gap Analysis → Implement.
- ✅ **It is a continuous loop**, not a one-time exercise (PDCA cycle).
- ✅ **Indian context:** CERT-In, IT Act 2000/2008, DPDP Act 2023.
- ✅ **Real-world use:** SolarWinds, Colonial Pipeline, healthcare (HIPAA), finance (PCI-DSS).
- ✅ **Notation:** Categories use *letters* (ID.AM); Subcategories use *numbers* (ID.AM-1).
- ✅ **Key risk equation:** $R = L \times I$ (Likelihood × Impact).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Utilize the Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) to Manage Activities, Tasks, and Outcomes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Utilize the Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) to Manage Activities, Tasks, and Outcomes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (NIST CSF 2.0 Terminology)

The **Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)** is a voluntary, risk-based, outcome-focused guidance document published by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** that enables organizations — regardless of sector, size, or maturity — to **organize, communicate, assess, prioritize, and continuously improve their cybersecurity activities** using a common language of *Functions, Categories, Subcategories, Tiers, and Profiles*.

When an organization "utilizes" the CSF, it systematically **manages Activities (operational work), Tasks (granular steps), and Outcomes (measurable results)** by mapping them against six top-level Functions: **Govern (GV), Identify (ID), Protect (PR), Detect (DE), Respond (RS), and Recover (RC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3**
> The current NIST CSF 2.0 (released February 26, 2024) **elevated "Govern" as a sixth Function** that sits above and informs the other five. This is a recent and high-yield point frequently asked in KTU University Exams.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Analogy

Imagine a **large multi-specialty hospital** managing patient safety.

| Hospital Element | Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hospital Governance Board (policy & ethics) | **GOVERN (GV)** — Policies, roles, risk strategy, oversight |
| Patient Registration & Diagnosis | **IDENTIFY (ID)** — Asset & risk inventory |
| Hygiene, PPE, Locked Medicine Cabinets | **PROTECT (PR)** — Preventive controls |
| Daily Vitals Monitoring, Lab Reports | **DETECT (DE)** — Anomaly & event detection |
| Emergency Response Team, ICU | **RESPOND (RS)** — Incident handling, containment |
| Post-op Rehab, Insurance Claims | **RECOVER (RC)** — Restoration, lessons learned |

The hospital **doesn't wait for a disease to strike**; it **runs continuous activities, tasks, and outcomes** to keep patients safe. The CSF does the same for digital assets — it converts abstract "security goals" into **concrete, measurable, repeatable activities and tasks** that produce verifiable outcomes.

### 1.3 Why Organizations Need a Framework to "Manage Activities, Tasks, and Outcomes"

Without a unifying framework, security work becomes:
- **Ad-hoc** (fire-fighting instead of planning),
- **Duplicated** (multiple teams running overlapping scans),
- **Unmeasurable** (no clear pass/fail criteria).

The CSF solves this by giving every activity a **Function → Category → Subcategory** home, a **Task** definition, and an **Outcome** statement beginning with the word "Outcome:" — making it directly auditable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Outcome Statement Convention**
> Every Subcategory in the CSF is phrased as an **outcome** (e.g., *"Physical access to assets is managed and protected"*). This shifts the organization from *checking-the-box compliance* to *proving actual security outcomes*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Structural Components of the CSF

The CSF is built from three interlocking structural pieces. An organization uses them **simultaneously** to manage activities, tasks, and outcomes.

#### A. The Core (Functions → Categories → Subcategories)

The Core is the **heart of the CSF** — a taxonomy of cybersecurity outcomes arranged into six Functions.

- **GOVERN (GV)** — Establishes and monitors the organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy, policies, processes, and procedures. The "supervisory" function. (Categories: Organizational Context, Risk Management Strategy, Roles & Responsibilities, Policies & Procedures, Oversight; plus 3 sub-categories for *Cybersecurity Supply Chain Risk Management*.)
- **IDENTIFY (ID)** — Develops the organizational understanding to manage cybersecurity risk to systems, people, assets, data, and capabilities. (Categories: Asset Management, Risk Assessment, Risk Management Strategy, *Supply Chain Risk Management*, Improvement.)
- **PROTECT (PR)** — Implements appropriate safeguards to ensure delivery of critical services and to limit the impact of a cybersecurity event. (Categories: Identity & Access Management, Awareness & Training, Data Security, Platform Security (formerly Infrastructure), Technology Infrastructure Resilience.)
- **DETECT (DE)** — Defines the appropriate activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event. (Categories: Continuous Monitoring, Adverse Event Analysis.)
- **RESPOND (RS)** — Takes action regarding a detected cybersecurity incident. (Categories: Incident Management, Incident Analysis, Incident Response Reporting & Communication, Incident Mitigation.)
- **RECOVER (RC)** — Maintains plans for resilience and restores any capabilities or services impaired due to a cybersecurity incident. (Categories: Incident Recovery Plan Execution, Incident Recovery Communication, Improvement.)

#### B. The Tiers (Implementation Maturity)

Tiers describe the **degree to which an organization's cybersecurity risk management practices** exhibit the characteristics defined in the Framework. They range from **Tier 1 (Partial)** to **Tier 4 (Adaptive)**.

- **Tier 1 — Partial**: Ad-hoc, reactive; risk is managed in an inconsistent manner; limited awareness.
- **Tier 2 — Risk Informed**: Risk-aware but not enterprise-wide; approved by management but not standardized.
- **Tier 3 — Repeatable**: Formally approved, expressed as policy; consistently applied organization-wide.
- **Tier 4 — Adaptive**: Continuously improved using lessons learned, predictive indicators, and advanced tech; actively adapts to evolving threats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tiers ≠ Maturity Models**
> Tiers are **descriptive characteristics**, not a maturity model with pass/fail. An organization may legitimately *choose* Tier 1 or Tier 2 if its risk profile warrants it.

#### C. The Profiles (Current vs Target)

A Profile is the **alignment of Functions, Categories, and Subcategories with business requirements, risk tolerance, and resources**.

- **Current Profile**: indicates the cybersecurity outcomes that are *currently being achieved*.
- **Target Profile**: indicates the outcomes needed to *achieve the desired cybersecurity risk management goals*.

> The **gap** between Current and Target drives prioritization of Activities and Tasks.

### 2.2 Mapping Activities → Tasks → Outcomes

A **Framework user** performs three nested loops of work:

1. **Activity** (broad work effort) — e.g., "Conduct vulnerability scans of all internet-facing assets."
2. **Task** (specific action) — e.g., "Run authenticated Nmap scan weekly; record CVSS score; file in JIRA."
3. **Outcome** (measurable result) — e.g., "GV.OC-01: The organizational mission is understood and informs cybersecurity risk management." Outcome statements begin with the word "Outcome:".

This is captured concisely in the following KTU Formula Sheet.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| CSF Element | Definition | KTU Exam Weight | Common Sub-elements (count in CSF 2.0) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Highest-level cybersecurity activity group | High (definition-based questions) | **6** Functions (GV, ID, PR, DE, RS, RC) |
| **Category** | Group of cybersecurity outcomes within a Function | Medium | **22** Categories in CSF 2.0 |
| **Subcategory** | Outcome-based statement within a Category | High (mapping questions) | **106** Subcategories |
| **Tier** | Qualitative measure of organizational rigor | Medium (comparison questions) | **4** Tiers (Partial → Adaptive) |
| **Profile** | Alignment of Core with business needs | High (case-study questions) | 2 views: Current and Target |
| **Implementation Example** | Actionable guidance for achieving a Subcategory | Low (reference) | Hundreds of illustrative examples |
| **Informative Reference** | Mapping to existing standards (ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, CIS) | Medium | Maps to external standards |

### 2.4 The CSF Lifecycle — 7-Step Process to Manage Activities, Tasks, Outcomes

Per the NIST CSF 2.0 *Quick-Start Guide*, organizations proceed as follows. These steps convert the static document into **dynamic project management work**.

1. **Prioritize and Scope** — define business process, asset boundaries, and risk considerations.
2. **Orient** — identify the systems, assets, data, and the threats/risks relevant to the scope.
3. **Create a Current Profile** — record the current Subcategory outcomes being achieved (Tier assignment per Function).
4. **Conduct a Risk Assessment** — analyze the operational environment for likelihood and impact.
5. **Create a Target Profile** — articulate the desired outcomes; choose a Target Tier.
6. **Determine, Analyze, and Prioritize Gaps** — compare Current vs Target; rank gaps by risk impact and resource cost.
7. **Implement Action Plan** — allocate Activities, Tasks, and resources to close the gaps; **measure outcomes** continuously.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Critical Infrastructure**: Energy grids use CSF to align with NERC CIP and TSA directives.
- **Banking/Finance**: Maps to FFIEC, PCI-DSS, and ISO 27001.
- **Healthcare**: HIPAA + CSF for ransomware resilience.
- **B.Tech Industry Projects**: Use CSF as a *lightweight risk model* in capstone projects, replacing ad-hoc security checklists.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The "Management Loop" as a Mathematical Mapping

Although the CSF is qualitative, we can **formally derive** the relationship between *Activities, Tasks, and Outcomes* using set theory. This is a KTU-favored analytical question.

Let:
- $F = \{F_1, F_2, \dots, F_6\}$ be the set of **Functions** in the CSF Core.
- $C_{F_i} = \{c_{i,1}, c_{i,2}, \dots, c_{i,n_i}\}$ be the set of **Categories** under Function $F_i$, where $n_i$ is the number of categories.
- $S_{c_{i,j}} = \{s_{i,j,1}, s_{i,j,2}, \dots, s_{i,j,m_{i,j}}\}$ be the set of **Subcategories** under Category $c_{i,j}$.
- $A_{s_{i,j,k}}$ be the set of **Activities** (organizational work efforts) that produce outcome $s_{i,j,k}$.
- $T_{a} = \{t_1, t_2, \dots, t_{p_a}\}$ be the set of **Tasks** that constitute Activity $a$.
- $O_{a}$ be the measurable **Outcome** of Activity $a$.

The traceability relationship is:

$$
\forall F_i \in F \;:\; F_i = \bigcup_{j=1}^{n_i} C_{F_i}^{(j)}
$$

$$
\forall c_{i,j} \in C_{F_i} \;:\; c_{i,j} = \bigcup_{k=1}^{m_{i,j}} S_{c_{i,j}}^{(k)}
$$

$$
\forall s \in S \;:\; s = \text{Outcome of } A_s, \quad \text{where } A_s = \{a \in A \;\vert\; a \text{ realizes } s\}
$$

$$
\forall a \in A \;:\; a = \bigotimes_{p=1}^{p_a} t_p, \quad \text{where } \bigotimes \text{ denotes sequential/parallel task composition}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Mapping an Activity to a Subcategory

**Scenario:** A mid-sized SaaS company wishes to "manage the activities, tasks, and outcomes" of vulnerability management.

**Step 1 — Identify the Subcategory outcome to be achieved**

From the CSF 2.0 Core, the relevant Subcategory is **ID.RA-01**:
> *"Vulnerabilities in assets are identified, validated, and recorded."*

This is our target **Outcome** $O$.

**Step 2 — Define the Activity (broad work effort) to realize this outcome**

The Activity $A_{ID.RA-01}$ is **"Continuous Vulnerability Management."**

**Step 3 — Decompose the Activity into Tasks**

$$
T_{A} = \{t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4, t_5\}
$$

where:
- $t_1$ = "Run authenticated vulnerability scanner (Nessus / Qualys) across all in-scope assets every 7 days."
- $t_2$ = "Triage findings; eliminate false positives; assign CVSS v3.1 base scores."
- $t_3$ = "Record each validated finding in a centralized vulnerability database (e.g., JIRA / ServiceNow)."
- $t_4$ = "Assign remediation owner; set SLA: Critical (CVSS $\geq$ 9.0) → 7 days; High → 30 days; Medium → 60 days."
- $t_5$ = "Re-scan after remediation; record residual risk acceptance for any unfixed finding."

**Step 4 — Define measurable success criteria (Outcome validation)**

We declare the outcome achieved when:

$$
\text{Outcome}_{ID.RA-01} \;\Leftrightarrow\; \Big(\, \#\text{critical unpatched} = 0 \,\Big) \;\land\; \Big(\, \text{Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR)}_{CVSS \geq 9.0} \leq 7 \text{ days} \,\Big)
$$

**Step 5 — Assign the Tier rating for this Function**

A simple *Task Coverage Tier* can be derived as:

$$
\tau_{A} = \frac{\vert \{ t \in T_A \;\vert\; t \text{ is fully implemented and measured} \} \vert}{\vert T_A \vert}
$$

For the company above, all 5 tasks are implemented and measured, hence:

$$
\tau_{A} = \frac{5}{5} = 1.0
$$

A simple mapping rule:

$$
\text{Tier}(A) =
\begin{cases}
\text{Tier 4 (Adaptive)} & \text{if } \tau_A \geq 0.9 \text{ and continuous improvement loop exists} \\
\text{Tier 3 (Repeatable)} & \text{if } 0.7 \leq \tau_A < 0.9 \\
\text{Tier 2 (Risk Informed)} & \text{if } 0.4 \leq \tau_A < 0.7 \\
\text{Tier 1 (Partial)} & \text{if } \tau_A < 0.4
\end{cases}
$$

For our example, $\tau_A = 1.0$ with a quarterly lessons-learned review, so the **Activity qualifies as Tier 3 (Repeatable)** under Function IDENTIFY.

### 3.3 Gap Analysis Derivation

Let $P_{\text{current}}$ and $P_{\text{target}}$ be the set of Subcategory outcomes in the Current and Target Profiles respectively. The **Gap Set** is:

$$
G = P_{\text{target}} \setminus P_{\text{current}}
$$

Each gap $g \in G$ is then prioritized by a *Risk Priority Number* (RPN) borrowed from FMEA:

$$
\text{RPN}(g) = L_g \times I_g \times D_g
$$

where $L_g$ is the **Likelihood** of the underlying risk materializing (1–5), $I_g$ is the **Impact** severity (1–5), and $D_g$ is the **Detectability** difficulty (1–5). Gaps are then sequenced by descending RPN for action-plan execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**
> The CSF does **not** prescribe quantitative weights, but using $L \times I \times D$ to rank gaps is the accepted academic practice taught in PECCT754 and is a likely Part B (14-mark) derivation.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Function vs Tier vs Typical Activity

| Function | Tier 1 (Partial) | Tier 2 (Risk Informed) | Tier 3 (Repeatable) | Tier 4 (Adaptive) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GV (Govern)** | No formal cyber policy | Cyber policy approved by mgmt. | Enterprise-wide policy, reviewed yearly | Continuous policy feedback loop; board-level KPIs |
| **ID (Identify)** | Spreadsheet asset list | Annual risk assessment | CMDB-integrated, monthly delta | Real-time asset discovery; threat intel-driven |
| **PR (Protect)** | Basic antivirus | MFA on critical apps only | Phishing-resistant MFA org-wide | Zero-Trust, passwordless, microsegmentation |
| **DE (Detect)** | Manual log review | SIEM with basic rules | UEBA, SOAR-driven alerts | AI-driven behavioral analytics + threat hunting |
| **RS (Respond)** | Ad-hoc war room | Documented IR plan (not tested) | Quarterly tabletop exercises | Automated playbooks; < 1 hr MTTD |
| **RC (Recover)** | Backups exist, untested | Monthly restore tests | RTO/RPO defined and met | Chaos-engineering; continuous DR drills |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**Define the NIST Cybersecurity Framework and list its six Functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)** is a voluntary, risk-based, outcome-focused guidance published by the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology to help organizations **identify, assess, and manage cybersecurity risk** using a common language of activities, tasks, and outcomes. (1.5 marks)

The six Functions in CSF 2.0 are:

1. **GV — Govern** (Cybersecurity risk governance and policy)
2. **ID — Identify** (Asset and risk identification)
3. **PR — Protect** (Preventive safeguards)
4. **DE — Detect** (Detection of anomalies and events)
5. **RS — Respond** (Incident response)
6. **RC — Recover** (Restoration and resilience)

(1.5 marks — 0.25 mark per correct Function)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Differentiate between a Current Profile and a Target Profile in the CSF.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Current Profile (1.5 marks):** Captures the cybersecurity outcomes — i.e., which Functions/Categories/Subcategories — that the organization is *currently* achieving. It is created after assessing present practices, often using Tiers to describe rigor per Function. It represents the *as-is* state.
- **Target Profile (1.5 marks):** Captures the *desired* outcomes aligned to the organization's risk tolerance, mission, regulatory obligations, and business goals. It represents the *to-be* state and is informed by a risk assessment.

The **gap** between the two profiles drives the prioritization of Activities, Tasks, and resource allocation for improvement.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **seven-step process** for using the NIST CSF 2.0 to manage cybersecurity activities, tasks, and outcomes. Draw a labeled block diagram showing the flow.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* For a **B.Tech engineering college's central IT system** with the following situation:
- No documented cybersecurity policy exists.
- An inventory of 450 assets exists only in Excel sheets, last updated 12 months ago.
- Backups are taken daily but never tested.
- Firewall logs are reviewed manually once a month.
- A recent ransomware attempt was contained using an ad-hoc team call.

**Create** a Current Profile (identify Function/Category status) and propose a **1-year Target Profile** with at least **three concrete Activities**, each broken into **Tasks** and measurable **Outcomes**.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) The Seven-Step Process (7 marks):**

| Step | Name | Purpose (Activity) | Output (Outcome) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Prioritize and Scope | Define the business process, organizational boundaries, and risk considerations (Govern / ID) | Scope statement |
| 2 | Orient | Identify the systems, assets, data, and the relevant threats (Identify) | Risk picture |
| 3 | Create a Current Profile | Assess which Subcategories are achieved; assign Tiers per Function (Identify / Govern) | Current Profile |
| 4 | Conduct a Risk Assessment | Analyze likelihood and impact; compute RPNs (Govern / Identify) | Risk register |
| 5 | Create a Target Profile | State desired outcomes and target Tiers (Govern) | Target Profile |
| 6 | Determine, Analyze, and Prioritize Gaps | Compute $G = P_{\text{target}} \setminus P_{\text{current}}$; rank by RPN (Identify / Govern) | Prioritized gap list |
| 7 | Implement Action Plan | Allocate Activities, Tasks, resources; measure outcomes; iterate (Protect/Detect/Respond/Recover) | Verified outcomes; updated Profile |

(0.5 mark per row, 3.5 marks total. Plus 1 mark for the diagram below, 1 mark for "Activities–Tasks–Outcomes" linkage, 1.5 marks for the closure/feedback loop explanation.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Step 1 Prioritize and Scope] --> A2[Step 2 Orient]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Current Profile]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Risk Assessment]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Target Profile]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Gap Analysis]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Action Plan]
    A7 -. continuous improvement .-> A1
```

Each step converts **Activities** into **Tasks**, and the completion of Tasks produces measurable **Outcomes** that update the Profiles.

**(b) College IT Case Study — Current & Target Profile (7 marks):**

**Current Profile Assessment (3.5 marks):**

| Function | Category | Status | Tier |
|---|---|---|---|
| GV | Organizational Context | No policy → **Not Achieved** | 1 |
| ID | Asset Management | Excel, 12 months stale → **Partially Achieved** | 1 |
| ID | Risk Assessment | None → **Not Achieved** | 1 |
| PR | Data Security (Backups) | Daily but untested → **Partially Achieved** | 1 |
| DE | Continuous Monitoring | Manual monthly log review → **Partially Achieved** | 1 |
| RS | Incident Management | Ad-hoc → **Partially Achieved** | 1 |
| RC | Recovery Plan Execution | No restore test → **Not Achieved** | 1 |

Overall **Current Profile = Tier 1 (Partial)** across all six Functions.

**Target Profile after 1 year (1.5 marks):**
Aim for **Tier 2 (Risk Informed) → Tier 3 (Repeatable)** in core Functions.

**Three Concrete Activities with Tasks and Outcomes (2 marks):**

**Activity 1 — "Implement Cybersecurity Governance" (Function: GV)**
- *Task 1.1*: Draft Cybersecurity Policy aligned with NIST CSF; review with IQAC.
- *Task 1.2*: Approve at Academic Council; publish on intranet.
- *Task 1.3*: Appoint a CISO-equivalent (e.g., HoD-IT with charter).
- *Outcome*: GV.OC-01 achieved — *"The organizational mission is understood and informs cybersecurity risk management."* Measured by presence of a board-approved policy document (binary: 1 = achieved).

**Activity 2 — "Continuous Asset and Risk Management" (Function: ID)**
- *Task 2.1*: Deploy a lightweight CMDB (e.g., Snipe-IT) and scan all 450 assets.
- *Task 2.2*: Conduct a semester-wise risk assessment; produce risk register with RPNs.
- *Task 2.3*: Review register at quarterly IT Committee meetings.
- *Outcome*: ID.AM-01 achieved — *"Inventories of hardware and software assets are maintained."* Measured by `(CMDB records) / (Total known assets) ≥ 0.95`.

**Activity 3 — "Test, Detect, and Respond" (Functions: DE / RS / RC)**
- *Task 3.1*: Configure centralized logging (Wazuh) from firewall and servers.
- *Task 3.2*: Quarterly restore test from backup; record RTO ≤ 4 hours.
- *Task 3.3*: Conduct a tabletop ransomware drill with IT and HoDs; record MTTD.
- *Outcome*: RC.RP-03 achieved — *"Recovery plans are tested and updated."* Measured by `(Successful restore tests) / (Scheduled restore tests) = 1.0` and RTO ≤ 4 hours.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B**
> Students commonly lose **2 to 3 marks** in part (b) by (i) listing Activities without decomposing them into **Tasks**, (ii) forgetting to phrase the **Outcome as a CSF Subcategory statement** (use wording like *"Outcome: ID.AM-01 is achieved when..."*), and (iii) omitting a **measurable criterion** (e.g., a percentage, RTO, or RPN). Always show the full A → T → O chain.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **four CSF Implementation Tiers** with one concrete example of *Activity, Task, and Outcome* for the **PROTECT (PR)** Function at each tier.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With a suitable diagram, explain how an organization uses the **CSF Core, Tiers, and Profiles** together in a single management cycle. Use the **GOVERN (GV)** Function as the central driver in your diagram.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Four Tiers for PROTECT (PR) — Activity/Task/Outcome (7 marks):**

For each tier we present: **Activity → Task → Outcome**, mapped to a specific PR Subcategory. We use **PR.AA-01 (Identity & Credential Management)** as the anchor.

| Tier | Activity | Task | Measurable Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1 — Partial** | Basic password protection | (a) Issue a username/password to each student; (b) Reset on demand. | Outcome: PR.AA-01 *partially* achieved; ≥ 60% of users have credentials, but no MFA. Evidence: help-desk logs. *(1.5 marks)* |
| **Tier 2 — Risk Informed** | Risk-based MFA on critical apps | (a) Identify critical apps (LMS, ERP); (b) Enroll all staff in TOTP MFA; (c) Document in policy. | Outcome: 100% of staff have MFA on the ERP system. Evidence: identity-provider report. *(1.5 marks)* |
| **Tier 3 — Repeatable** | Enterprise-wide phishing-resistant MFA | (a) Mandate FIDO2 hardware keys for all faculty; (b) Integrate with SSO; (c) Quarterly access reviews. | Outcome: 100% of faculty logins use phishing-resistant MFA. Access-review findings closed within 30 days. *(2 marks)* |
| **Tier 4 — Adaptive** | Continuous, risk-adaptive authentication | (a) Deploy UEBA-driven risk scoring; (b) Trigger step-up auth on anomaly; (c) Quarterly red-team password-spray tests. | Outcome: Zero credential-stuffing breaches for 12 consecutive months; mean time to step-up ≤ 30 seconds. *(2 marks)* |

**(b) Integrated CSF Management Cycle (7 marks):**

A complete cycle uses the **Core** to define *what* outcomes to achieve, **Tiers** to define *how rigorously* to achieve them, and **Profiles** to align achievement with *business risk tolerance*. The **GOVERN function** anchors and supervises the entire cycle because it provides policy, roles, and oversight that every other Function depends on.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef coreFill fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef tierFill fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef profFill fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef govFill fill:#7B1FA2,stroke:#fff,color:#fff

    gov((GOVERN GV\nPolicy Roles Risk Strategy)):::govFill

    core[CSF Core: 6 Functions 22 Categories 106 Subcategories]:::coreFill
    tiers[Implementation Tiers 1 Partial to 4 Adaptive]:::tierFill
    profileCurrent[Current Profile As Is Outcomes]:::profFill
    profileTarget[Target Profile To Be Outcomes]:::profFill
    gap[Gap Set and RPN Ranking]:::coreFill
    act[Activities Tasks Resources]:::coreFill
    outcome[Measured Outcomes Updated Profile]:::profFill

    gov --> core
    gov --> tiers
    gov --> profileTarget
    core --> profileCurrent
    core --> profileTarget
    profileCurrent --> gap
    profileTarget --> gap
    gap --> act
    act --> outcome
    outcome --> profileCurrent
    outcome -. feedback to .-> gov
```

**Step-by-step narrative (2 marks for the description):**

1. **GOVERN** sets the strategy, policy, and risk tolerance. (0.5)
2. The **Core** enumerates the universe of desired outcomes (Subcategories). (0.5)
3. The organization creates a **Current Profile** by mapping existing practices to Subcategories. (0.5)
4. **Tiers** are assigned per Function to describe the rigor of current practices. (0.5)
5. **GOVERN** plus the risk assessment define a **Target Profile** with chosen Tiers. (0.5)
6. **Gaps** are computed and ranked. (0.5)
7. **Activities and Tasks** close the gaps. (0.5)
8. The new outcomes **update the Current Profile** and **feedback to GOVERN** for continuous improvement. (0.5)

(Adjudicate 7 marks: 2 marks for diagram quality, 5 marks for narrative covering Core, Tiers, Profiles, gap analysis, and feedback.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (Q B)**
> Students often draw the diagram but **forget the GOVERN node** as the supervisory anchor in CSF 2.0, costing 1 full mark. Also avoid listing the six Functions *inside* the diagram without showing the **arrow flow** of the cycle — examiners want a true cycle, not a static wheel.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Remember Bullets for KTU 2024 Exam**

- The **CSF 2.0** (Feb 2024) has **6 Functions**, **22 Categories**, and **106 Subcategories**.
- **GOVERN (GV)** is the *new sixth Function* in CSF 2.0 — frequently tested.
- The mnemonic is **"G-I-P-D-R-R"** = **Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover**.
- The CSF has **three structural pieces**: **Core, Tiers, Profiles**.
- **Tiers** range from **1 (Partial) → 2 (Risk Informed) → 3 (Repeatable) → 4 (Adaptive)**. They are *descriptive*, not pass/fail.
- A **Profile** is the alignment of Functions/Categories/Subcategories with business needs. There is always a **Current Profile** and a **Target Profile**.
- The **Gap** is $G = P_{\text{target}} \setminus P_{\text{current}}$, prioritized by RPN $= L \times I \times D$.
- The **7-step implementation process**: Prioritize & Scope → Orient → Current Profile → Risk Assessment → Target Profile → Gap Analysis → Action Plan.
- **Activities** are broad work efforts; **Tasks** are specific steps; **Outcomes** are measurable Subcategory statements beginning with the word "Outcome:".
- The CSF is **voluntary, outcome-based, and risk-based**; it maps to **ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, CIS Controls**.
- For any 14-mark question, always include: **(i) Definition, (ii) Six Functions with mnemonic, (iii) Diagram, (iv) A worked example** with Activity → Task → Outcome.
- **Watch the GOVERN function** — its inclusion makes any answer CSF 2.0-compliant rather than 1.1-compliant.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tailor the Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) to Create a Security Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tailor the Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) to Create a Security Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — Tailor the Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) to Create a Security Plan

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) Tailoring** is the *systematic process of customizing, scoping, and adapting the NIST Cybersecurity Framework* — comprising the **Framework Core**, **Framework Implementation Tiers**, and **Framework Profiles** — to align with an organization's unique mission, business drivers, risk tolerance, regulatory environment, and operational realities, in order to produce a coherent, executable, and measurable **Cybersecurity Security Plan**.

In the context of KTU's PECCT754 syllabus (Module 3), this process transforms the *generic, one-size-fits-all* NIST CSF (a voluntary guidance framework originally published by the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology in **NIST SP 800-53 / NIST CSF 1.1 / CSF 2.0**) into a **bespoke, organization-specific cybersecurity governance and engineering roadmap**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tailor and the Suit"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine buying a *ready-made* suit from a store. It fits reasonably well, but the shoulders are slightly wide, the trouser length is too long, and the color is close to — but not exactly — your corporate color. A **tailor** takes that generic suit and *alters* the seams, shortens the sleeves, adjusts the fit, and re-stitches the lining so that the suit becomes *uniquely yours*. 

The **NIST CSF** is the generic suit. **Tailoring** is what the cybersecurity engineer does — picking only the relevant controls (Functions/Categories/Subcategories), matching them to the organization's risk appetite, and stitching them together into a **Security Plan**. Without tailoring, an organization is wearing someone else's suit.

### 1.3 Why Tailoring is Mandatory (Not Optional)

A *raw* CSF applied verbatim to every organization will:

1. **Over-spend** on irrelevant controls (wasting budget on ICS security for a pure SaaS startup).
2. **Under-protect** critical assets (missing industry-specific risks like HIPAA for a hospital).
3. **Mis-align** with legal/regulatory mandates (PCI-DSS, GDPR, DPDP Act 2023).
4. **Fail board-level scrutiny** because it cannot be mapped to business KPIs.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Anchored for KTU

| Term | Plain Definition | KTU Exam-Ready Phrase |
|---|---|---|
| **CSF Core** | The taxonomy of cybersecurity activities grouped into 5 Functions | "A catalog of desired cybersecurity outcomes expressed as Functions → Categories → Subcategories" |
| **Framework Profile** | The alignment of CSF Core with business needs, risk tolerance, and resources | "A prioritization of cybersecurity outcomes based on the organization's mission" |
| **Current Profile** | The "as-is" state of cybersecurity | "Indicates the cybersecurity outcomes currently being achieved" |
| **Target Profile** | The "to-be" desired state | "Indicates the outcomes needed to achieve the desired cybersecurity risk posture" |
| **Gap Analysis** | The delta between Current and Target | "Identifies the gaps that need to be closed to move from current to target state" |
| **Implementation Tier** | The degree to which cybersecurity risk management is institutionalized | "Describes the rigor of an organization's risk governance practices (Tier 1–4)" |
| **Security Plan** | The actionable document derived from CSF tailoring | "A documented roadmap of prioritized actions, owners, timelines, and KPIs" |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Structural Components of the NIST CSF (Recall)

> [!NOTE]
> Before we *tailor*, we must remember what we are tailoring. The NIST CSF has **three** structural pillars. Examiners frequently test whether you can list these in order.

1. **Framework Core** — *What* should be done (Functions, Categories, Subcategories, Informative References).
2. **Framework Implementation Tiers** — *How well* it is being done (Tiers 1 → 4).
3. **Framework Profiles** — *Where* we are vs. *where* we want to be (Current + Target).

## 2.2 The Five Functions of the CSF Core (Acronym: **IDPRR**)

$$
\text{CSF Core} = \{ \text{Identify}, \text{Protect}, \text{Detect}, \text{Respond}, \text{Recover} \}
$$

| Function | Purpose | Sample Category | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Identify (ID)** | Develop organizational understanding to manage cybersecurity risk | Asset Management (ID.AM) | *"Know yourself"* |
| **Protect (PR)** | Implement appropriate safeguards to ensure delivery of critical services | Access Control (PR.AC) | *"Build walls"* |
| **Detect (DE)** | Implement activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event | Anomalies & Events (DE.AE) | *"Set alarms"* |
| **Respond (RS)** | Take action regarding a detected cybersecurity incident | Response Planning (RS.RP) | *"Put out fires"* |
| **Recover (RC)** | Maintain plans for resilience and restoration of capabilities | Recovery Planning (RC.RP) | *"Rebuild stronger"* |

## 2.3 The Four Implementation Tiers (Quantitative Indicator of Maturity)

| Tier | Label | KTU Description | KTU Scoring Hint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier 1** | Partial | Risk management is *ad-hoc*, reactive; no org-wide awareness. | "Worst — not recommended for any mature firm." |
| **Tier 2** | Risk-Informed | Risk management is *approved* but not enterprise-wide. | "Some practices exist." |
| **Tier 3** | Repeatable | Risk management is *formally approved* as policy. | "Industry standard." |
| **Tier 4** | Adaptive | Risk management is *continuously improved* using lessons learned. | "Best — predictive, proactive." |

## 2.4 The Seven-Step Tailoring Methodology (NIST Reference)

The tailoring workflow recommended by NIST for creating a security plan can be condensed into seven logical phases. Each phase has a *gate question* used by examiners.

| Phase | Gate Question | Deliverable |
|---|---|---|
| **P1. Scope & Prioritize** | *Which business units, systems, and assets are in scope?* | Scoping document |
| **P2. Orient** | *Which threats, vulnerabilities, and regulations apply?* | Threat catalogue |
| **P3. Create Current Profile** | *Where are we today?* | Current Profile |
| **P4. Risk Assessment** | *What is our actual risk exposure?* | Risk register |
| **P5. Create Target Profile** | *Where do we want to be?* | Target Profile |
| **P6. Gap Analysis** | *What is the delta?* | Gap report |
| **P7. Implement Action Plan** | *How do we close the gaps?* | Security Plan + Roadmap |

## 2.5 The Tailoring Equation (Quantitative Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While CSF is *qualitative*, KTU examiners reward students who can express tailoring as a measurable equation:

$$
\text{Tailoring Factor} \; (T_f) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot C_i^{current}}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot C_i^{target}}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = total number of selected Subcategories
- $w_i$ = business-criticality weight of Subcategory $i$ (typically $1 \le w_i \le 5$)
- $C_i^{current}$ = current implementation score (0 = not implemented, 1 = partially, 2 = fully)
- $C_i^{target}$ = target implementation score (always 2 for a fully-implemented profile)

**Interpretation Scale:**

| $T_f$ Value | Interpretation | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $T_f \ge 0.85$ | Strong posture | Maintain & monitor |
| $0.55 \le T_f < 0.85$ | Moderate posture | Targeted improvements |
| $T_f < 0.55$ | Weak posture | Urgent remediation roadmap |

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners often frame a 14-mark question as: *"Compute the tailoring factor for an organization given X subcategories, their weights, and current scores."* Memorize this formula.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of CSF Tailoring

- **Healthcare**: Tailored profile enables HIPAA mapping.
- **BFSI (Indian Banking)**: Maps directly to RBI Cyber Security Framework (2016) and SEBI CSCRF.
- **Critical Infrastructure**: Aligns with NCIIPC guidelines and ISA/IEC 62443.
- **Startups**: Provides a low-cost, scalable, defensible governance model for investors and auditors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Scoring & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Computing the Tailoring Factor

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most frequently tested numerical** in PECCT754 Module 3. Walk through every step.

### Problem Statement

An Indian BFSI firm has selected the following **N = 5** CSF Subcategories for tailoring. Business-criticality weights and current implementation scores are:

| i | Subcategory | Function | $w_i$ (Weight) | $C_i^{current}$ | $C_i^{target}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ID.AM-1 (Physical devices inventoried) | Identify | 4 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | PR.AC-1 (Identity & credential mgmt) | Protect | 5 | 2 | 2 |
| 3 | PR.DS-1 (Data-at-rest protection) | Protect | 5 | 0 | 2 |
| 4 | DE.CM-1 (Network monitoring) | Detect | 3 | 1 | 2 |
| 5 | RS.RP-1 (Response plan exists) | Respond | 4 | 1 | 2 |

**Compute the Tailoring Factor $T_f$ and recommend an action.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Numerator: weighted current score**

$$
\sum w_i \cdot C_i^{current} = (4)(1) + (5)(2) + (5)(0) + (3)(1) + (4)(1)
$$

Compute each product separately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1} &= 4 \times 1 = 4 \\
\text{Term 2} &= 5 \times 2 = 10 \\
\text{Term 3} &= 5 \times 0 = 0 \\
\text{Term 4} &= 3 \times 1 = 3 \\
\text{Term 5} &= 4 \times 1 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$
\sum w_i \cdot C_i^{current} = 4 + 10 + 0 + 3 + 4 = 21
$$

**Step 2 — Denominator: weighted target score**

$$
\sum w_i \cdot C_i^{target} = (4)(2) + (5)(2) + (5)(2) + (3)(2) + (4)(2)
$$

Each target score is **2**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1} &= 4 \times 2 = 8 \\
\text{Term 2} &= 5 \times 2 = 10 \\
\text{Term 3} &= 5 \times 2 = 10 \\
\text{Term 4} &= 3 \times 2 = 6 \\
\text{Term 5} &= 4 \times 2 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$
\sum w_i \cdot C_i^{target} = 8 + 10 + 10 + 6 + 8 = 42
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_f$**

$$
T_f = \frac{21}{42} = 0.50
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret against the scale**

Since $T_f = 0.50 < 0.55$, the firm falls in the **"Weak Posture — Urgent Remediation"** band.

### Step 5 — Recommend Tailoring Action

The largest *weighted gap* occurs at Subcategory **PR.DS-1** (Data-at-rest protection), where $w_i = 5$ but $C_i^{current} = 0$. Therefore, the **first** item in the security plan should be deploying encryption-at-rest for sensitive customer data, followed by formalizing the incident response plan (RS.RP-1).

## 3.2 The Gap Analysis Matrix (KTU Favourite)

For each Subcategory, define:

$$
\text{Gap}_i = w_i \cdot (C_i^{target} - C_i^{current})
$$

| i | Subcategory | $w_i$ | $C_i^{target} - C_i^{current}$ | Gap$_i$ | Priority Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | PR.DS-1 | 5 | $2 - 0 = 2$ | **10** | 🥇 #1 |
| 1 | ID.AM-1 | 4 | $2 - 1 = 1$ | 4 | 🥈 #2 |
| 4 | DE.CM-1 | 3 | $2 - 1 = 1$ | 3 | 🥉 #3 |
| 5 | RS.RP-1 | 4 | $2 - 1 = 1$ | 4 | 🥈 tied |
| 2 | PR.AC-1 | 5 | $2 - 2 = 0$ | 0 | — Complete |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** Gap$_i$ ranking *drives the sequence of the security plan roadmap*. Highest gap = highest priority = first deliverable.

## 3.3 Profile Construction (Qualitative)

A **Profile** is a 2-column matrix:

$$
\text{Profile}_j = \{ (S_i, L_i) \mid i \in [1, N] \}
$$

Where $S_i$ = Subcategory ID and $L_i \in \{ \text{Current, Target} \}$ level (Partial / Risk-Informed / Repeatable / Adaptive).

## 3.4 Summary of the Tailoring Output — The Security Plan

The final **Cybersecurity Security Plan** must include (per NIST SP 800-181 & CSF 2.0):

1. **Executive Summary** (Risk posture statement).
2. **Scope Definition**.
3. **Current Profile & Tier**.
4. **Target Profile & Tier**.
5. **Gap Matrix with Priorities**.
6. **Action Roadmap** (90-day, 6-month, 12-month buckets).
7. **Owners, Budgets, KPIs**.
8. **Continuous Monitoring Plan** (to feed back into the next CSF cycle).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — CSF Tailoring Workflow (Phases P1–P7)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Phase P1: Scope and Prioritize] --> B[Phase P2: Orient Threats Laws Assets]
    B --> C[Phase P3: Create Current Profile]
    C --> D[Phase P4: Conduct Risk Assessment]
    D --> E[Phase P5: Create Target Profile]
    E --> F[Phase P6: Perform Gap Analysis]
    F --> G[Phase P7: Implement Action Plan and Security Roadmap]
    G --> H[Continuous Monitoring and Feedback Loop]
    H --> C
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — CSF Core, Tiers, and Profiles Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CoreBlock[CSF Core]
        F1[Identify]
        F2[Protect]
        F3[Detect]
        F4[Respond]
        F5[Recover]
    end
    subgraph TierBlock[Implementation Tiers]
        T1[Tier 1 Partial]
        T2[Tier 2 Risk Informed]
        T3[Tier 3 Repeatable]
        T4[Tier 4 Adaptive]
    end
    subgraph ProfileBlock[Profiles]
        CP[Current Profile AS IS]
        TP[Target Profile TO BE]
    end
    CoreBlock --> CP
    CoreBlock --> TP
    TierBlock --> CP
    TierBlock --> TP
    CP --> GA[Gap Analysis]
    TP --> GA
    GA --> SP[Organization Security Plan]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Profile Comparison Block (As-Is vs. To-Be)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CurrentState[Current Profile AS IS]
        C1[ID.AM-1 Score 1]
        C2[PR.AC-1 Score 2]
        C3[PR.DS-1 Score 0]
        C4[DE.CM-1 Score 1]
        C5[RS.RP-1 Score 1]
    end
    subgraph TargetState[Target Profile TO BE]
        T1[ID.AM-1 Score 2]
        T2[PR.AC-1 Score 2]
        T3[PR.DS-1 Score 2]
        T4[DE.CM-1 Score 2]
        T5[RS.RP-1 Score 2]
    end
    C1 -.gap 1.-> T1
    C2 -.no gap.-> T2
    C3 -.gap 2 critical.-> T3
    C4 -.gap 1.-> T4
    C5 -.gap 1.-> T5
    CurrentState --> GA[Gap Matrix]
    TargetState --> GA
    GA --> RM[Roadmap Buckets 90d 6m 12m]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Security Plan Roadmap Buckets

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Approved Security Plan] --> B1[Bucket 1: 0 to 90 Days Critical Gaps]
    SP --> B2[Bucket 2: 3 to 6 Months Moderate Gaps]
    SP --> B3[Bucket 3: 6 to 12 Months Maturity Uplift]
    B1 --> OW1[Owner: CISO + Infra Lead]
    B2 --> OW2[Owner: SOC + GRC Lead]
    B3 --> OW3[Owner: Audit + Training Lead]
    B1 --> KP1[KPI: 100 percent critical gap closure]
    B2 --> KP2[KPI: Tier 2 to Tier 3 advancement]
    B3 --> KP3[KPI: Continuous compliance score greater than 90]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]

**Define "Framework Profile" in the context of the NIST Cybersecurity Framework and distinguish between a Current Profile and a Target Profile.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Framework Profile** is the alignment of the CSF Core (Functions, Categories, Subcategories) with an organization's specific business requirements, risk tolerance, and resources. 
>
> - **Current Profile** captures the cybersecurity outcomes that are *already being achieved* (the "as-is" state) by the organization. 
> - **Target Profile** captures the outcomes *needed* to achieve the desired cybersecurity risk posture aligned with business goals (the "to-be" state). 
>
> The **delta** between the two forms the basis of the gap analysis, which in turn drives the organization's **Security Plan**. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]

**List the four NIST CSF Implementation Tiers in ascending order of maturity and state one defining characteristic of Tier 4.**

**Model Answer:**

The four Implementation Tiers in ascending order are:

1. **Tier 1 — Partial** (ad-hoc, reactive risk management).
2. **Tier 2 — Risk-Informed** (risk-aware but not enterprise-wide).
3. **Tier 3 — Repeatable** (formally approved as policy, regularly updated).
4. **Tier 4 — Adaptive** (continuously improved using lessons learned and predictive analytics).

**Defining characteristic of Tier 4:** The organization *actively adapts* its cybersecurity practices based on previous incidents, threat intelligence, and predictive indicators — risk management becomes part of the organizational culture, not just a compliance checkbox. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven-phase methodology** for tailoring the NIST CSF to create an organization's security plan. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a healthcare organization handling electronic Protected Health Information (ePHI), illustrate how you would **create Current and Target Profiles** and compute the **Tailoring Factor** for the following N = 4 selected Subcategories. **(7 marks)**

| i | Subcategory | $w_i$ | $C_i^{current}$ | $C_i^{target}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ID.AM-1 (Asset inventory) | 4 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | PR.AC-3 (Remote access managed) | 5 | 1 | 2 |
| 3 | PR.DS-2 (Data-in-transit protected) | 5 | 2 | 2 |
| 4 | RS.MI-1 (Incidents contained) | 3 | 0 | 2 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The seven-phase CSF tailoring methodology (as recommended by NIST) is:

1. **Phase 1 — Scope & Prioritize**: Identify the *boundaries* of the cybersecurity plan — which business units, geographic sites, information systems, and assets are in-scope. Document the mission objectives.
2. **Phase 2 — Orient**: Identify the *unique threat landscape* and applicable *regulatory mandates* (e.g., HIPAA, HITRUST, DPDP Act 2023, ABDM for India).
3. **Phase 3 — Create Current Profile**: Map the *existing* cybersecurity practices to the CSF Subcategories. Each Subcategory is scored as 0 (not implemented), 1 (partially implemented), or 2 (fully implemented).
4. **Phase 4 — Risk Assessment**: Conduct a *risk assessment* using NIST SP 800-30 to identify the threats, vulnerabilities, likelihood, and impact. Populate a risk register.
5. **Phase 5 — Create Target Profile**: Based on the *risk appetite* of leadership, define the desired state — which Subcategories must be fully implemented to achieve the target risk posture.
6. **Phase 6 — Gap Analysis**: Compute $\text{Gap}_i = w_i \cdot (C_i^{target} - C_i^{current})$ for each Subcategory. Rank gaps to prioritize.
7. **Phase 7 — Implement Action Plan**: Translate the prioritized gaps into a **Security Plan** with roadmapped actions (90-day, 6-month, 12-month), resource allocation, owners, and KPIs. Feed results back into continuous monitoring.

*(Each phase: 1 mark = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i \cdot C_i^{current} &= (4)(1) + (5)(1) + (5)(2) + (3)(0) \\
&= 4 + 5 + 10 + 0 \\
&= 19
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i \cdot C_i^{target} &= (4)(2) + (5)(2) + (5)(2) + (3)(2) \\
&= 8 + 10 + 10 + 6 \\
&= 34
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Tailoring Factor:**

$$
T_f = \frac{19}{34} \approx 0.5588
$$

**Step 4 — Profile Description (Qualitative):**

- **Current Profile**: The healthcare organization has fully protected data-in-transit (PR.DS-2 = 2), but has *weak* incident containment capability (RS.MI-1 = 0) and partial asset inventory and remote-access controls (ID.AM-1, PR.AC-3 = 1).
- **Target Profile**: All four Subcategories must reach a score of 2, reflecting HIPAA-grade hygiene.

**Step 5 — Gap Ranking and Recommendation:**

| i | Subcategory | Gap$_i$ | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
