| 4 | RS.MI-1 | $3 \times 2 = 6$ | 🥇 #1 |
| 1 | ID.AM-1 | $4 \times 1 = 4$ | 🥈 #2 |
| 2 | PR.AC-3 | $5 \times 1 = 5$ | 🥇 #1 (tie) |
| 3 | PR.DS-2 | $5 \times 0 = 0$ | — Complete |

**Interpretation**: $T_f \approx 0.56$ places the firm in the **moderate posture** band. The first two priorities for the security plan should be **(i)** deploying a SIEM-enabled incident containment playbook for RS.MI-1 and **(ii)** enforcing VPN + MFA for all remote access to ePHI systems (PR.AC-3).

*(7 marks: 1 for numerator, 1 for denominator, 1 for $T_f$, 2 for profile description, 2 for gap ranking + interpretation.)*

---

### Question B — (14 Marks) **[Alternative Choice for Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **three structural components** of the NIST CSF — Framework Core, Implementation Tiers, and Profiles — and explain how they *interact* during tailoring. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An e-commerce startup wishes to tailor the CSF. Compute the **Tailoring Factor** and recommend an action plan given:

| i | Subcategory | $w_i$ | $C_i^{current}$ | $C_i^{target}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ID.GV-1 (Cybersecurity policy established) | 4 | 0 | 2 |
| 2 | PR.IP-1 (Baseline configuration created) | 3 | 1 | 2 |
| 3 | PR.PT-1 (Removable media policy) | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| 4 | DE.AE-2 (Detected events analyzed) | 5 | 1 | 2 |
| 5 | RC.IM-1 (Recovery plans incorporate lessons) | 4 | 0 | 2 |

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

*(Framework Core)* The **Framework Core** is a taxonomy of high-level cybersecurity activities and desired outcomes, organized into **5 Functions** (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover), **23 Categories**, and **108 Subcategories** (CSF 1.1). Each Subcategory is paired with **Informative References** that point to existing standards such as ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, CIS Controls, and COBIT. The Core answers the question: *"What outcomes should we be achieving?"*

*(Implementation Tiers)* The **Tiers** describe the *degree of rigor* and *institutionalization* of an organization's cybersecurity risk governance. They range from **Tier 1 (Partial)** to **Tier 4 (Adaptive)**. Tiers do not represent maturity levels in absolute terms — they provide context on *how* the risk management practices are integrated into broader enterprise risk management.

*(Profiles)* A **Profile** represents the alignment of the Core with the organization's business needs, risk tolerance, and resources. A **Current Profile** indicates the cybersecurity outcomes currently being achieved, while a **Target Profile** indicates the outcomes needed to achieve the desired risk posture. Profiles enable the comparison of "as-is" and "to-be" states.

*(Interaction during Tailoring)* During tailoring, the **Core** provides the menu of Subcategories. **Tiers** allow the organization to *characterize* its current and desired governance rigor. **Profiles** then *express* both the current state (using Tier-appropriate descriptions) and the target state. The interaction is iterative: organization selects Core Subcategories → assigns Tier scores → builds Profiles → runs Gap Analysis → feeds Security Plan → re-evaluates Tier annually.

*(7 marks: 2 Core + 2 Tiers + 2 Profiles + 1 Interaction.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i \cdot C_i^{current} &= (4)(0) + (3)(1) + (2)(2) + (5)(1) + (4)(0) \\
&= 0 + 3 + 4 + 5 + 0 \\
&= 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i \cdot C_i^{target} &= (4)(2) + (3)(2) + (2)(2) + (5)(2) + (4)(2) \\
&= 8 + 6 + 4 + 10 + 8 \\
&= 36
\end{aligned}
$$

**Tailoring Factor:**

$$
T_f = \frac{12}{36} = 0.333
$$

**Interpretation**: $T_f = 0.33 < 0.55$ → **Weak Posture**, urgent remediation required.

**Gap Ranking:**

| i | Subcategory | Gap$_i$ | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | DE.AE-2 | $5 \times 1 = 5$ | 🥇 #1 |
| 5 | RC.IM-1 | $4 \times 2 = 8$ | 🥇 #1 (highest) |
| 1 | ID.GV-1 | $4 \times 2 = 8$ | 🥇 #1 (highest) |
| 2 | PR.IP-1 | $3 \times 1 = 3$ | 🥈 #4 |
| 3 | PR.PT-1 | $2 \times 0 = 0$ | — Complete |

**Recommended Action Plan (Security Plan):**

1. **90-Day Bucket (Critical)**: Draft and ratify the cybersecurity policy (ID.GV-1); stand up a SIEM/SOC for event analysis (DE.AE-2); build a lessons-learned loop into incident postmortems (RC.IM-1).
2. **6-Month Bucket**: Establish configuration baselines via Infrastructure-as-Code (PR.IP-1).
3. **12-Month Bucket**: Move toward Tier 3 (Repeatable) governance, with quarterly CSF profile refreshes.

*(7 marks: 1 numerator + 1 denominator + 1 $T_f$ + 1 interpretation + 3 gap ranking & action plan.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Profile with Tier.** Tiers rate *governance maturity*; Profiles rate *outcome alignment*. Students who swap these definitions lose 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the weight $w_i$.** The Tailoring Factor without weights is *not* accepted for full marks — always state "business-criticality weighted".
> 3. **Skipping the interpretation band.** Computing $T_f$ without stating the band (Weak / Moderate / Strong) costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Not listing gaps in rank order.** Examiners explicitly look for "Prioritized Action Plan"; an unranked gap list = partial credit only.
> 5. **Missing continuous monitoring.** A security plan without a feedback loop (continuous monitoring → re-profile) is incomplete per NIST CSF 2.0.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Tailor CSF to Create a Security Plan**

- **CSF = 3 components**: Core + Tiers + Profiles.
- **Core = 5 Functions**: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover (mnemonic **IDPRR**).
- **Tiers = 4 levels**: Partial → Risk-Informed → Repeatable → Adaptive (1 → 4).
- **Profiles = 2 types**: Current (as-is) and Target (to-be).
- **Tailoring = 7 phases**: Scope → Orient → Current Profile → Risk Assessment → Target Profile → Gap Analysis → Action Plan.
- **Gap Formula**: $\text{Gap}_i = w_i \cdot (C_i^{target} - C_i^{current})$.
- **Tailoring Factor Formula**: $T_f = \dfrac{\sum w_i \cdot C_i^{current}}{\sum w_i \cdot C_i^{target}}$.
- **Interpretation Bands**: $T_f \ge 0.85$ (Strong) | $0.55 \le T_f < 0.85$ (Moderate) | $T_f < 0.55$ (Weak).
- **Security Plan Deliverables**: Executive summary, Scope, Current/Target profiles, Gap matrix, Roadmap (90-day / 6-month / 12-month), Owners, Budget, KPIs, Continuous monitoring loop.
- **NIST References**: NIST CSF 1.1, CSF 2.0 (2024), NIST SP 800-30 (Risk Assessment), NIST SP 800-181 (Workforce).
- **Always** close your answer with a feedback loop — CSF tailoring is *iterative, not one-shot*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cyber Resiliency Engineering Framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cyber Resiliency Engineering Framework

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cyber Resiliency** is the *ability of a system to anticipate, withstand, recover from, and adapt to adverse conditions, stresses, attacks, or compromises* in order to maintain critical mission/business functions. It is an emergent property that complements traditional cybersecurity (which focuses on prevention) by adding the dimensions of **continuity, recovery, and adaptivity**.

The **Cyber Resiliency Engineering Framework (CREF)** is the disciplined, structured engineering methodology — formally codified in **NIST SP 800-160, Volume 2** — used to integrate cyber resiliency considerations into the systems engineering lifecycle. It provides a coherent set of **goals, objectives, techniques, approaches, and design principles** that engineers apply alongside the traditional systems security engineering process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Definition (memorize verbatim):**
> *"The Cyber Resiliency Engineering Framework (CREF) is a systems engineering framework that defines, refines, and applies the concepts of cyber resiliency — namely the goals, objectives, techniques, approaches, and design principles — to engineer systems that maintain critical functions despite adverse cyber events."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Fire-Resistant Smart Hospital"

Imagine a **large, modern hospital** in a city prone to earthquakes and power outages:

- The hospital does **not** rely solely on *preventing* a disaster. It cannot stop an earthquake.
- Instead, the hospital is engineered to **anticipate** that a disaster *will* happen, **withstand** the shock with seismic isolators, **recover** critical care within minutes via backup generators and triage protocols, and **adapt** by rerouting patients to satellite facilities.
- It uses **redundancy** (parallel oxygen lines), **diversity** (solar + diesel + battery power), **segmentation** (isolating the ICU from floods), and **rehearsed recovery playbooks**.

Cyber resiliency engineering applies the **same philosophy** to digital systems: a sophisticated attacker *will* eventually penetrate the firewall, so the system must be engineered to **survive, recover, and learn** from that breach.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Adverse Cyber Event** | Any event that compromises the cybersecurity or mission assurance of a system (attack, failure, human error, natural disaster affecting infrastructure). |
| **Critical Function** | The set of tasks/assets whose disruption would cause unacceptable mission impact. |
| **Resilience Goal** | High-level statement of what resiliency must achieve (Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt). |
| **Resilience Objective** | Specific statement of how the goal is to be achieved (e.g., *Prevent/Avoid*, *Recover*, *Reconstitute*). |
| **Resilience Technique** | A solution-neutral method to satisfy an objective (e.g., *Redundancy*, *Dynamic Positioning*). |
| **Resilience Approach** | A *class* of techniques grouped by how they work (Architectural, Analytical, Operational, Threat-aware, etc.). |
| **Design Principle** | A guiding rule for applying techniques (e.g., *Defensiveness*, *Realignment*). |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** *Cybersecurity* = **Prevent** the attack. *Cyber Resiliency* = **Survive, Recover, and Adapt** after a prevention failure. Both are required; they are **complementary**, not competing disciplines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

## 2.1 The Four Pillars: Resiliency Goals

The CREF organizes all activities around **four top-level goals**. These are the *outcomes* the engineered system must demonstrate:

1. **Anticipate** — Maintain a state of informed preparedness to forestall or preempt adverse cyber events.
2. **Withstand** — Continue essential functions despite an active adverse event (contain the damage).
3. **Recover** — Restore essential functions to normal operation within acceptable time/quality limits after an event.
4. **Adapt** — Modify mission/business functions and/or supporting processes in response to a changing threat environment or post-event lessons learned.

## 2.2 Eight Resiliency Objectives (The "How")

Each goal is refined into one or more **objectives**. There are **eight canonical objectives** in NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2:

| # | Objective | Goal Mapping | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|---|
| O1 | **Prevent / Avoid** | Anticipate | Stop the adverse event from occurring. |
| O2 | **Prepare** | Anticipate | Maintain pre-event readiness (training, playbooks, threat intel). |
| O3 | **Continue** | Withstand | Keep critical functions running during the event. |
| O4 | **Constrain** | Withstand | Limit damage, scope, and spread of the event. |
| O5 | **Recover** | Recover | Restore capabilities quickly and completely. |
| O6 | **Reconstitute** | Recover | Bring back full mission capacity (not just essential functions). |
| O7 | **Understand** | Adapt | Maintain situational awareness; analyze the event and its impact. |
| O8 | **Transform** | Adapt | Re-architect or re-engineer processes based on learning. |

## 2.3 The 14 Resiliency Techniques (The "What")

Techniques are the **engineering tools** the designer uses to satisfy the objectives. They are grouped into **five approaches**.

### Approach A — Architectural (Design-time structural patterns)

1. **Adaptive Response** — Dynamically reconfigure system responses based on attack state.
2. **Analytic Monitoring** — Continuously monitor for behavioral anomalies and threat indicators.
3. **Contextual Awareness** — Maintain real-time awareness of mission state, threats, and dependencies.
4. **Coordinated Protection** — Layer security controls so multiple mechanisms must fail for a compromise.
5. **Design Diversity** — Use heterogeneous implementations of the same function (e.g., two ORMs by different vendors).
6. **Dynamic Positioning** — Move workloads, services, or data across geographic/cyber domains.
7. **Non-Persistence** — Discard and re-provision components to limit attacker dwell time (e.g., immutable containers).
8. **Privilege Restriction** — Tighten access controls during high-risk periods.
9. **Realignment** — Decouple mission functions from business-as-usual infrastructure.
10. **Redundancy** — Provide multiple protected instances of critical resources.
11. **Substantiated Integrity** — Verify the integrity of components before use (e.g., signed boot).
12. **Unpredictability** — Introduce randomness in behavior to confuse attackers.

### Approach B — Analytic (Detection & awareness)

- **Analytic Monitoring** + **Malware / Forensic Analysis** + **Behavioral Analysis**.

### Approach C — Operational (Process & response)

- **Coordination**, **Operational Continuity**, **Self-healing**, **Backups & Recovery**.

### Approach D — Threat-aware (Adversary modeling)

- **Threat Modeling**, **Adversary-driven Testing (Red Teaming)**, **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)**.

### Approach E — Situational Awareness (Mission context)

- **Mission Dependency Mapping**, **Impact Analysis**.

## 2.4 Design Principles (The "Guiding Rules")

| Principle | One-line Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Focus on critical functions** | Protect what matters; accept risk on non-critical assets. |
| **Architect for adaptability** | Build the ability to change, not just to resist. |
| **Design defense in depth** | Layer controls; do not rely on a single barrier. |
| **Trust but verify** | Continuously authenticate and re-validate components. |
| **Plan for graceful degradation** | Reduce service level cleanly when full function is impossible. |
| **Assume compromise** | Design as if the adversary is already inside (Zero-Trust mindset). |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Goal × Objective × Sample Techniques

| Goal | Objective | Example Techniques |
|---|---|---|
| Anticipate | Prevent / Avoid | Privilege Restriction, Coordinated Protection, Substantiated Integrity |
| Anticipate | Prepare | Threat Modeling, Red Teaming, Cyber Threat Intelligence |
| Withstand | Continue | Redundancy, Design Diversity, Adaptive Response, Dynamic Positioning |
| Withstand | Constrain | Fragmentation, Segmentation, Least Privilege, Uncoupling |
| Recover | Recover | Backups, Self-healing, Coordinated Recovery |
| Recover | Reconstitute | Non-Persistence (rebuild from gold image), Realignment |
| Adapt | Understand | Analytic Monitoring, Contextual Awareness, Forensic Analysis |
| Adapt | Transform | Realignment, Design Diversity, Re-architecting |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization aid for exams:** the four goals form the acronym **A-W-R-A** (*Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt*). The eight objectives can be remembered as **PP-CC-RR-UU** (*Prevent, Prepare, Continue, Constrain, Recover, Reconstitute, Understand, Transform*).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Application of the CREF (Analytical Walk-through)

The CREF is not a single document — it is a **procedure** the engineering team executes in tandem with the standard systems engineering lifecycle (Concept → Development → Production → Utilization → Support → Retirement). The KTU board routinely tests whether a student can **apply** the framework, not merely list its parts.

## 3.1 Generic Application Procedure

The following is the canonical seven-step CREF application procedure as defined in NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2.

### Step 1 — Identify the Mission / Critical Functions

The first activity is **mission analysis**. The team asks: *"What must the system absolutely keep doing, even under attack?"*

$$\text{Critical Function} = f(\text{Asset}, \text{Service Level}, \text{Time Sensitivity}, \text{Mission Impact})$$

If function $F_i$ has a *Mission Impact Index* $M_i \in [1, 10]$, and *Recovery Time Objective* $RTO_i$, then the **criticality score** is:

$$C_i = M_i \times \frac{1}{RTO_i}$$

Functions with the highest $C_i$ values are placed at the top of the resiliency analysis.

### Step 2 — Identify the Adverse Cyber Events

For each critical function, enumerate the **adverse cyber events** that could disrupt it. Each event $E_j$ has:

- **Likelihood** $L_j \in [0, 1]$
- **Adverse Impact** $A_j \in [1, 10]$
- **Risk Score** $R_j = L_j \times A_j$

### Step 3 — Identify the Resiliency Goals & Objectives

For each adverse event, the team selects one or more of the four **goals** (Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt) and the corresponding **objectives**. This produces a mapping matrix.

### Step 4 — Identify the Applicable Techniques

From the set of **14 techniques**, the team selects those that satisfy the chosen objectives. Multiple techniques may be combined for defense in depth.

### Step 5 — Select or Design Implementation Approaches

Each technique can be implemented in many concrete ways. For example, *Redundancy* could be *active-active* (both nodes run in parallel) or *active-passive* (standby takes over).

### Step 6 — Implement and Integrate

The selected techniques are integrated into the system architecture and operational procedures. **Trade-off analyses** are documented.

### Step 7 — Validate, Verify, and Monitor

The system is **tested** (penetration testing, red team exercises, chaos engineering) to confirm that the resiliency requirements are met. Performance is **continuously monitored**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Applying CREF to an E-Commerce Web Platform

Let $S$ be a payment-processing web platform with three critical functions:

| Function | $M_i$ | $RTO_i$ | $C_i = M_i / RTO_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 — Authorize Card Payment | 10 | 0.5 h | **20.0** |
| F2 — Display Product Catalogue | 4 | 24 h | 0.17 |
| F3 — Send Email Receipts | 6 | 2 h | 3.0 |

> F1 is clearly the most critical; F2 can tolerate a full day of outage.

**Adverse Events for F1:**

| Event $E_j$ | Description | $L_j$ | $A_j$ | $R_j$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | DDoS on payment API | 0.7 | 9 | **6.3** |
| E2 | SQL injection in payment DB | 0.4 | 10 | **4.0** |
| E3 | Insider credential theft | 0.2 | 8 | 1.6 |

**Selected Resiliency Response:**

$$\text{For } E1, E2 \rightarrow \text{Goals} = \{\text{Withstand, Recover}\} \rightarrow \text{Objectives} = \{\text{Continue, Constrain, Recover}\}$$

$$\text{Techniques} = \{\text{Redundancy, Dynamic Positioning, Coordinated Protection, Self-healing}\}$$

| Technique | Concrete Implementation for F1 |
|---|---|
| Redundancy | Deploy payment microservice in **3 availability zones** with auto-failover. |
| Dynamic Positioning | Use **Anycast routing** to push traffic to the nearest healthy region. |
| Coordinated Protection | Combine **WAF + rate-limiting + mTLS + least-privilege IAM**. |
| Self-healing | Kubernetes liveness probes restart unhealthy pods automatically. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this example is exam-friendly:** it links *criticality scoring* → *risk scoring* → *goal/objective selection* → *technique selection* → *concrete implementation*. Most 14-mark KTU questions can be answered with exactly this chain of reasoning.

## 3.3 Mathematical Representation of the CREF Mapping

For completeness, the CREF can be modelled as a tuple:

$$CREF = (G, O, T, A, P, E)$$

where:

- $G$ = set of **goals** = {Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt}
- $O$ = set of **objectives** = {Prevent, Prepare, Continue, Constrain, Recover, Reconstitute, Understand, Transform}
- $T$ = set of **techniques** (14 elements)
- $A$ = set of **approaches** = {Architectural, Analytic, Operational, Threat-aware, Situational-Awareness}
- $P$ = set of **design principles**
- $E$ = set of **adverse cyber events**

The mapping functions are:

$$\phi: G \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(O), \quad \psi: O \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(T), \quad \chi: T \rightarrow A$$

For a given event $e \in E$ and a chosen subset of goals $G_e \subseteq G$, the engineer computes the corresponding objectives $O_e = \bigcup_{g \in G_e} \phi(g)$ and the candidate technique set $T_e = \bigcup_{o \in O_e} \psi(o)$. The final design selects $T_{final} \subseteq T_e$ subject to **cost, performance, and complexity constraints**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The CREF Top-Level Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n1[Mission and Critical Functions] --> n2[Adverse Cyber Events]
    n2 --> n3[Resiliency Goals]
    n3 --> n4[Resiliency Objectives]
    n4 --> n5[Resiliency Techniques]
    n5 --> n6[Implementation Approaches]
    n6 --> n7[Design Principles Applied]
    n7 --> n8[Engineered Resilient System]
    n8 --> n9[Validate, Monitor, Adapt]
    n9 --> n3
```

**How to read the diagram:** The process is **iterative**, as shown by the feedback arrow from *Validate, Monitor, Adapt* back to *Resiliency Goals*. This reflects the *Adapt* goal of the CREF — the framework is never "finished."

## 4.2 Goal → Objective → Technique Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    ga[Anticipate] --> oa1[Prevent / Avoid]
    ga --> oa2[Prepare]
    ga --> ta1[Privilege Restriction]
    ga --> ta2[Threat Modeling]
    ga --> ta3[CTI]

    gw[Withstand] --> ow1[Continue]
    gw --> ow2[Constrain]
    gw --> tw1[Redundancy]
    gw --> tw2[Design Diversity]
    gw --> tw3[Fragmentation]

    gr[Recover] --> or1[Recover]
    gr --> or2[Reconstitute]
    gr --> tr1[Backups]
    gr --> tr2[Self Healing]
    gr --> tr3[Non Persistence]

    gad[Adapt] --> od1[Understand]
    gad --> od2[Transform]
    gad --> td1[Analytic Monitoring]
    gad --> td2[Contextual Awareness]
    gad --> td3[Realignment]
```

## 4.3 CREF Integration with the Systems Engineering Lifecycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Concept] --> s2[Development]
    s2 --> s3[Production]
    s3 --> s4[Utilization]
    s4 --> s5[Support]
    s5 --> s6[Retirement]
    cref1[Apply CREF] -.-> s1
    cref1 -.-> s2
    cref1 -.-> s3
    cref2[Validate Resiliency] -.-> s4
    cref2 -.-> s5
    cref3[Lessons Learned] -.-> s1
    s6 --> cref3
```

**Reading aid:** the **dotted** arrows show where the CREF activities (apply, validate, learn) feed into the standard systems engineering phases. The CREF is therefore **not a separate lifecycle**; it is a **parallel engineering discipline** that runs alongside.

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram of a Resilient System (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ext[External Clients] --> lb[Load Balancer with WAF]
    lb --> api1[API Node 1]
    lb --> api2[API Node 2]
    lb --> api3[API Node 3]
    api1 --> cache[(Encrypted Cache)]
    api2 --> cache
    api3 --> cache
    api1 --> db1[(Primary DB)]
    api2 --> db2[(Standby DB)]
    api3 --> db2
    db1 <-->|Async Replication| db2
    siem[SIEM and Analytic Monitor] -.-> api1
    siem -.-> api2
    siem -.-> api3
    siem -.-> db1
    cti[Threat Intel Feed] --> siem
    backup[Immutable Offsite Backups] <-.-> db1
```

**Reading aid:** This block diagram shows how multiple CREF techniques are operationalized in a single architecture:

- **Redundancy** → 3 API nodes + standby DB.
- **Diversity** → multiple availability zones (implied).
- **Coordinated Protection** → WAF + mTLS + IAM (implied).
- **Analytic Monitoring** → SIEM with CTI feed.
- **Non-Persistence / Self-healing** → API nodes can be re-provisioned.
- **Substantiated Integrity** → encrypted cache + signed artefacts.
- **Backup & Recover** → immutable offsite backups.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the Cyber Resiliency Engineering Framework (CREF). Mention its key components. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> The Cyber Resiliency Engineering Framework (CREF) is a systems engineering framework, defined in **NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2**, that integrates cyber resiliency concerns into the engineering of trustworthy secure systems. Its key components are:
>
> 1. **Goals** — four high-level outcomes: Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt.
> 2. **Objectives** — eight refinements of the goals (Prevent, Prepare, Continue, Constrain, Recover, Reconstitute, Understand, Transform).
> 3. **Techniques** — 14 solution-neutral engineering methods (e.g., Redundancy, Dynamic Positioning, Analytic Monitoring).
> 4. **Approaches** — five categories of techniques (Architectural, Analytic, Operational, Threat-aware, Situational-awareness).
> 5. **Design Principles** — guiding rules such as *Assume Compromise*, *Defense in Depth*, *Architect for Adaptability*.
> 6. **Adverse Cyber Events** — the set of threats the framework is designed to handle.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the six components.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between *Cybersecurity* and *Cyber Resiliency*. Why are they complementary? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cybersecurity | Cyber Resiliency |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary focus** | **Prevent** adverse cyber events. | **Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt**. |
| **Time horizon** | Before the event. | Before, during, and after the event. |
| **Engineering outcome** | A hardened, defended system. | A system that **continues mission** even after compromise. |
| **Failure assumption** | Threats can be blocked at the perimeter. | Threats *will* penetrate; system must survive. |
| **Key techniques** | Firewalls, encryption, authentication, patching. | Redundancy, diversity, self-healing, adaptive response, reconstitution. |

> They are **complementary** because cybersecurity reduces the *frequency* of breaches, while cyber resiliency reduces the *impact* of the breaches that still occur. A system relying only on cybersecurity has brittle survivability; a system relying only on resiliency is inefficient. Modern trustworthy systems need **both**.

*(3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **four goals** of cyber resiliency in detail, with one example technique for each. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Apply the CREF to a **Smart Grid Electrical Substation** system. Identify the critical functions, list three adverse cyber events, and propose suitable techniques for each. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — The Four Goals

**1. Anticipate**
*Meaning:* Maintain a state of informed preparedness to forestall, preclude, or reduce the impact of adverse cyber events.
*Example technique:* **Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI)** — feeding real-time adversary indicators into a SIEM.
*Engineering value:* A substation that consumes CTI on known ICS malware (e.g., Industroyer) can pre-block indicators of compromise.

**2. Withstand**
*Meaning:* Continue essential functions despite an active adverse event.
*Example technique:* **Redundancy** — duplicate protection relays on independent hardware.
*Engineering value:* Even if the primary relay is attacked, the backup maintains the protection function.

**3. Recover**
*Meaning:* Restore essential functions and full mission capacity after the event.
*Example technique:* **Self-healing** — automated re-imaging of compromised SCADA workstations.
*Engineering value:* Reduces *Mean Time To Recover (MTTR)* and limits cascading outages.

**4. Adapt**
*Meaning:* Modify mission functions or supporting processes in response to a changing threat environment.
*Example technique:* **Realignment** — moving the SCADA control plane off the corporate IT network after detecting lateral movement.
*Engineering value:* Closes the feedback loop — the system *learns* from prior incidents.

*(Each goal carries ~1.5 marks, plus 0.5 mark for the example technique. Total: 7.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Smart Grid Substation Case Study

**Step 1 — Critical Functions**

| Function | Description | $C_i$ (sample) |
|---|---|---|
| F1 | Automatic overcurrent protection | **High** |
| F2 | Real-time telemetry to control centre | Medium |
| F3 | Local HMI for operator | Low |

**Step 2 — Adverse Cyber Events**

| $E_j$ | Description | $R_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| E1 | Manipulation of protection relay settings | **High** |
| E2 | Loss of telemetry link to control centre | Medium |
| E3 | Compromise of engineering workstation | Medium |

**Step 3 — CREF Mapping**

| Event | Goals | Objectives | Selected Techniques | Implementation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1 | Withstand, Recover | Continue, Constrain, Recover | Redundancy, Substantiated Integrity, Self-healing | Dual protection relays; signed firmware; auto re-imaging |
| E2 | Withstand, Recover | Continue, Recover | Redundancy, Dynamic Positioning | Redundant cellular + fibre telemetry; auto failover |
| E3 | Anticipate, Adapt | Prepare, Constrain, Transform | Privilege Restriction, Analytic Monitoring, Realignment | Jump host with MFA; EDR on every endpoint; air-gap after detection |

*(7 marks — distributed across critical functions, adverse events, CREF mapping, and implementation.)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture of the Cyber Resiliency Engineering Framework** and the relationship between its goals, objectives, and techniques. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss any **five resiliency techniques** in detail, with one real-world implementation example for each. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — CREF Architecture

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):** Use a three-tier layered structure:

- **Top tier — Goals:** Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt.
- **Middle tier — Objectives:** eight objectives, each linked to one or two goals.
- **Bottom tier — Techniques:** 14 techniques feeding into objectives.

(Use the Mermaid block from SECTION 4.1 / 4.2 as the reference sketch.)

**Relationship explanation:**

- **Goals** define *what* must be achieved (the "why").
- **Objectives** define *how* each goal is refined (the "what specifically").
- **Techniques** are the engineering *means* to satisfy the objectives.
- **Approaches** group the techniques by the *methodology* they share.
- **Design Principles** govern *how* the techniques are chosen and combined.

The flow is: $E_j \rightarrow G_e \rightarrow O_e \rightarrow T_e \rightarrow A_e \rightarrow P$ and then back through monitoring and adaptation.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 5 for relationships.)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Five Resiliency Techniques

**1. Adaptive Response** — *Reconfigure the system's defensive posture based on detected threat state.*
*Example:* AWS WAF auto-switches from *Allow* to *Block* mode when GuardDuty flags an IP as malicious.

**2. Analytic Monitoring** — *Continuously observe system behaviour for anomalies.*
*Example:* Splunk SIEM correlating firewall, EDR, and authentication logs to detect lateral movement.

**3. Design Diversity** — *Use heterogeneous implementations of the same function.*
*Example:* Running a Java-based and a Go-based reverse proxy in parallel behind a load balancer for an e-commerce site.

**4. Dynamic Positioning** — *Move workloads across cyber/geographic domains.*
*Example:* Kubernetes federated clusters that shift workloads away from a region experiencing DDoS.

**5. Non-Persistence** — *Provision resources only for the time they are needed, then discard.*
*Example:* AWS Lambda functions spun up per request, or immutable AMIs rebuilt nightly for web servers.

*(5 × 1.4 = 7 marks — 0.5 mark for definition, 0.5 mark for example, plus logic explanation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse *goal* with *objective*.** Writing "Anticipate is a technique" is an automatic 0 for that sub-question. Memorize: *Goals* are high-level outcomes (4), *Objectives* are specific refinements (8), *Techniques* are the engineering tools (14).
> 2. **Do NOT list techniques without linking them to an objective or goal.** Examiners award marks for *mapping*, not memorization. Always write *"This technique satisfies the **Continue** objective of the **Withstand** goal."*
> 3. **Do NOT forget the *Adapt* goal.** Many students only discuss Anticipate/Withstand/Recover and skip Adapt. Adapt is what distinguishes *cyber resiliency* from simple *disaster recovery*.
> 4. **Do NOT confuse *CREF* with *RMF* (Risk Management Framework) or *CSF* (Cybersecurity Framework).** CREF is *engineering-focused*, lives in NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2, and is concerned with *building* resilient systems.
> 5. **In case-study questions, always structure your answer as:** *Critical Functions → Adverse Events → Goal/Objective → Technique → Concrete Implementation*. The 14-mark valuation key is built around this exact flow.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **CREF** = *NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2* framework for engineering cyber-resilient systems.
- **4 Goals:** *Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt* → acronym **A-W-R-A**.
- **8 Objectives:** *Prevent, Prepare, Continue, Constrain, Recover, Reconstitute, Understand, Transform* → acronym **PP-CC-RR-UU**.
- **14 Techniques** grouped into **5 Approaches** (Architectural, Analytic, Operational, Threat-aware, Situational-awareness).
- **Design principles** include *Assume Compromise*, *Defense in Depth*, *Focus on Critical Functions*, *Architect for Adaptability*.
- CREF is applied **in parallel** with the systems engineering lifecycle, not as a separate process.
- CREF mapping flow: **Adverse Event → Goal → Objective → Technique → Approach → Implementation**.
- **Cyber resiliency ≠ cybersecurity** — it complements it; cybersecurity prevents, cyber resiliency survives.
- For case-study answers, use the 5-step structure: *Critical Functions → Adverse Events → Goal/Objective → Technique → Concrete Implementation*.
- Key formulas: $C_i = M_i / RTO_i$ (criticality), $R_j = L_j \times A_j$ (risk).
- The CREF tuple is $CREF = (G, O, T, A, P, E)$ with mapping functions $\phi, \psi, \chi$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always end a CREF answer with a short sentence on *Adapt* — it demonstrates to the examiner that you understand the **iterative, learning-oriented** nature of cyber resiliency, which is the highest cognitive level in Bloom's taxonomy (KTU 2024 Scheme uses Revised Bloom's Taxonomy, with **CO4** and **CO5** typically mapped to *Analyse* and *Evaluate* levels).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interpret and Describe Cyber Resiliency Goals and Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interpret and Describe Cyber Resiliency Goals and Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyber Resiliency** is the ability of a system, organization, mission, or business to **anticipate, withstand, recover from, and adapt to adverse conditions, stresses, attacks, or compromises** on the cyber resources it needs to function. (Adapted from **NIST SP 800-160, Volume 2**)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PECCT754 — Engineering of Trustworthy Secure Systems), the phrase **"Interpret and Describe Cyber Resiliency Goals and Objectives"** is anchored to the **NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2** framework, which forms the canonical reference for designing systems that can survive sophisticated cyber-attacks while sustaining mission-critical operations.

### 1.1.1 Distinction: Goals vs. Objectives
| Term | Scope | Purpose |
|------|-------|---------|
| **Goal** | Strategic / High-level | Define *what* must be achieved to be cyber resilient |
| **Objective** | Tactical / Specific | Define *how* the goals are realized in design, operation, and recovery |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Goals are *abstract strategic aspirations*. Objectives are *tangible, measurable, design-actionable outcomes*. A common pitfall is to use these terms interchangeably — do not do this in Part B answers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a modern **hospital complex** that must continue operating even when a natural disaster strikes:

- **Anticipate** → Hospital has a 24/7 weather monitoring team and disaster drills. (Pre-emptive readiness)
- **Withstand** → Backup generators, earthquake-resistant buildings, redundant oxygen lines keep critical wards running during the disaster. (Survive the shock)
- **Recover** → Triage teams, mobile medical units, and pre-stocked pharmacy chains bring full services back within hours. (Bounce back)
- **Adapt** → After the event, the hospital redesigns its flood defenses and shifts critical storage to higher floors. (Learn & evolve)

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Intuition:** Cyber Resiliency is to a *secure system* what *business continuity + disaster recovery + adaptive learning* is to an *organization* — bundled into one engineering discipline. While traditional security asks *"How do we stop the attack?"*, cyber resiliency asks *"How do we keep operating meaningfully even when the attack succeeds?"*

---

## 1.3 The Four Cyber Resiliency Goals (NIST Canonical Set)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Four Goals of Cyber Resiliency** form the strategic foundation. Memorize them in this exact order — KTU questions frequently present them in scrambled form.

1. **Anticipate** — *Maintain a state of informed readiness.*
2. **Withstand** — *Continue essential operations despite adverse events.*
3. **Recover** — *Restore normal operations to the maximum extent possible.*
4. **Adapt** — *Modify mission/business functions and supporting processes in response to changes.*

These four goals are the **anchoring vocabulary** for Module 4 and the basis for all subsequent cyber resiliency techniques and analyses.

---

## 1.4 Foundational Terminology (Bold-Defined Constants)

- **Adverse Cyber Event:** Any event that compromises the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of cyber resources. *(Symbolically denoted as $E_{adv}$)*
- **Adversary:** An entity responsible for an adverse event. *(Denoted as $A$)*
- **Mission/Business Function (MBF):** A set of operations required to achieve a stated objective. *(Denoted as $M_i$)*
- **Cyber Resources:** Information, information systems, and the services they provide.
- **Essential Function:** A function that *cannot be allowed to fail* without severely impacting the mission.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Goals — Detailed Structural Analysis

### 2.1.1 Goal 1: ANTICIPATE
**Strategic Intent:** Establish awareness of the threat landscape *before* an adverse event occurs.

| Dimension | What It Covers |
|-----------|----------------|
| Threat awareness | Adversary TTPs, vulnerabilities, zero-days |
| Mission dependency mapping | Identifying *which* cyber resources support *which* MBFs |
| Risk framing | Quantifying likelihood × impact |
| Planning | Continuity of Operations (COOP) planning, training |

> **Operational Why:** You cannot defend what you cannot see; you cannot protect what you do not understand.

---

### 2.1.2 Goal 2: WITHSTAND
**Strategic Intent:** Maintain essential mission/business functions *during* the adverse event.

| Dimension | What It Covers |
|-----------|----------------|
| Containment | Preventing lateral movement of the adversary |
| Degradation tolerance | Graceful degradation under attack |
| Diversity | Multiple implementations of the same function |
| Hardening | Reducing attack surface, micro-segmentation |

> **Operational Why:** Even with the best anticipation, breach is inevitable. The system must keep core functions alive when compromised.

---

### 2.1.3 Goal 3: RECOVER
**Strategic Intent:** Restore services and data to a trusted, functional state *after* the event.

| Dimension | What It Covers |
|-----------|----------------|
| Restoration priority | Which systems come back first (RTO-based) |
| Forensics | Root cause analysis, evidence preservation |
| Backup integrity | Ensuring backups are themselves untainted |
| Validation | Confirming restored systems are trustworthy |

> **Operational Why:** Recovery is not just "reboot" — it is the engineering discipline of *verified restoration* with provable integrity.

---

### 2.1.4 Goal 4: ADAPT
**Strategic Intent:** Modify the system, processes, or mission in response to observed changes.

| Dimension | What It Covers |
|-----------|----------------|
| Lessons learned | Post-incident integration into design |
| Re-architecting | Replacing brittle components with resilient ones |
| Threat evolution | Responding to changing adversary capabilities |
| Policy modification | Updating procedures to reflect new realities |

> **Operational Why:** A system that does not learn is a system that has already lost the long war.

---

## 2.2 The Eight Cyber Resiliency Objectives (NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Eight Objectives** are the tactical realization of the four goals. Each objective maps to one or more goals. KTU questions often test these mappings — this is a **high-frequency exam topic**.

| # | Objective | Definition | Maps to Goal(s) |
|---|-----------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Prevent / Avoid** | Preclude the execution of an adverse event | Anticipate |
| 2 | **Prepare** | Maintain a set of realistic courses of action to address risks | Anticipate |
| 3 | **Continue** | Maximize the duration and viability of essential MBFs | Withstand |
| 4 | **Constrain** | Reduce the impact of an adverse event | Withstand |
| 5 | **Reconstrain** | Re-apply constraints during recovery | Recover |
| 6 | **Reconstitute** | Restore essential MBFs to full operation | Recover |
| 7 | **Transform** | Modify mission functions to address changes | Adapt |
| 8 | **Re-architect** | Modify the system architecture to address changes | Adapt |

### 2.2.1 Mapping Table — The Most-Asked KTU Visual

```
   ANTICIPATE  →  Prevent/Avoid, Prepare
   WITHSTAND   →  Continue, Constrain
   RECOVER     →  Reconstrain, Reconstitute
   ADAPT       →  Transform, Re-architect
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam Hall:** **"Angry Wolves Can Ravage Anything"**
> - **A**nticipate → **P**revent/**P**repare
> - **W**ithstand → **C**ontinue/**C**onstrain
> - **R**ecover → **R**econstrain/**R**econstitute
> - **A**dapt → **T**ransform/**R**e-architect

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Construct | Formal Representation | Domain | Notes |
|---|-----------|------------------------|--------|-------|
| 1 | Cyber Resiliency Index (simplified) | $R = \alpha A + \beta W + \gamma V + \delta D$ | $[0, 1]$ | $A$=Anticipate, $W$=Withstand, $V$=Recover, $D$=Adapt; weights $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta$ sum to 1 |
| 2 | Mean Time To Recovery | $MTTR = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{recover,i} - t_{failure,i})$ | seconds / minutes | Critical KPI for RECOVER goal |
| 3 | Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF = \frac{T_{operational}}{N_{failures}}$ | hours | Inversely related to stress level |
| 4 | System Availability | $A_{sys} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | fraction $[0, 1]$ | High-level resilience metric |
| 5 | Coverage Ratio (Resilient Techniques) | $C_{R} = \frac{\vert \text{Techniques applied} \vert}{\vert \text{Techniques available} \vert}$ | fraction $[0, 1]$ | Measures breadth of resiliency application |
| 6 | Goal–Objective Coverage | $GOC = \frac{\vert \text{Objectives supporting a goal} \vert}{2}$ | discrete $\{0, 0.5, 1\}$ | 2 objectives per goal, so normalizer is 2 |
| 7 | Risk (in Anticipate) | $R = L \times I$ | dimensionless | $L$ = Likelihood, $I$ = Impact |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** When using vertical bars for set cardinalities (e.g., $|\text{S}|$), **always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX** — the bare pipe `|` will break markdown table rendering in some renderers.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (e.g., Boeing 787):** Implements the *Withstand* and *Recover* goals through redundant flight-control computers and software diversity.
- **Banking Sector:** *Anticipate* via threat intelligence feeds (e.g., FS-ISAC); *Recover* via real-time transaction replay.
- **Smart Grid:** *Adapt* objectives are realized through self-healing distribution networks.
- **Cloud Providers (AWS, Azure):** Architect for *Continue* through availability zones and *Re-architect* through chaos engineering (e.g., Netflix Chaos Monkey).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Cyber Resiliency Index (CRI)

The **Cyber Resiliency Index (CRI)** is a composite metric used to express the overall cyber-resilient posture of a system. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to be able to **derive and apply this index** for Part B numerical scenarios.

### 3.1.1 Starting Premises

Let us define four normalized sub-scores, each in the range $[0, 1]$:

- $S_A$ = Anticipate sub-score
- $S_W$ = Withstand sub-score
- $S_V$ = Recover sub-score
- $S_D$ = Adapt sub-score

Each sub-score is computed based on the *fraction of cyber resiliency techniques applied* under that goal relative to the techniques available.

### 3.1.2 Algebraic Derivation

We begin with the weighted sum of the four sub-scores:

$$
CRI = w_A \cdot S_A + w_W \cdot S_W + w_V \cdot S_V + w_D \cdot S_D
$$

where $w_A, w_W, w_V, w_D$ are non-negative weights reflecting the organizational priority for each goal.

To make $CRI$ a valid normalized index, the weights must satisfy the **normalization constraint**:

$$
w_A + w_W + w_V + w_D = 1
$$

This ensures:

$$
CRI \in [\min(S_A, S_W, S_V, S_D), \ \max(S_A, S_W, S_V, S_D)] \subset [0, 1]
$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Example (14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:** A defense system has the following resiliency sub-scores and weights. Compute the CRI.

| Goal | Sub-score $S_x$ | Weight $w_x$ |
|------|------------------|----------------|
| Anticipate | 0.80 | 0.30 |
| Withstand  | 0.60 | 0.30 |
| Recover    | 0.75 | 0.25 |
| Adapt      | 0.50 | 0.15 |

**Step 1 — Verify normalization:**

$$
w_A + w_W + w_V + w_D = 0.30 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.15 = 1.00 \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted sum:**

$$
CRI = (0.30)(0.80) + (0.30)(0.60) + (0.25)(0.75) + (0.15)(0.50)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each product:**

$$
(0.30)(0.80) = 0.240
$$
$$
(0.30)(0.60) = 0.180
$$
$$
(0.25)(0.75) = 0.1875
$$
$$
(0.15)(0.50) = 0.075
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the products:**

$$
CRI = 0.240 + 0.180 + 0.1875 + 0.075
$$

$$
CRI = 0.6825
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

A CRI of **0.6825** (or **68.25%**) indicates a **moderately resilient system**. The lowest sub-score (Adapt = 0.50) is the *weakest link* — the engineering recommendation is to invest in adaptive capabilities (e.g., automated threat hunting, machine-learning-based anomaly detection).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Goal–Objective Coverage (GOC) — Analytical Matrix

For each of the four goals, NIST defines *exactly two* objectives. The **Goal–Objective Coverage** is defined as:

$$
GOC(g) = \frac{\vert \text{Objectives realized for goal } g \vert}{2}, \quad g \in \{A, W, V, D\}
$$

This produces an analytical matrix mapping goals to their coverage.

| Goal $g$ | Objective 1 | Objective 2 | $GOC(g)$ |
|----------|--------------|--------------|------------|
| Anticipate ($A$) | Prevent/Avoid | Prepare | $GOC(A) = \frac{\vert\{OP, PP\}\vert}{2} = 1$ if both realized |
| Withstand ($W$) | Continue | Constrain | $GOC(W) = \frac{\vert\{CT, CN\}\vert}{2}$ |
| Recover ($V$) | Reconstrain | Reconstitute | $GOC(V) = \frac{\vert\{RC, RN\}\vert}{2}$ |
| Adapt ($D$) | Transform | Re-architect | $GOC(D) = \frac{\vert\{TF, RA\}\vert}{2}$ |

### 3.2.1 Aggregate Coverage Index

$$
GOC_{total} = \frac{1}{4} \sum_{g \in \{A, W, V, D\}} GOC(g)
$$

### 3.2.2 Worked Example

A hospital's cyber resilience program reports:
- Anticipate: 2/2 objectives (full coverage)
- Withstand: 1/2 objectives (only Continue is implemented)
- Recover: 2/2 objectives (full coverage)
- Adapt: 0/2 objectives (no formal adapt strategy)

**Step 1 — Compute each $GOC(g)$:**

$$
GOC(A) = \frac{2}{2} = 1.00
$$
$$
GOC(W) = \frac{1}{2} = 0.50
$$
$$
GOC(V) = \frac{2}{2} = 1.00
$$
$$
GOC(D) = \frac{0}{2} = 0.00
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $GOC_{total}$:**

$$
GOC_{total} = \frac{1.00 + 0.50 + 1.00 + 0.00}{4} = \frac{2.50}{4} = 0.625
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Conclusion:**

The system covers **62.5%** of NIST's defined cyber resiliency objectives. Critical gaps exist in *Adapt* — meaning the system cannot transform or re-architect itself in response to evolving threats, leaving it vulnerable long-term.

---

## 3.3 System Availability Derivation (KTU High-Yield)

For systems engineered under the *Withstand* and *Recover* goals, system availability is:

$$
A_{sys} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

### 3.3.1 Worked Scenario

A web service has:
- $MTBF = 720$ hours
- $MTTR = 4$ hours

$$
A_{sys} = \frac{720}{720 + 4} = \frac{720}{724} \approx 0.9945
$$

This corresponds to a **"three nines"** service level (99.45% uptime) — typical of mission-critical systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** To *increase* $A_{sys}$ without redesigning the system, the most effective lever is **reducing $MTTR$** (faster recovery). Improving $MTBF$ alone is asymptotically less effective since $A_{sys} \to 1$ as $MTBF \to \infty$ but at diminishing returns.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Hierarchical Mapping of Goals → Objectives

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2 mapping from the four high-level **Cyber Resiliency Goals** to the eight **Cyber Resiliency Objectives**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeG1["ANTICIPATE"] --> nodeO1["Prevent or Avoid"]
    nodeG1 --> nodeO2["Prepare"]
    nodeG2["WITHSTAND"] --> nodeO3["Continue"]
    nodeG2 --> nodeO4["Constrain"]
    nodeG3["RECOVER"] --> nodeO5["Reconstrain"]
    nodeG3 --> nodeO6["Reconstitute"]
    nodeG4["ADAPT"] --> nodeO7["Transform"]
    nodeG4 --> nodeO8["Re-architect"]
    nodeA1["Adverse Event Strikes"] --> nodeG1
    nodeA1 --> nodeG2
    nodeA1 --> nodeG3
    nodeA1 --> nodeG4
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This second diagram models the **engineering workflow** of a cyber-resilient system, showing how the four goals interlock operationally over the timeline of an adverse event.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStage0["Normal Operations"] --> nodeStage1["Anticipate Phase: Threat Intel and Risk Analysis"]
    nodeStage1 --> nodeStage2["Withstand Phase: Active Defense in Depth"]
    nodeStage2 --> nodeEvt["Adverse Cyber Event Detected"]
    nodeEvt --> nodeStage3["Recover Phase: Forensic Backup and Replay"]
    nodeStage3 --> nodeStage4["Adapt Phase: Lessons Learned and Redesign"]
    nodeStage4 --> nodeStage0
    nodeStage0 -.Telemetry Feedback.-> nodeStage1
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Goal–Objective Coverage Matrix (Visual Heatmap)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeGoal1["ANTICIPATE"] --> nodeObj1A["Prevent and Avoid"]
    nodeGoal1 --> nodeObj1B["Prepare"]
    nodeGoal2["WITHSTAND"] --> nodeObj2A["Continue"]
    nodeGoal2 --> nodeObj2B["Constrain"]
    nodeGoal3["RECOVER"] --> nodeObj3A["Reconstrain"]
    nodeGoal3 --> nodeObj3B["Reconstitute"]
    nodeGoal4["ADAPT"] --> nodeObj4A["Transform"]
    nodeGoal4 --> nodeObj4B["Re-architect"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above three diagrams are textually equivalent. The first is the canonical NIST hierarchy; the second illustrates the *temporal workflow* (Anticipate → Withstand → Recover → Adapt); the third is a structural matrix used for **Goal–Objective Coverage (GOC)** analysis — a frequent KTU 2024 Scheme problem type.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define Cyber Resiliency and list its four strategic goals.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Cyber Resiliency is the ability of a system, organization, mission, or business to anticipate, withstand, recover from, and adapt to adverse conditions, stresses, attacks, or compromises on the cyber resources it needs to function. *(Source: NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2)*

**Four Goals (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Anticipate** — Maintain a state of informed readiness for adverse events.
2. **Withstand** — Continue essential operations despite adverse events.
3. **Recover** — Restore normal operations to the maximum extent possible.
4. **Adapt** — Modify mission/business functions in response to changes.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between the Cyber Resiliency goals "Withstand" and "Recover" with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Withstand | Recover |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **Timing** | *During* the adverse event | *After* the adverse event |
| **Primary objective** | Keep essential functions alive | Restore full functional capability |
| **Engineering focus** | Redundancy, diversity, segmentation | Backups, forensics, reconstitution |
| **Key metric** | MTBF (longer is better) | MTTR (shorter is better) |
| **Example** | Air-gapped failover server continues taking transactions during a DDoS attack | Restoring the main database from verified backups after ransomware encryption |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B for a module carries **14 marks** and offers **internal choice** between two questions, each with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the **eight cyber resiliency objectives** defined by NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2. Clearly state the **goal-to-objective mapping** for each objective. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A critical infrastructure system has the following normalized sub-scores and weights:

| Goal | Sub-score | Weight |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Anticipate | 0.85 | 0.25 |
| Withstand | 0.70 | 0.30 |
| Recover | 0.65 | 0.25 |
| Adapt | 0.40 | 0.20 |

**Compute the Cyber Resiliency Index (CRI), verify weight normalization, identify the weakest sub-system, and provide an engineering recommendation.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

The eight NIST objectives are:

1. **Prevent / Avoid** — *Maps to Anticipate.* Preclude execution of an adverse event (e.g., firewalls, patching, threat intel).
2. **Prepare** — *Maps to Anticipate.* Maintain realistic courses of action for known risks (e.g., IR playbooks, drills).
3. **Continue** — *Maps to Withstand.* Maximize duration of essential MBFs (e.g., redundant systems, graceful degradation).
4. **Constrain** — *Maps to Withstand.* Reduce the impact of the event (e.g., micro-segmentation, least privilege).
5. **Reconstrain** — *Maps to Recover.* Re-apply containment controls during recovery to prevent re-breach.
6. **Reconstitute** — *Maps to Recover.* Restore essential MBFs to full operation (e.g., verified backups, clean-room restoration).
7. **Transform** — *Maps to Adapt.* Modify mission functions to address new realities (e.g., shifting to zero-trust operations).
8. **Re-architect** — *Maps to Adapt.* Modify system architecture to address changes (e.g., replacing monolithic apps with microservices).

> Each objective carries **0.75 marks** for the description and **0.125 marks** for the correct goal mapping. *(Examiner's allocation scheme.)*

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Verify normalization:**

$$
0.25 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 1.00 \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CRI:**

$$
CRI = (0.25)(0.85) + (0.30)(0.70) + (0.25)(0.65) + (0.20)(0.40)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each product:**

$$
(0.25)(0.85) = 0.2125
$$
$$
(0.30)(0.70) = 0.2100
$$
$$
(0.25)(0.65) = 0.1625
$$
$$
(0.20)(0.40) = 0.0800
$$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$
CRI = 0.2125 + 0.2100 + 0.1625 + 0.0800 = 0.6650
$$

**Step 5 — Identify weakest subsystem:**

The **Adapt** sub-score ($S_D = 0.40$) is the lowest.

**Step 6 — Engineering recommendation:**

The organization should invest in adaptive capabilities: machine-learning-based anomaly detection, automated patch management, and a formal post-incident review process. Target: increase $S_D$ from 0.40 to at least 0.70 within one fiscal cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **omit the weight normalization verification step** (Step 1) and lose 1 mark. Always show this — it is an explicit valuation checkpoint.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2025 - Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **four cyber resiliency goals** and the **operational phases** of a cyber-resilient system lifecycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multinational bank reports the following objective coverage:

- **Anticipate:** Both Prevent/Avoid and Prepare implemented.
- **Withstand:** Only Constrain implemented; Continue is partial.
- **Recover:** Both Reconstrain and Reconstitute implemented.
- **Adapt:** Transform is partial; Re-architect not implemented.

**Compute the Goal–Objective Coverage Index ($GOC_{total}$) and recommend remediation steps.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Four Cyber Resiliency Goals (4 marks):**

1. **Anticipate** — Threat awareness, risk analysis, mission mapping.
2. **Withstand** — Redundancy, diversity, segmentation, graceful degradation.
3. **Recover** — Backup integrity, forensic analysis, validated restoration.
4. **Adapt** — Re-architecture, transformation, post-incident learning.

**Operational Lifecycle Phases (3 marks):**

The cyber-resilient lifecycle has four operational phases that *correspond* to the four goals:

- **Phase 1 — Anticipate (Pre-event):** Threat intel, risk framing, training.
- **Phase 2 — Withstand (During event):** Active defense, isolation, segmentation.
- **Phase 3 — Recover (Post-event short-term):** Forensics, restoration, validation.
- **Phase 4 — Adapt (Post-event long-term):** Re-architect, transform, integrate lessons learned.

> A flowchart mapping `Normal Operations → Anticipate → Withstand → Adverse Event → Recover → Adapt → Normal Operations` is required for full marks. *(Use the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.2 as a reference.)*

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Assign coverage fractions:**

$$
GOC(A) = \frac{2}{2} = 1.00
$$
$$
GOC(W) = \frac{0.5 + 1.0}{2} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75
$$

*(Note: "Constrain" implemented fully = 1.0; "Continue" partial = 0.5.)*

$$
GOC(V) = \frac{2}{2} = 1.00
$$
$$
GOC(D) = \frac{0.5 + 0.0}{2} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 — Compute aggregate:**

$$
GOC_{total} = \frac{1.00 + 0.75 + 1.00 + 0.25}{4} = \frac{3.00}{4} = 0.75
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret:**

The bank covers **75%** of cyber resiliency objectives. Strong in Anticipate, Recover; moderate in Withstand; weak in Adapt.

**Step 4 — Remediation steps:**

1. Implement **Continue** objective fully via multi-site active-active failover.
2. Implement **Transform** to enable dynamic policy adjustment during incidents.
3. Implement **Re-architect** by adopting zero-trust and software-defined perimeters.
4. Establish a formal **post-incident review board** that mandates adapt-phase deliverables.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When a sub-objective is "partial," students often assign a fractional score of **0.5** without justification. Always state: *"Partial implementation is assumed to contribute 0.5 toward the objective count, in line with NIST's binary-with-partial convention."* This single sentence is worth 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam Hall:**

- ✅ The **Four Goals** are: **Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt** — *memorize in this order.*
- ✅ The **Eight Objectives** are: **Prevent/Avoid, Prepare, Continue, Constrain, Reconstrain, Reconstitute, Transform, Re-architect.**
- ✅ The **Goal–Objective mapping** is: Anticipate → {Prevent/Avoid, Prepare}; Withstand → {Continue, Constrain}; Recover → {Reconstrain, Reconstitute}; Adapt → {Transform, Re-architect}.
- ✅ The **canonical source** is **NIST SP 800-160, Volume 2** (do not cite Vol. 1 — that is systems security engineering, not cyber resiliency).
- ✅ Cyber Resiliency ≠ Cybersecurity. Cybersecurity prevents breaches; Cyber Resiliency *assumes breach and ensures mission continuity*.
- ✅ Key formulas to remember:
  - $CRI = w_A S_A + w_W S_W + w_V S_V + w_D S_D$
  - $A_{sys} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
  - $GOC_{total} = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{g} GOC(g)$
  - $R = L \times I$
- ✅ Mnemonic: **"Angry Wolves Can Ravage Anything"** → A→PP, W→CC, R→RR, A→TR.
- ✅ Always **verify weight normalization** before computing CRI.
- ✅ Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX, **never bare `|`**, to preserve markdown table integrity.
- ✅ When a sub-objective is "partial," explicitly state the convention (e.g., 0.5).
- ✅ The **weakest sub-score** in a CRI calculation is the **most actionable engineering target** — mention this in your conclusion for full marks.
- ✅ Do **not** confuse the four *goals* with the four *lifecycle phases* — they correspond one-to-one in NIST's framework, but goals are *strategic aspirations* while phases are *operational windows in time*.
- ✅ For Mermaid diagrams: node IDs must be **alphanumeric prefixed with letters** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`); never use reserved words (`end`, `graph`) as node names.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differentiate Cyber Resiliency Techniques, Approaches, and Design Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT754 - ENGINEERING OF TRUSTWORTHY SECURE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Differentiate Cyber Resiliency Techniques, Approaches, and Design Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cyber Resiliency** is the ability of a system to anticipate, withstand, recover from, and adapt to adverse conditions, stresses, attacks, or compromises of the cyber resources on which the system depends. It is formally codified by **NIST Special Publication SP 800-160, Volume 2** (Systems Security Engineering — Cyber Resiliency Considerations for the Engineering of Trustworthy Secure Systems).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this concept is differentiated into three hierarchical layers:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Layering of Cyber Resiliency (KTU Module 4 Focus)**
> - **Design Principles (Foundational Layer):** Why we engineer a system for resilience (philosophical axioms).
> - **Approaches (Strategic Layer):** How we organise the engineering process (defensive, threat-informed, adaptive).
> - **Techniques (Tactical Layer):** What specific mechanisms are deployed (14 NIST techniques).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body's Immune System

Imagine a modern hospital operating theatre:

| Resiliency Layer | Hospital Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Design Principle** | "Expect infections to occur" (axiom guiding every protocol) |
| **Approach** | "Combination of hygiene, surveillance, treatment, vaccination" (overall strategy) |
| **Technique** | Sterilising instruments, isolating infected wards, redundant blood banks |

Just as the hospital cannot *prevent* every pathogen but is engineered to **withstand, recover, and adapt**, a cyber-resilient system accepts that compromise is inevitable and engineers mechanisms to maintain mission continuity.

### 1.3 Operational Context in Trustworthy Secure Systems Engineering

A **trustworthy secure system** is one that exhibits sufficient *reliability*, *dependability*, *survivability*, and *resilience*. Within this broader engineering goal, cyber resiliency specifically addresses the **adversarial threat model** — i.e., intelligent attackers with intent, capability, and persistence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Recap**
> - **Adverse Condition:** Any circumstance that degrades mission capability (e.g., DoS attack, insider threat, ransomware).
> - **Cyber Resource:** Information, software, hardware, firmware, services, or data on which the mission depends.
> - **Trustworthy System:** System exhibits confidentiality, integrity, availability, accountability, authenticity, and non-repudiation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cyber Resiliency Design Principles (Why)

The **eight foundational design principles** from NIST SP 800-160 Vol 2 establish the engineering axioms that drive every architectural decision. They are *not* mechanisms — they are the *rationale* behind the mechanisms.

| # | Design Principle | Core Tenet (Why) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Focus on critical assets** | Prioritise protection of mission-essential functions, not all assets equally |
| 2 | **Architect for adaptability** | Design to support change, reconfiguration, and evolution |
| 3 | **Reduce attack surfaces** | Minimise exposed functionality and vulnerabilities |
| 4 | **Assume compromised resources** | Treat every component as potentially hostile — zero-trust mindset |
| 5 | **Expect adversarial behaviour** | Use threat intelligence; assume intent, capability, and persistence |
| 6 | **Plan and manage risk** | Apply disciplined risk-management frameworks (NIST RMF) |
| 7 | **Make the system trustworthy** | Ensure the system itself is worthy of reliance |
| 8 | **Support adaptive response** | Enable real-time reconfiguration based on threat posture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Design Principles answer **WHY**. If a question asks *"Why is segmentation important?"* — the correct framing is Principle #1 (Focus on critical assets) and Principle #3 (Reduce attack surfaces), NOT the technique description itself.

### 2.2 Cyber Resiliency Approaches (How)

Approaches are **strategic organisational postures** that combine multiple techniques under a unified engineering philosophy. NIST identifies three primary approaches:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Approaches — Strategic Engineering Postures**

#### A. Defensive Approach
- **Philosophy:** *Build a fortress.*
- **Orientation:** **Anticipate** and **Withstand** adverse conditions.
- **Techniques Dominantly Used:** Segmentation, Redundancy, Privilege Restriction, Design Diversity.
- **Goal:** Prevent the adversary from gaining a foothold and contain damage if they do.

#### B. Threat-Informed Approach
- **Philosophy:** *Know your enemy.*
- **Orientation:** **Anticipate**, **Withstand**, **Recover**, **Adapt** (all four goals).
- **Techniques Dominantly Used:** Analytic Monitoring, Contextual Awareness, Substantiated Integrity, Dynamic Representation.
- **Goal:** Continuously integrate adversary intelligence (e.g., MITRE ATT&CK) into engineering decisions.

#### C. Adaptive (Offensive / Reactive) Approach
- **Philosophy:** *Adapt faster than the adversary.*
- **Orientation:** **Recover** and **Adapt** (with strong anticipation).
- **Techniques Dominantly Used:** Adaptive Response, Dynamic Positioning, Deception, Non-Persistence, Realignment.
- **Goal:** Actively reshape the system's attack surface in response to real-time threat data.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Approach" with "Technique". A technique is a **specific mechanism** (e.g., "honeypot deployment"); an approach is the **strategic reason** for using many such mechanisms (e.g., "deception is part of an adaptive approach").

### 2.3 Cyber Resiliency Techniques (What)

The **14 techniques** are the engineering implementation primitives, grouped under four resiliency goals: **Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt.**

**KTU Formula Sheet — Cyber Resiliency Technique Matrix**

| Goal | Technique | Engineering Function | Typical Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anticipate | **Analytic Monitoring** | Detect adversary behaviour | SIEM, UEBA, threat hunting |
| Anticipate | **Contextual Awareness** | Understand asset, mission, adversary context | CMDB, asset tagging |
| Anticipate | **Dynamic Representation** | Maintain live situational awareness | Attack graphs, dashboards |
| Withstand | **Adaptive Response** | Reconfigure in real-time | Auto-isolation, quarantine |
| Withstand | **Coordinated Defense** | Synchronise defensive actions | Orchestration, playbooks |
| Withstand | **Deception** | Mislead adversary | Honeypots, decoys, breadcrumbs |
| Withstand | **Design Diversity** | Use varied implementations | N-version programming, polyglot persistence |
| Withstand | **Non-Persistence** | Limit adversary dwell time | Container rotation, immutable infra |
| Withstand | **Privilege Restriction** | Limit blast radius | RBAC, least privilege, PAM |
| Withstand | **Segmentation** | Contain lateral movement | Micro-segmentation, VLANs, ZTNA |
| Withstand | **Substantiated Integrity** | Verify trust state | TPM, attestation, code signing |
| Recover | **Redundancy** | Continue through failure | N+1 servers, geo-replication |
| Recover | **Realignment** | Restore to trusted state | Re-imaging, known-good backups |
| Adapt | **Unifying Concept** | Learn and evolve | Post-incident analysis, threat intel feedback |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Financial Systems:** Combine Redundancy (geo-replicated databases) with Adaptive Response (auto-failover) — a *defensive + adaptive* approach driven by the principle *Assume Compromised Resources*.
- **Healthcare IoT:** Apply Segmentation + Substantiated Integrity to medical devices — driven by *Focus on Critical Assets*.
- **Cloud-Native Microservices:** Use Non-Persistence (immutable containers) + Dynamic Positioning (auto-scaling) — driven by *Architect for Adaptability*.

### 2.5 Mathematical Notations & Reference Standards

While cyber resiliency is not a closed-form mathematical discipline, key metrics are formalised:

$$
R(t) = \frac{MTTR}{MTTR + MTBF}
$$

Where:
- $R(t)$ = **Resilience Ratio** (dimensionless, $\vert R(t) \vert \le 1$)
- $MTTR$ = **Mean Time To Recover**
- $MTBF$ = **Mean Time Between Failures**

$$
A(t) = \frac{U(t)}{U(t) + D(t)}
$$

Where:
- $A(t)$ = **System Availability** at time $t$
- $U(t)$ = Uptime window
- $D(t)$ = Downtime window (includes *graceful degradation* periods)

> [!NOTE]
> The metric $R(t)$ is high-yield for KTU Part B numerical/descriptive questions because it directly quantifies recovery capability — a hallmark of resilient engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cyber Resiliency Engineering Loop

Cyber resiliency is not a one-time property; it is an **iterative engineering process**. We derive the closed-loop control flow that converts design principles into measurable resilience:

$$
S_{n+1} = f(S_n, T_n, P_n, A_n)
$$

Where:
- $S_n$ = System state at iteration $n$
- $T_n$ = Threat intelligence input at iteration $n$
- $P_n$ = Set of selected design principles
- $A_n$ = Chosen approach posture
- $f(\cdot)$ = Resiliency transformation operator

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Threat Assimilation} \quad & T_n = \mathcal{A}(\text{INTEL}_n, \text{HISTORY}_n) \\
\text{Step 2: Principle Application} \quad & P_n = \text{Filter}(\text{PRINCIPLES}, \text{MISSION\_PRIORITY}) \\
\text{Step 3: Approach Selection} \quad & A_n = \text{SelectPosture}(\text{T}_n, \text{RISK\_TOLERANCE}) \\
\text{Step 4: Technique Deployment} \quad & \text{TECHNIQUES}_n = \text{Instantiate}(A_n, P_n) \\
\text{Step 5: State Update} \quad & S_{n+1} = \text{Apply}(\text{TECHNIQUES}_n, S_n) \\
\text{Step 6: Resilience Measurement} \quad & R_n = \text{Evaluate}(S_{n+1}, S_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

This derivation shows that **techniques are the visible outputs** of an engineering process driven by **principles (axioms)** and **approaches (strategy)**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Availability Calculation

**Problem:** A banking server experiences $D(t) = 4$ hours of total downtime over $U(t) + D(t) = 8760$ hours (one year). Compute availability and the resilience ratio given $MTTR = 2$ hours, $MTBF = 720$ hours.

**Step 1: Compute Availability**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A(t) &= \frac{U(t)}{U(t) + D(t)} \\
&= \frac{8760 - 4}{8760} \\
&= \frac{8756}{8760} \\
&\approx 0.999543 \text{ or } 99.9543\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Resilience Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(t) &= \frac{MTTR}{MTTR + MTBF} \\
&= \frac{2}{2 + 720} \\
&= \frac{2}{722} \\
&\approx 0.002770 \text{ or } 0.277\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Engineering Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A low $R(t)$ indicates **high resilience** because recovery time is small relative to the operational interval. This is the metric cyber resiliency techniques aim to *minimise* — particularly **Adaptive Response** (reduces MTTR) and **Redundancy** (increases MTBF).

### 3.3 Analytical Differentiation Matrix — Principles vs Approaches vs Techniques

This is the **highest-yield table** for KTU Module 4 comparative questions.

| Differentiator | Design Principles | Approaches | Techniques |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer in hierarchy** | Foundational (Why) | Strategic (How) | Tactical (What) |
| **Number in NIST SP 800-160 Vol 2** | 8 | 3 | 14 |
| **Answer the question** | *Why engineer this way?* | *What is the strategic posture?* | *What specific mechanism is used?* |
| **Scope** | Universal to all systems | System-class dependent | Component-class dependent |
| **Examples** | Assume compromised resources | Threat-Informed Approach | Honeypot deployment |
| **Time horizon** | Lifetime of system | Engineering phase + operations | Deployment phase + runtime |
| **Evaluation metric** | Conformance to principles | Risk reduction ratio | Resilience Ratio $R(t)$ |
| **KTU 3-mark framing** | Define and justify | Classify and justify | Define with example |

### 3.4 Mapping Principles → Approaches → Techniques (Vertical Trace)

To prove the differentiation, we trace *one engineering decision* end-to-end:

> [!NOTE]
> **Trace Example: "Protect the customer database from ransomware"**
>
> 1. **Principle #1 (Focus on critical assets):** Database is mission-critical → prioritise.
> 2. **Principle #4 (Assume compromised resources):** Endpoint may already be infected → trust nothing.
> 3. **Approach Chosen:** *Threat-Informed Approach* (adversary capability known via MITRE ATT&CK).
> 4. **Techniques Selected:** **Non-Persistence** (rotate DB credentials hourly), **Segmentation** (isolate DB subnet), **Substantiated Integrity** (TPM-attested queries), **Realignment** (snapshot-based recovery).
>
> The **principles** drove the decision; the **approach** structured it; the **techniques** implemented it. This is the canonical NIST engineering pattern.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Hierarchy Diagram — Principles → Approaches → Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mission and Threat Context] --> B[Design Principles Layer Why]
    B --> P1[P1 Focus on Critical Assets]
    B --> P2[P2 Architect for Adaptability]
    B --> P3[P3 Reduce Attack Surfaces]
    B --> P4[P4 Assume Compromised Resources]
    B --> P5[P5 Expect Adversarial Behaviour]
    B --> P6[P6 Plan and Manage Risk]
    B --> P7[P7 Make System Trustworthy]
    B --> P8[P8 Support Adaptive Response]

    P1 --> C1[Approach Layer How]
    P2 --> C1
    P4 --> C1
    P5 --> C1

    C1 --> AP1[AP1 Defensive Approach]
    C1 --> AP2[AP2 Threat Informed Approach]
    C1 --> AP3[AP3 Adaptive Approach]

    AP1 --> T1[Techniques Layer What]
    AP2 --> T1
    AP3 --> T1

    T1 --> TC1[TC1 Segmentation]
    T1 --> TC2[TC2 Redundancy]
    T1 --> TC3[TC3 Deception]
    T1 --> TC4[TC4 Non Persistence]
    T1 --> TC5[TC5 Adaptive Response]
    T1 --> TC6[TC6 Analytic Monitoring]
    T1 --> TC7[TC7 Substantiated Integrity]
```

### 4.2 NIST Cyber Resiliency Goals — Technique Grouping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GOAL1[Goal Anticipate]
        G1A[Analytic Monitoring]
        G1B[Contextual Awareness]
        G1C[Dynamic Representation]
    end

    subgraph GOAL2[Goal Withstand]
        G2A[Adaptive Response]
        G2B[Coordinated Defense]
        G2C[Deception]
        G2D[Design Diversity]
        G2E[Non Persistence]
        G2F[Privilege Restriction]
        G2G[Segmentation]
        G2H[Substantiated Integrity]
    end

    subgraph GOAL3[Goal Recover]
        G3A[Redundancy]
        G3B[Realignment]
    end

    subgraph GOAL4[Goal Adapt]
        G4A[Unifying Adaptive Loop]
    end

    GOAL1 --> GOAL2
    GOAL2 --> GOAL3
    GOAL3 --> GOAL4
    GOAL4 -.feedback.-> GOAL1
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cyber Resiliency Engineering Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Input Mission Requirements and Threat Intel] --> POLICY[Block A Policy Engine Holds Design Principles]
    POLICY --> POSTURE[Block B Posture Selector Chooses Approach]
    POSTURE --> IMPL[Block C Technique Library Implements Mechanisms]
    IMPL --> RUNTIME[Block D Runtime System Under Defense]
    RUNTIME --> MON[Block E Continuous Monitoring]
    MON --> METRIC[Block F Metrics MTTR MTBF R t]
    METRIC --> FEEDBACK[Block G Feedback to Policy Engine]
    FEEDBACK --> POLICY
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block D Note:** The runtime system itself is the *object* of resiliency, while Blocks A–G are the *meta-system* engineering the resiliency. This distinction is what differentiates cyber resiliency from conventional cybersecurity — it treats the system as **continuously re-engineerable**, not statically deployed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
*Define cyber resiliency and list any four design principles from NIST SP 800-160 Vol 2.*

**Model Answer:**
Cyber resiliency is the ability of a system to **anticipate, withstand, recover from, and adapt to** adverse conditions, stresses, attacks, or compromises.

Four design principles:
1. Focus on critical assets
2. Architect for adaptability
3. Reduce attack surfaces
4. Assume compromised resources

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner Note: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correct listing of four principles.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
*Differentiate between a cyber resiliency approach and a cyber resiliency technique with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**
